Document 85 of 315

PS unions must be careful over return-to-office

Author: Kurl, Shachi

Publication info: Kurl, Shachi. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ps-unions-must-be-careful-over-return-office/docview/3060678747/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PS+unions+must+be+careful+over+return-to-office&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-05-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Kurl%2C+Shachi&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: So, nearly 10,000 members of the Public Service Alliance of Canada who work for the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) have voted near-unanimously to strike if needed - which in turn could throw travel, tourism and, critically, the flow of goods in and out of this country into chaos.
The vote brings into sharper focus what has been to this point more of a tempest in the teapot that is the nation's capital: the threat of a so-called "summer of discontent," driven by the Trudeau government's decision to require federal public service employees to be at a physical place of work three days a week.
To be sure, there's plenty not to like about the way it's all happened: a lack of consultation alleged by union leaders caught off-guard by this policy change. The rationale, at least by some (including Ontario Premier Doug Ford), that such a change was needed because commercial life in downtown Ottawa continues to struggle. Valid questions over where many public servants, whether in the capital or other cities, might actually sit (or stand; standing is healthier) given their employer's existing commitment to sell many of its buildings.
But even as one powerful union is upping the ante in its seriousness over remote and telework issues, the reaction of Canadians, especially those of working age, and notably, even other public sector union members, shows a lack of ... solidarity ... on the issue.
"Return-to-office" has been a fraught process for employers and workers across any number of sectors in the post-pandemic world. In major Canadian cities, office towers remain unfilled, although downtown cores are arguably busier now than were the eerie tumbleweed-downmain-street days of 2020 and 2021. Those who worked from home during that period (or who continue to) say if anything, they were as productive as they had been in-office. As this country began to emerge from the worst of COVID-19, many remote employees vowed they'd quit their jobs rather than return fulltime to a physical place of work.
But data soon to be released by the Angus Reid Institute also shows that while a slice of these folks did, indeed, make good on their intentions, many more have come back to their workplace, in some cases fully relieved and glad to see their colleagues face-to-face again. This perhaps goes some way to explaining why six-in-10 Canadians support the federal government increasing the number of days its employees will be required to return to a physical place of work.
Consider also that nearly half of public sector union members (47 per cent) also support this requirement, according to polling released by the Institute. To be sure, these respondents are not all federal union members: they may be nurses, teachers or others who work for various levels of government and carry a union card. But their views represent an important reality check as this conflict threatens to intensify.
A significant segment among public sector union members, fully one-third of them, also say the Trudeau government should stand firm on its three-days-in-office requirement, even if it means service disruptions.
Generational and gender differences drive opinion on this matter. Younger women are much more likely to oppose increasing the time-at-physical-work requirement. Older men, many of whom have already retired or are near retirement age, are most supportive. Fairly or unfairly, regardless of demographics, the vast majority (75 per cent) of those surveyed believe federal government employees'working conditions are "much better" than those of other employed Canadians.
The last time CBSA workers took job action, few were travelling. It could will be different this time. Given the current public mood, striking workers may not have the general population on their side, especially if Canadians face the prospect of endless airport and border lineups. The unanswered question remains: who will feel more discontent this summer, and which side will blink first? Shachi Kurl is president of the Angus Reid Institute, a national, not-for-profit, non-partisan public opinion research foundation.

Document 86 of 315

Poilievre accused of avoiding issue of federal workers return to office; Conservative leader remains evasive when asked for his opinion on policy

Author: Morrison, Carine

Publication info: Morrison, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-accused-avoiding-issue-federal-workers/docview/3060122832/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+accused+of+avoiding+issue+of+federal+workers+return+to+office%3B+Conservative+leader+remains+evasive+when+asked+for+his+opinion+on+policy&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-05-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Morrison%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Leader of the Official Opposition of Canada Pierre Poilievre has declined to take a firm stance on the federal government's decision to increase its employees'office presence. While one expert has deemed Poilievre's silence to be "surprising," he says there are multiple political reasons the Conservative leader could be choosing not to state his opinion on the issue.
The federal government updated its policy on remote work earlier in May, saying it would require public servants to spend three days a week in the office by mid-September. Executives will be expected to be on-site at least four days per week.
The decision has been met with frustration from employees and unions, some of whom have filed unfair labour practice complaints and policy grievances.
When asked at a media availability last week about his thoughts on the policy change and whether he thought federal employees should work in their offices three days a week, Poilievre responded by commenting on the increase in the size of the public service under Liberal Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, service delays and government spending on outside consultants, arguing that the work should be brought "in-house."
"Everyone should be working five days a week," Poilievre told reporters. "It's clear that, after nine years of Trudeau, he's not worth the cost. It's time for a government that delivers more for less, that delivers value for money. That is common sense."
Sebastian Skamski, Poilievre's director of media relations, clarified in an May 16 email that the leader's comment was not about being "in-office" and that Conservatives wanted the public service working five days a week, "as they already are."
Skamski said no other comments were available, including on the return-to-office policy.
Patrick Fafard, a professor at the University of Ottawa's graduate school of public and international affairs, said the most likely reasons the Conservative leader had not taken a stance on the issue were because many private-sector workers across Canada were in favour of remote work and because his team was just trying to avoid the issue.
"The Conservatives want to paint themselves as being not the same as the Liberals," Fafard said. "In a way, the Liberals have already occupied the space that the Conservatives could otherwise, so this is not a winning issue for them."
Shachi Kurl, president of the Angus Reid Institute, said one of the notable things about the messaging from Poilievre and the Conservatives was that, when they went "off-script" and talked about issues that were not necessarily top of mind for Canadians, there was "a greater risk."
"I think some of this is just message discipline," Kurl said, noting that topics that had been "moving the needle" included inflation, government spending and crime.
"If there's no differentiation or contrast, why talk about it?" Kurl said remote work was a much larger issue, and one that was more resisted, in Ottawa than in other parts of the country, given how much of the local population worked in the public service. According to the Government of Canada website, 42.2 per cent of employees are in the National Capital Region.
"In many ways, this is an Ottawa issue," Kurl said. "Maybe Pierre Poilievre hasn't said very much about it because he's an Ottawa-area MP."
The Angus Reid Institute shared the results of a survey of more than 1,900 Canadians that found that the majority of respondents (59 per cent) supported the government's new policy. That outcome even remained true among respondents who were in public-sector unions, with 47 per cent indicating they supported the decision versus 41 per cent who said they opposed it.
"That in and of itself is a fairly striking finding," Kurl said. "It may give reason for the unions to take a moment to reflect, to pause on their stance."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford has urged the federal government to send its workers back to the office.
Despite the premier's arguments about on-site work supporting downtown Ottawa and its businesses, Fafard said there was "no natural Conservative position" on the issue, as "businesses themselves are all over the map" about remote work.
Fafard said Poilievre's silence was surprising given that his team had previously taken "strong," "rural" and "working-class Canada" positions on other issues.
"It's a bit mysterious," Fafard said, adding that "five days a week" is the line people would expect the Conservatives to emphasize. "Unless finally, finally, finally they figured out that they need to shift and be more nuanced and this is the first example of that."
Another possibility, Fafard said, is that the party is "trying out lines" to see what sticks with voters. He said Poilievre might also be waiting to see how the public reacted to a skirmish between the government and public-sector unions.
"Maybe the Conservative position will shift over time," Fafard said. "The billion-dollar question is will any of this affect federal government services? That remains to be seen."
Chris Aylward, president of the Public Service Alliance of Canada, said in a statement that Poilievre was "doing everything possible to reinvent himself as a friend of workers," but that "his abysmal track record on public services and workers speaks for itself."
PSAC has promised the government a "summer of discontent" as it plans to fight the updated return-to-office policy alongside other unions.
"Dodging this issue is just another example of Poilievre speaking out of both sides of his mouth when it comes to workers'rights," Aylward said.
Aylward said it was important that Poilievre tell workers where he stood on the "critical" issue.
"Does he support the future of work for Canada's largest employer, or does he stand arm-in-arm with the Trudeau Liberals and their regressive stance on telework?" Aylward asked.
Fafard said that, while unions are going to try to fight the on-site policy, they're "probably going to fail."
"Maybe the Conservatives are correct to try and ignore it because it's just gonna fade to black," he said. "So why waste political capital on an issue that won't be an issue in a while?"

Subject: Work at home; Conservatism; Federal government; Federal employees; Polls & surveys; Government spending; Onsite

Document 87 of 315

'Ottawa: two rivers, three bureaucracies'; Readers not shy about suggestions to help new 'night mayor' boost city's reputation

Author: Deachman, Bruce

Publication info: Deachman, Bruce. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ottawa-two-rivers-three-bureaucracies-readers-not/docview/3060122798/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Ottawa%3A+two+rivers%2C+three+bureaucracies%27%3B+Readers+not+shy+about+suggestions+to+help+new+%27night+mayor%27+boost+city%27s+reputation&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-05-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=Deachman%2C+Bruce&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When I was a youngster, the two-month summer holiday was divided into two. July was filled with more activities than it could reasonably hold, while August was just the opposite: a month-long void during which the sheer stillness of life in Ottawa weighed on me like a heavy blanket. My sole pastime each August was complaining: "I'm bo-o-o-r-r-red!" to which my mother always replied, "Well, whose fault is THAT?" If she thought I was going to concede that it was mine, she had another think coming. It was Ottawa's fault; there was nothing interesting to do here.
Judging by many of the more than 130 suggestions I received after a column last week urging readers to invent fresh mottoes for the city (our current official motto, "Advance -Ottawa -En Avant" is rather staid), I'm far from the only person to have experienced Ottawa ennui.
I don't imagine, for example, that reader Jack Waghorn was being entirely complimentary when he suggested "Ottawa: All the Excitement of Winnipeg, But Colder" and "Ottawa: The Saskatoon of the East." Nor was Malcolm Drury, who submitted "Ottawa: Closed for Maintenance."
Rather than submit a motto, one reader, Georges Clermont, simply wrote, "There is no motto however snappy that will make Ottawa anything but BORING." My mother would disagree, but instead I'll let David Alburger's suggestion for a motto do the talking: "If you're not having fun, the problem is YOU."
Meanwhile, the motto "Ottawa: Two Rivers, Three Bureaucracies" offered by Grant Boyd was one of the kinder suggestions regarding the city's many political roles. Government was the area where readers'frustrations spilled over into spleen-venting.
"Incompetence we guarantee!" wrote Callie Joist.
"Ottawa: Where Incompetence Reigns Supreme" echoed Maxime Verite.
"Ottawa: Over-governed and Under-led," was George Schoenhofer's submission.
"LRT, not as bad as Phoenix," suggested Ross Couchman.
"Ottawa: Where Your Tax Dollars Go To Die," wrote Terry White.
Fred Hughes turned political bluster into a positive with "Ottawa: Bask in the Hot Air!" while others looked at the thermometer from the other end. "Ottawa: "The City Where Cold is Minted," wrote Sevilla G. At least I assume she meant the weather, but perhaps she intended it more as a comment on the residents, similar to Michael Bussiere's motto: "Sorry, We're Just Not That Friendly." Waghorn, meanwhile, included a more weather-balanced quip when he suggested "Ottawa: Three Weeks of Summer - We Just Don't Know When."
Bruce Stewart, after pointing out what he views as many of the city's flaws, including Lansdowne, LRT, parking and snow removal, came up with the all-encompassing "Ottawa: The City Where You Just Can't."
Of course, none of these will likely help Ottawa's soon-to-beappointed Nightlife Commissioner, a.k.a. night mayor, create a livelier version of our city - which was why I sought reader input on a motto in the first place.
Some clever responses are simply too long to fit on a highway sign welcoming visitors. I can imagine motorists errantly driving off the road while taking in all of Boyd's "Ottawa: Too Long for Wordle, One Tile Shy of a Scrabble Bingo" or Waghorn's "Ottawa: Between Toronto and Montreal, and Don't Slow Down (We Need the Money from the Traffic Cameras)." I loved another of Waghorn's suggestions: "Ottawa: Doug Ford Just Found Us - Why Don't You?" If massaging Ottawa's image to help improve its nightlife is a goal, then you have to award full marks to Guy Milne's "U OTTAWAntoparty here" (read it aloud for the full effect) and Rakesh Misra's "Ottawa: Where Bad Decisions are Made - Make Yours Tonight!" Another favourite motto suggestion came via Ben Kutner, who, when asked to don his thinking cap, came up with "Ottawa: Canada's Thinking Capital."
John Holmes riffed off of one of the city's most famous exports with "Ottawa: Isn't It Ironic?" and a fishing movie with "Ottawa: A Canal Runs Through It."
Owing to the sheer geographic size of Ottawa, Peter Sharp offered "The City of Endless Horizons."
I confess, though, that I'm saddened every time I think of Beverley McKiver's proposed motto, "Ottawa: It Used to be Nicer," because, after reading her email, I recognize that it was born of her genuine love for a city she sees as decaying. In contrast, I find great hope and promise in Megan Piercey Monafu's "Ottawa: It Won't Initiate It, But it Will Hug You Back." And there's something very Canadian in Joseph Rikhof's "Ottawa: From Boast to Boast to Boast."
And readers offered plenty to boast about, including Boyd, again, with "OTTAWA: Proud to Capitalize All its Letters." Many others homed in on the physical beauty of the area. Nancy from Stittsville submitted handfuls of mostly alliterative mottoes, such as "Bytown Beauty" and "Capital Charm," although my favourite of hers was "Tulip Town," for its almost cartoonish whimsy. It conjures some imaginary mash-up of the Chinatown and Who Framed Roger Rabbit? movies: "Forget it, Jake, it's Tulip Town."
Others painting scenic landscapes in their mottoes included Matt's "Ottawa: City of beauty, culture and festivals," Don Pajot's "Ottawa the Beautiful" and Helen Tikhonova's "Ottawa: The Garden-Green Capital."
Still others looked to more aspirational heights, such as Marty McCormack's "Demonstrating -The Best! Demontrant -Le Meilleur," Barb Grant's "City on the Rise" and Dear Mayer's "For a Better Future." Two readers, Lucie Masson and Betty Hunt, submitted "Ottawa, Try It, You'll Like It," while, much to my surprise, only one, James Wiens, incorporated one of the city's most iconic dishes, with "Ottawa: The Shawarma Capital, Where Flavours Unite the Nation."
My favourite, though, came from expat Ottawan and now Prince Edward County resident Tom Curran, one of a few readers who invoked the city's post-amalgamation heart shape when he suggested "The Heart of the Nation." I truly - it.
Thanks to all who played along.
Postscript: Reader Douglas Cornish believes that what Ottawa needs more than a motto or slogan is an unforgettable song, similar to John Kander and Fred Ebb's classic New York, New York - made famous by Frank Sinatra - and George Cory and Douglass Cross's I Left My Heart in San Francisco, popularized by Tony Bennett. "A successful song would outlast any slogan and would be forever associated with this city," Cornish wrote. Just something to think about. We'll talk more about it later this summer. You can reach columnist Bruce Deachman at bdeachman@postmedia.com.

Subject: Bureaucracy

Location: New York; United States--US

Identifier / keyword: youngster; summer; holiday; divided; filled; activities

Title: 'Ottawa: two rivers, three bureaucracies'; Readers not shy about suggestions to help new 'night mayor' boost city's reputation

First page: A.18

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: May 25, 2024

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Ottawa, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Document 88 of 315

Kurl: 'Summer of discontent'? Federal public service should tread carefully in return-to-office dispute

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/kurl-summer-discontent-federal-public-service/docview/3059924078/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Kurl%3A+%27Summer+of+discontent%27%3F+Federal+public+service+should+tread+carefully+in+return-to-office+dispute&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: So, nearly 10,000 members of the Public Service Alliance of Canada who work for the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) have voted near-unanimously to strike if needed - which in turn could throw travel, tourism and, critically, the flow of goods in and out of this country into chaos.

To be sure, there's plenty not to like about the way it's all happened: a lack of consultation alleged by union leaders caught off-guard by this policy change. The rationale, at least by some (including Ontario Premier Doug Ford), that such a change was needed because commercial life in downtown Ottawa continues to struggle. Valid questions over where many public servants, whether in the capital or other cities, might actually sit (or stand; standing is healthier) given their employer's existing commitment to sell many of its buildings

But data soon to be released by the Angus Reid Institute also shows that while a slice of these folks did, indeed, make good on their intentions, many more have come back to their workplace, in some cases fully relieved and glad to see their colleagues face-to-face again. This perhaps goes some way to explaining why six-in-10 Canadians support the federal government increasing the number of days its employees will be required to return to a physical place of work.
Consider also that nearly half of public sector union members (47 per cent) also support this requirement, according to polling released by the Institute. To be sure, these respondents are not all federal union members: they may be nurses, teachers or others who work for various levels of government and carry a union card. But their views represent an important reality check as this conflict threatens to intensify.
A significant segment among public sector union members, fully one-third of them, also say the Trudeau government should stand firm on its three-days-in-office requirement, even if it means service disruptions.
Generational and gender differences drive opinion on this matter. Younger women are much more likely to oppose increasing the time-at-physical-work requirement. Older men, many of whom have already retired or are near retirement age, are most supportive. Fairly or unfairly, regardless of demographics, the vast majority (75 per cent) of those surveyed believe federal government employees' working conditions are "much better" than those of other employed Canadians.
The last time CBSA workers took job action, few were travelling. It could will be different this time. Given the current public mood, striking workers may not have the general population on their side, especially if Canadians face the prospect of endless airport and border lineups. The answered question remains: who will feel more discontent this summer, and which side will blink first?
Shachi Kurl is president of the Angus Reid Institute, a national, not-for-profit, non-partisan public opinion research foundation.

Document 89 of 315

Pierre Poilievre's failure to take stance on remote work 'surprising,' expert says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pierre-poilievres-failure-take-stance-on-remote/docview/3059913634/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pierre+Poilievre%27s+failure+to+take+stance+on+remote+work+%27surprising%2C%27+expert+says&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Leader of the Official Opposition of Canada Pierre Poilievre has declined to take a firm stance on the federal government's decision to increase its employees' office presence. The federal government updated its policy on remote work earlier in May, saying it would require public servants to spend three days a week in the office by mid-September. Executives will be expected to be on-site at least four days per week.
The decision has been met with frustration from employees and unions, some of whom have filed unfair labour practice complaints and policy grievances.
When asked at a media availability last week about his thoughts on the policy change and whether he thought federal employees should work in their offices three days a week, Poilievre responded by commenting on the increase in the size of the public service under Liberal Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, service delays and government spending on outside consultants, arguing that the work should be brought "in-house."
"Everyone should be working five days a week," Poilievre told reporters. "It's clear that, after nine years of Trudeau, he's not worth the cost. It's time for a government that delivers more for less, that delivers value for money. That is common sense."
Sebastian Skamski, Poilievre's director of media relations, clarified in an May 16 email that the leader's comment was not about being "in-office" and that Conservatives wanted the public service working five days a week, "as they already are."
Skamski said no other comments were available, including on the return-to-office policy.
Patrick Fafard, a professor at the University of Ottawa's graduate school of public and international affairs, said the most likely reasons the Conservative leader had not taken a stance on the issue were because many private-sector workers across Canada were in favour of remote work and because his team was just trying to avoid the issue.
"The Conservatives want to paint themselves as being not the same as the Liberals," Fafard said. "In a way, the Liberals have already occupied the space that the Conservatives could otherwise, so this is not a winning issue for them."
Shachi Kurl, president of the Angus Reid Institute, said one of the notable things about the messaging from Poilievre and the Conservatives was that, when they went "off-script" and talked about issues that were not necessarily top of mind for Canadians, there was "a greater risk."
"I think some of this is just message discipline," Kurl said, noting that topics that had been "moving the needle" included inflation, government spending and crime. "If there's no differentiation or contrast, why talk about it?"
Kurl said remote work was a much larger issue, and one that was more resisted, in Ottawa than in other parts of the country, given how much of the local population worked in the public service. According to the Government of Canada website, 42.2 per cent of employees are in the National Capital Region.
"In many ways, this is an Ottawa issue," Kurl said. "Maybe Pierre Poilievre hasn't said very much about it because he's an Ottawa-area MP."
The Angus Reid Institute shared the results of a survey of more than 1,900 Canadians that found that the majority of respondents (59 per cent) supported the government's new policy. That outcome even remained true among respondents who were in public-sector unions, with 47 per cent indicating they supported the decision versus 41 per cent who said they opposed it.
"That in and of itself is a fairly striking finding," Kurl said. "It may give reason for the unions to take a moment to reflect, to pause on their stance."
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre speaks during a news conference in Vancouver on May 14.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford has urged the federal government to send its workers back to the office.
Despite the premier's arguments about on-site work supporting downtown Ottawa and its businesses, Fafard said there was "no natural Conservative position" on the issue, as "businesses themselves are all over the map" about remote work.
Fafard said Poilievre's silence was surprising given that his team had previously taken "strong," "rural" and "working-class Canada" positions on other issues.
"It's a bit mysterious," Fafard said, adding that "five days a week" is the line people would expect the Conservatives to emphasize. "Unless finally, finally, finally they figured out that they need to shift and be more nuanced and this is the first example of that."
Another possibility, Fafard said, is that the party is "trying out lines" to see what sticks with voters. He said Poilievre might also be waiting to see how the public reacted to a skirmish between the government and public-sector unions.
"Maybe the Conservative position will shift over time," Fafard said. "The billion-dollar question is will any of this affect federal government services? That remains to be seen."
Chris Aylward, president of the Public Service Alliance of Canada, said in a statement that Poilievre was "doing everything possible to reinvent himself as a friend of workers," but that "his abysmal track record on public services and workers speaks for itself."
PSAC has promised the government a "summer of discontent" as it plans to fight the updated return-to-office policy alongside other unions.
"Dodging this issue is just another example of Poilievre speaking out of both sides of his mouth when it comes to workers' rights," Aylward said.
Aylward said it was important that Poilievre tell workers where he stood on the "critical" issue.
"Does he support the future of work for Canada's largest employer, or does he stand arm-in-arm with the Trudeau Liberals and their regressive stance on telework?" Aylward asked.
Fafard said that, while unions are going to try to fight the on-site policy, they're "probably going to fail."
"Maybe the Conservatives are correct to try and ignore it because it's just gonna fade to black," he said. "So why waste political capital on an issue that won't be an issue in a while?"
Our website is your destination for up-to-the-minute news, so make sure to bookmark our homepage and sign up for our newsletters so we can keep you informed.

Today's letters: Yes, Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones should resign

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-yes-ontario-health-minister-sylvia/docview/3059825139/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+Yes%2C+Ontario+Health+Minister+Sylvia+Jones+should+resign&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The health minister just doesn't get it
Re: Family doctor group calls for Ontario health minister's resignation over 'slap in the face' comments, May 10.
The Union of Family Physicians is calling for the resignation of Ontario's health minister. Her comments, stating that the diminished supply of physicians is of no concern, go way beyond tone-deaf. In fact, Sylvia Jones showed a complete lack of knowledge about the reasons for the poor state of health care in (even though they're clear to most of us). There are more than two million Ontarians without doctors.
Our current family physician, whom we will lose at the end of July with no replacement in sight, is an energetic young lady. She obviously does not wish to continue in family medicine, along with many others who are either opting to leave this field or to never go into it in the first place. Look at the facts, Ms. Jones. CHEO also recently shared its difficulties in treating children in an appropriate timeframe. And it goes on.
Therefore, I, too, am calling for the minister to resign. We need someone who at least appreciates the full and complicated scope of the problems and shows a willingness to listen to those who suggest solutions. Then to do something about it, quickly. While we're at it, Doug Ford should also take some responsibility for this massive gaff and resign too.
Thank goodness that those of us without family doctors will enjoy his legacy as we drown our sorrows and dull our aches and pains with "buck a beer."
Joanne Rohe, Stittsville
Those dandelions aren't the problem
Re: Greenhouse gas targets - where, exactly, is Canada's plan to meet them? May 17.
I agree with Tom Spears. Canadians and their governments are an unteachable lot when it comes to the environment.
If Canadians put half the passion and work into the climate as they do into their pointless and poisonous battles against the harmless dandelion, there would be no climate crisis in Canada.
Thomas Brawn, Orléans
Even Robert Pickton deserves safety
Re: Prison attack on serial killer Robert Pickton, May 21.
While few will have much sympathy for Robert Pickton, the fact remains that persons incarcerated deserve a safe environment. The authorities have all the tools needed to keep inmates safe, yet we often read of prison attacks. If Pickton is unsafe, so are all others in penitentiaries, jails or holding cells. It needs to be remembered that not all being held are guilty of the charges against them.
David Polk, Blackburn Hamlet
You CAN vote for 'none of the above'
Re: Letter, I can't vote for any of the above, May 17.
There is a way. Present yourself to vote, and when they give you the ballot you give it back saying you decline to vote. This indicates there is no party that deserves your vote and this is registered and counted in the official tally. Election workers fully know about this choice as I have done it twice with no questions asked.
Nicole Beggs, Orléans

Document 92 of 315

Reaching out to 'lost Canadians'; Liberals table bill extending Citizenship rights to Children born Abroad

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reaching-out-lost-canadians-liberals-table-bill/docview/3059793253/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reaching+out+to+%27lost+Canadians%27%3B+Liberals+table+bill+extending+Citizenship+rights+to+Children+born+Abroad&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadians born abroad who have children overseas will be able to pass along their citizenship under new legislation introduced into the House of Commons on Thursday.
Immigration Minister Marc Miller tabled the legislation, which aims to close loopholes that have created so called "lost Canadians" - people with ties to the country who are unable to pass along their citizenship to their children.
Under current rules, Canadians who have children abroad can only pass along their citizenship if their children were born in Canada. The then-Conservative government of Stephen Harper changed the law in 2009 so that Canadian parents who were born abroad could not pass down their citizenship to children who were also born abroad.
The legislation would automatically confer citizenship rights to children born since 2009 who were affected by the Conservatives' changes.
It would also create a new test for children born after the legislation comes into force.
Parents who were born outside of Canada will need to have spent at least three years in Canada before the birth or adoption of their child to pass on their Canadian citizenship.
The government has no idea how many people will be automatically granted citizenship if the legislation is passed.
"We're a country that supports human rights, equality, and respect for all people," Immigration Minister Marc Miller said in the foyer outside of the House of Commons after he tabled the bill.
Miller said there have to be rules for citizenship, but they must be reasonable.
"There's no doubt that Canadian citizenship is highly valued and recognized around the world. We want citizenship to be fair, accessible, with clear and transparent rules," he said.
NDP MP Jenny Kwan, who is supporting the Liberal bill, said it was past time the issue was addressed. She said Canadians travel the world to work and study and should be able to hold onto their citizenship rights.
"Canada is a global community of Canadians: people travel, people study abroad, people work abroad, people fall in love abroad, people have families abroad."
The Ontario Superior Court of Justice ruled last year that the 2009 change to the law was unconstitutional, and gave Ottawa until June 19 to fix the problem.
"This is an example of Conservatives having taken away Canadians'rights and something they hold most dear to them, in their citizenship," Miller said.
The Conservatives did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
The government may have to request an extension while the bill makes its way through Parliament, Miller said, but he doesn't want to wait long to fix the issue because people are being prejudiced in the meantime.
National Post, with files from The Canadian Press

Subject: Study abroad; Citizenship; Canadians; Legislation; Immigration

Location: Canada

Document 93 of 315

Federal unions rally for work-at-home amid mayoral event; Public servants call for more flexibility while Sutcliffe, Anand chat at city hall

Author: Morrison, Carine

Publication info: Morrison, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/federal-unions-rally-work-at-home-amid-mayoral/docview/3059793033/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Federal+unions+rally+for+work-at-home+amid+mayoral+event%3B+Public+servants+call+for+more+flexibility+while+Sutcliffe%2C+Anand+chat+at+city+hall&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Morrison%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Members of the Canadian Association of Professional Employees and other federal unions rallied in front of Ottawa City Hall on Thursday morning to condemn the federal government's decision to increase public servants'required office presence.
Treasury Board President Anita Anand was the special guest at Mark Sutcliffe's monthly Mayor's Breakfast on Thursday, a two-hour event that included presentations and an interview between Sutcliffe and Anand.
The Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat updated its policy on remote work this month, requiring public servants to spend at least three days a week in the office by mid-September. Executives will be expected on-site at least four days a week.
Members from various unions, including the Public Service Alliance of Canada (PSAC), the Professional Institute of the Public Service of Canada (PIPSC) and the Union of Safety and Justice Employees (USJE) attended the rally hosted by CAPE. Many held signs with messages such as "housing not offices" and "telework keeps life local."
Sonia O'Brien-Colterman, US-JE's regional vice-president for the RCMP in the National Capital Region, said that, while many of the union's members worked on-site, a "large contingent" did administrative work, especially in the Ottawa region.
"Because of the way the Treasury Board has phrased this, everybody's just all lumped in together," O'Brien-Colterman said. "We'd just like the option to have some flexibility."
At the breakfast event, Anand spoke with Sutcliffe about her political journey and the work she's done in various government positions.
When asked about remote work, the minister said the decision was made by the public service and was not a political decision. She said the return to the workplace had been "difficult," adding COVID-19 was "so hard on our workforce."
"Hybrid work is important," Anand said. "It is important to have flexibility. It is important to be able to hear from public servants and employees generally about what they need to cope in the workplace."
Anand said the change from two to three days on-site was "still in the range" of the policy that was in place before she became Treasury Board president, adding that people's focus should be on the possibility of accommodations and exceptions for public servants who need them.
"I really have faith that we will do more with the telework options so that public servants feel continually supported," Anand said.
Anand said in a statement to this newspaper that the policy update was an administrative decision taken by TBS, working with the Privy Council and deputy ministers from across the government. The minister said she had and would continue prioritizing relationships with unions, adding she had met "on several occasions" with leaders from across the public service.
Member of Parliament for Ottawa-Vanier and former Treasury Board president Mona Fortier said there was still a "very strong hybrid model" in place.
The decision to change the remote-work policy has induced frustration from employees and unions, with several groups filing unfair labour practice complaints and policy grievances.
PSAC and CAPE promised the government a "summer of discontent" over the issue, including letter-writing campaigns and encouraging their members to contact Anand and their members of Parliament to ask them to withdraw the forthcoming decision.
The campaign organized by CAPE stated that the federal government was "bending to political pressure" rather than supporting its employees.
Before the decision was announced, Ontario Premier Doug Ford urged the federal government to send its workers back to the office.
While Sutcliffe has said the change would be beneficial for downtown Ottawa and public transit, he told reporters on Thursday that he "never made any request of the federal government privately or publicly with regard to the number of days that employees are working in the office."
Sutcliffe said he was excited to see new ideas from the Ottawa Board of Trade on downtown revitalization. Their strategy, released Wednesday, outlined that 40,000 more people could live downtown by 2034 through various measures, including repurposing federal buildings.
CAPE president Nathan Prier said Thursday that it was "really unclear" what justified the policy update, noting that no data or explanation had been provided and the 2024 budget committed to converting half of federal buildings into housing.
"We think that the path through all of this is to grant remote work rights, have an idea of what you actually need for office space for occasional meetings, for collaboration, and then start really coming up with a community plan," Prier said, adding the union was likely going to support the Ottawa Board of Trade's plan for downtown.
A report by the Angus Reid Institute, published Thursday, found a majority of Canadians (59 per cent) say they support public servants spending more time in the office.

Subject: Work at home; Federal government; Presidents; Politics; Employees; Telecommuting; Onsite; Flexibility

Document 94 of 315

More than 700 vehicles have been reported stolen across Ottawa this year. Who can fix this?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/more-than-700-vehicles-have-been-reported-stolen/docview/3059791048/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=More+than+700+vehicles+have+been+reported+stolen+across+Ottawa+this+year.+Who+can+fix+this%3F&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Theresa Kavanagh was recently out in her ward handing out signs to residents in hopes that it might curb a growing auto theft problem. The message on the signage was: "All valuables removed."
Since the beginning of 2024, Ottawa's Bay ward has had the second-highest reported number of stolen vehicles, with 59 including incidents of break-ins and joy-riding, a marked increase from 2023.
"People know that this is a trend across the country, but we were surprised by the high numbers (in the ward)," said Kavanagh, city councillor for Ward 7 Bay.
More than 700 vehicles have been reported stolen across the city this year, according to the Ottawa Police Services' interactive crime map.
Gloucester-Southgate Ward leads the list for vehicle theft so far in 2024 with 64 reports.
"At the end of the day, cases being identified by organized crimes are extremely lucrative and we need to actively get ourselves organized to address this," says David Hill, the Ward 3 councillor for Barrhaven West.
Last year, Barhaven was the "hot spot", with more than 180 reported vehicle thefts. This year, however, Barrhaven has seen a significant decrease, with only 38 theft reports in the Barraven West ward and 18 in Barrhaven East.
Hill attributes Barhavven's reduced theft rates to increased collaboration with government stakeholders and the private sector, as well as increasing police presence.
"I spoke to a number of concerned residents about neighbourhood watch, so I think there's more vigilance and awareness in the community," he said.
Sign placed in vehicle: 'All valuables have been removed'
Despite the decrease in theft rates in his ward, Hill insists that auto theft remains a community problem that needs addressing at multiple government levels.
"This is a cross-jurisdictional issue, and part of the problem is that one level of government can't solve this issue alone," Hill said, calling for investments in proper scanning technologies at the Port of Montreal to ensure cargo legality and prevent the shipping of stolen goods overseas.
Hill also advocates for technological solutions at the manufacturer and insurance levels, such as air tags and auto shut-off technology.
All three levels of government are taking notice of the issue. The federal government announced earlier this week a three-pronged national action plan to combat organized crime groups involved in auto theft. The plan includes new criminal code offenses, 14-year maximum sentences instead of 10 for violent thefts, more intelligence sharing domestically and abroad, and heightened border inspections to decrease exports of stolen vehicles across the Canadian border.
In an attempt to combat auto theft in Ontario, Premier Doug Ford's government proposed new legislation for convicted auto thieves: a 10-year license suspension for first offenses, and a lifetime suspension for third offenses.
Police are also taking stronger action. Barrhaven West councillor Wilson Lo highlighted the recent operation at the Port of Montreal in April 2024 where nearly 600 stolen vehicles were recovered. Lo said he believes such operations send a strong message that the police are actively tackling auto theft concerns.
"Even though [auto theft] is not a crime against a person, you still feel violated when something of yours is stolen," Lo said. "Incidents like these certainly shake your confidence in how safe you feel in your community and probably confidence in the police and political leaders."
Lo said the federal government's involvement is "obviously" very helpful given that many stolen cars are shipped overseas through the Port of Montreal. But he still advises his residents to take preventative measures.
"Put a club on your steering wheel, if you have to park it outside. Put your keys at the back of the house far away. And don't leave any valuables in your car."

Document 95 of 315

Union holds rally for remote work outside of Mayor's Breakfast event

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/union-holds-rally-remote-work-outside-mayors/docview/3059408325/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Union+holds+rally+for+remote+work+outside+of+Mayor%27s+Breakfast+event&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Members of the Canadian Association of Professional Employees and other federal unions rallied in front of Ottawa City Hall on Thursday morning to condemn the federal government's decision to increase public servants' required office presence.
Treasury Board president Anita Anand was the special guest at Mark Sutcliffe's monthly Mayor's Breakfast on Thursday, a two-hour event that included presentations and an interview between Sutcliffe and Anand.
The Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat updated its policy on remote work earlier this month, requiring public servants to spend at least three days a week in the office by mid-September. Executives will be expected on-site at least four days a week.
Members from various unions, including the Public Service Alliance of Canada (PSAC), the Professional Institute of the Public Service of Canada (PIPSC) and the Union of Safety and Justice Employees (USJE) attended the rally hosted by CAPE. Many held signs with messages like "housing not offices" and "telework keeps life local."
Sonia O'Brien-Colterman, USJE's regional vice-president for the RCMP in the National Capital Region, said that, while many of the union's members worked on-site, a "large contingent" did administrative work, especially in the Ottawa region.
"Because of the way the Treasury Board has phrased this, everybody's just all lumped in together," O'Brien-Colterman said. "We'd just like the option to have some flexibility."
Members of CAPE and other federal unions rallied in front of Ottawa City Hall on Thursday morning.
'Hybrid work is important,' says Anand
Anand spoke with Sutcliffe about her political journey and the work she's done in various government positions.
When asked about remote work, the minister said the decision was made by the public service and was not a political decision. She said the return to the workplace had been "difficult," noting that COVID was "so hard on our workforce."
"Hybrid work is important," Anand said. "It is important to have flexibility. It is important to be able to hear from public servants and employees generally about what they need to cope in the workplace."
Anand said that the change from two to three days to three days on-site was "still in the range" of the policy that was in place before she became Treasury Board president, adding that people's focus should be on the possibility of accommodations and exceptions for public servants who need them.
"I really have faith that we will do more with the telework options so that public servants feel continually supported," Anand said.
Anand said in a statement to this newspaper that the policy update was an administrative decision taken by TBS, working with the Privy Council and deputy ministers from across the government. The minister said she had and would continue prioritizing relationships with unions, noting that she had met "on several occasions" with leaders from across the public service.
Member of Parliament for Ottawa-Vanier and former Treasury Board president Mona Fortier said there was still a "very strong hybrid model" in place.
The decision to change the remote work policy has induced frustration from employees and unions, with several groups filing unfair labour practice complaints and policy grievances.
PSAC and CAPE promised the government a "summer of discontent" over the issue, including letter-writing campaigns and encouraging their members to contact Anand and their members of Parliament to ask them to withdraw the forthcoming decision.
'We need to work together,' says Sutcliffe
The campaign organized by CAPE stated that the federal government was "bending to political pressure" rather than supporting its employees.
Before the decision was announced, Ontario Premier Doug Ford urged the federal government to send its workers back to the office.
While Sutcliffe has said the change would be beneficial for downtown Ottawa and public transit, he told reporters on Thursday that he "never made any request of the federal government privately or publicly with regard to the number of days that employees are working in the office."
Sutcliffe said he was excited to see new ideas from the Ottawa Board of Trade on downtown revitalization. Their strategy, released Wednesday, outlined that 40,000 more people could live downtown by 2034 through various measures, including repurposing federal buildings.
CAPE's president, Nathan Prier, said Thursday that it was "really unclear" what justified the policy update, noting that no data or explanation had been provided and that the 2024 budget committed to converting half of federal buildings into housing.
"We think that the path through all of this is to grant remote work rights, have an idea of what you actually need for office space for occasional meetings, for collaboration, and then start really coming up with a community plan," Prier said, adding that the union was likely going to support the Ottawa Board of Trade's plan for downtown.
A report by the Angus Reid Institute, published Thursday, found that a majority of Canadians (59 per cent) supported public servants spending more time in the office.

Document 96 of 315

Speaker faces new calls to resign; Conservatives Call For Fergus To Step Down Over 'Partisan'Invite To Event In Riding

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/speaker-faces-new-calls-resign-conservatives-call/docview/3058509200/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Speaker+faces+new+calls+to+resign%3B+Conservatives+Call+For+Fergus+To+Step+Down+Over+%27Partisan%27Invite+To+Event+In+Riding&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Conservatives are calling once more for Greg Fergus to resign as House of Commons Speaker, arguing that an advertisement for an event in his riding used "very partisan, inflammatory language" concerning Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
The Liberal Party of Canada recently advertised a "Summer Evening with the Honourable Greg Fergus" in Hull, Que., on June 4 as an "exciting opportunity to join fellow Liberals and talk about the ways we can continue to build a better future for all Canadians."
"While Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives propose reckless policies that would risk our health, safety, and pocketbooks, our Liberal team is focused on making life more affordable for Canadians and moving forward with our bold plan," read the description.
Liberal party spokesperson Parker Lund said in an email that there was a "miscommunication" between the party and Fergus's riding association, "which led to the wrong text being put on the website," but he added that it is "now being corrected."
"Even as Speaker, Mr. Fergus remains the Member of Parliament for Hull-Aylmer. It is very common for MPs to hold summer events to thank and show their appreciation to local supporters and volunteers," said Lund.
On Tuesday morning, the event was briefly "no longer available" before reappearing on the party's website with a new description. It now invites Liberals to "enjoy a fun-filled Summer kick-off BBQ with good cheer, refreshing drink, and appetizing food."
Mathieu Gravel, director of outreach and media relations for the Speaker's office, said that the clerk of the House of Commons had raised "no concerns" with the event itself since it is taking place in Fergus's riding, which is allowed.
But the Hull-Aylmer Liberal association was apparently not aware that the event would be posted on the party's website. "The text used on the website was a generic text produced by the Party, and it had not been approved by Mr. Fergus's team," said Gravel.
It is not the first time that Fergus has faced calls to resign as Speaker. The Conservatives and Bloc Québécois said they could not support him after he filmed a partisan video in his office for an Ontario Liberal party event.
At the time, Fergus said he was prepared to resign if he lost the confidence of the House, but the NDP decided to give him one more chance to stay on as Speaker, saying that this type of partisan behaviour "cannot happen moving forward."
More recently, Conservatives renewed their calls for Fergus to resign after he expelled Poilievre from the House of Commons last month for calling Prime Minister Justin Trudeau an "extremist" and a "wacko" and refusing to withdraw his comments.
The official Opposition argued that Fergus had given Trudeau a pass when he accused Poilievre of showing what "spineless leadership" looks like moments before the "wacko" comment. Fergus asked Trudeau to reframe his question in a less offensive way, which he did.
Conservative MP and whip Chris Warkentin raised the latest partisan issue in a lengthy letter to Fergus on Tuesday and called on him to step down.
"It has simply become impossible to make any distinction now between Speaker Greg Fergus and Liberal Greg Fergus. Every ruling you give now - and, with hindsight, every ruling you have given - will now all be assumed to have been delivered with a red tint.
"You have failed at showing, and being seen to show, the impartiality required of a Speaker; in turn, you can no longer count on the trust and goodwill of Members from all corners of the House," reads the letter.
Warkentin later raised a question of privilege establishing a prima facie case of contempt in the House about the description of the Liberal event, which he said was "yet another instance of public display of partisanship on the part of our Speaker."
"I would submit that the Speaker must take personal responsibility for what his local team, the Hull-Aylmer federal Liberal association - whose past president, I would add, is the Speaker's chief of staff - has organized and published," he said.
Warkentin also called on New Democrats to stay "true to their word" and say they have lost confidence in the Speaker, thus forcing him to step down.
Bloc House Leader Alain Therrien said Fergus has once more demonstrated "a lack of judgment and neutrality," and it is time for him to do the honourable thing and step down.
"In all honesty, I'm asking myself more and more, without partisanship, what more will it take for the NDP and the Liberals to say: it's enough?" NDP whip Heather Mc-Pherson said the new information brought to light is "of course very worrying" but indicated her party would not take a position right away.
"We would like to reserve the right to come back at some point in the future," she said.
"As with all points of privilege, it's important for us to look at this very clearly."
National Post calevesque@postmedia.com

Document 97 of 315

Housing minister trying to convince cities to avoid development fee hikes; Fraser defends record despite drop in starts

Author: Tumilty, Ryan

Publication info: Tumilty, Ryan. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/housing-minister-trying-convince-cities-avoid/docview/3058509198/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Housing+minister+trying+to+convince+cities+to+avoid+development+fee+hikes%3B+Fraser+defends+record+despite+drop+in+starts&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.4&amp;au=Tumilty%2C+Ryan&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Housing Minister Sean Fraser said he is trying to coax big city mayors to keep development charges steady with new federal funding after some major Canadian cities recently raised fees for new housing construction.
The government has chosen to address the problem of development charges through a new program, the Housing Infrastructure Fund, which will give municipalities funding to build new infrastructure, like water and sewer lines, provided they don't increase charges, said Fraser, who appeared Tuesday at the House of Commons infrastructure committee to talk about his department's spending in the coming year.
Conservative MP Leslyn Lewis asked why there was nothing in the government's original Housing Accelerator Fund to prevent cities from increasing their development charges.
"Can you confirm that freezing or lowering development charges was not a precondition of any housing accelerator agreements," she asked Fraser.
Fraser said the Conservatives don't have any plan to address the problem.
"I'll note one point of contrast between our plans is the Conservative plan that was put forward includes no measures to address the issue of development cost charges," Fraser said. "If you want to raise the issue with someone who has not been clear on their position, you can talk to your party leader or perhaps you could write to the provincial Conservative government of Ontario."
After Toronto signed a housing accelerator deal last year, worth $471 million, it raised development charges for new construction by 20 per cent, with the cost developers need to pay for a one-bedroom unit rising to over $44,000. Ottawa also recently raised its fees, going ahead with the increase despite a warning from Fraser that it would cut the city off from future funds.
Development charges are meant to cover some of the costs a new development brings to a city, including things like water and sewer costs, as well as roads, parks, community centres and police. Most communities in the country charge some fees with differences about the overall cost and what is included.
Fraser defended his government's efforts despite a decline in new housing starts and cities across the country raising fees for new builds.
The Liberals have spent $4 billion through the Housing Accelerator Fund, giving cities across the country money in exchange for them agreeing to remove restrictive zoning rules and build more houses Housing starts declined in April 2024, the most recent available data, compared with the year prior, with 2.2 per cent fewer homes starting construction this year as compared with 2023. They were also lower across all of 2023 compared with 2022.
The Trudeau government pledged in the recent budget to build 3.9 million homes to get the country back to an affordable housing market.
Lewis asked Fraser to explain why his government's housing plan wasn't working.
"In the city of Toronto since the Liberals signed their housing accelerator funding agreement and that was in December 2023, housing starts are down by 21 per cent," she said.
Fraser said there were many reasons that housing starts were down including higher interest rates, but said his government was putting more on the table all the time to try to reverse that direction.
"We continue to put more measures on the table such as tax cuts, changes to municipal zoning and other measures to help speed up the pace of construction," he said.
Conser vative Leader Pierre Poilievre introduced a private member's bill on housing last year that would require cities to build 15 per cent more housing every year and would penalize communities that don't do that by taking away grants.
Poilievre's housing proposal would also require apartment buildings and other high density projects to be built around transit stations.
Fraser's proposal has a similar requirement, which he said is good not just for people looking for a place to live, but for the transit systems.
"We're not just saying we're transferring money, carte blanche to build out a system with no federal input," he said. "We want to make sure that we have enough riders for those systems and want to increase density within walking distance to the transit stations."
National Post rtumilty@postmedia.com

Document 98 of 315

Richardson: Banning cellphones in Ontario classrooms should be just the first step

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/richardson-banning-cellphones-ontario-classrooms/docview/3057690040/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Richardson%3A+Banning+cellphones+in+Ontario+classrooms+should+be+just+the+first+step&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-05-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Education Minister Stephen Lecce's decision to ban distracting smartphones from Ontario classrooms is a first step toward improving student engagement in learning. But it's only a start. Educators should also revamp teaching approaches that drive bored kids to turn to their cellphones in the first place.
Canadian provinces spend more than $85 billion a year to fund a school system that does a terrific job of turning kids off education. Rates of student engagement - meaning enjoyment in and commitment to learning - fall precipitously from kindergarten to Grade 12. This is a tragic squandering of human potential. As the OECD notes, "it takes engagement and motivation to learn." Disengaged students skip school, misbehave and zone out.
As parents of young athletes, Swifties, sports fans, gamers, techies, movie lovers, artists and musicians know, kids don't naturally become less curious or motivated as they age. It's more that our school system works pretty systematically to smother their love of learning. Educational historian Larry Cuban blames "the grammar of schooling": practices such as age-based groupings, rigidly separated subjects and periods, arbitrary units of study, and written exams that have been around for so long we think of them as impossible to change.
Many schools in 2024 are similar to 19th-century schools designed to train soldiers and factory workers. They are often ill-suited to our rapidly changing economy and dynamic society, and fail to connect with students raised with digital devices at their fingertips. Do we honestly expect kids to thrive when they are told to do worksheets from the website "Teachers Pay Teachers," read books disconnected from their identities and lives, or complete "science experiments" with predetermined outcomes? It's no wonder so many long to connect with the world at large via their phones. Who can blame them?
To take advantage of cellphone-free classrooms, educators could follow the example of eastern Ontario's Upper Canada District School Board (UCDSB), which is replacing the old grammar of schooling with "Real World Learning" (RWL), a pedagogical framework that leads students to learn through meaningful projects with community organizations such as businesses, historical archives, animal sanctuaries, seniors' residences, newspapers, libraries, theatres and hockey arenas.
I'm currently researching the effects of RWL and what I see is impressive and often quite moving. Primary students at Almonte's Naismith Memorial Public School creating businesses to raise funds for their local youth centre. Secondary history students at Prescott's South Grenville District High School figuring out ways to animate the St. Lawrence shoreline near where the steamship "Conestoga" sank in 1922. Nationview Public School students in South Mountain practising French while growing produce for a food bank. In each case, skilful teachers weave the Ontario curriculum through all that students do, but the difference is that students take the lead and there is purpose behind the learning. Kids and teachers love it.
Dave Balfour, principal of Elgin's South Crosby Public School, notices students heading out daily to learn in their community. "I'm seeing kids really excited about the things they are doing," he told me. "They take ownership of the work they're doing, and the school, in ways I haven't seen before. We're done with the paper/pencil worksheets, the textbooks, filling out the same thing every year. That's not engaging.
"I can think of three, maybe four occasions in the last six months, when teachers have been in my office in tears of joy because of the work their kids are doing. That's how powerful this pedagogy is."
Educators can capitalize on the cellphone ban by giving kids a reason to pay attention in the first place. The UCDSB's Real World Learning approach provides a made-in-Ontario blueprint for making school matter to young people eager to learn, grow and contribute to their communities.
John M. Richardson teaches at the uOttawa Faculty of Education.
Related
MacPherson and Zwaagstra: Doug Ford's smartphone restrictions in schools don't go far enough
Sherazi: Cellphones provide checks and balances in class, not just distractions

Subject: Public schools; Cellular telephones; Classrooms; Smartphones; School systems; Learning; Education; Teachers; Student participation; School boards

Document 99 of 315

Greenhouse gas targets: where's the plan?; Cutting emissions as population grows isn't really working, Tom Spears says

Author: Spears, Tom

Publication info: Spears, Tom. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/greenhouse-gas-targets-wheres-plan-cutting/docview/3056284194/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Greenhouse+gas+targets%3A+where%27s+the+plan%3F%3B+Cutting+emissions+as+population+grows+isn%27t+really+working%2C+Tom+Spears+says&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-05-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=Spears%2C+Tom&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's great that the federal Liberals want to create more housing. Homes are good. But we can't ignore that each new home (and new car, and new person) creates more greenhouse gases. Energy-efficient new buildings don't replace old buildings'emissions; they add to them. And today the federal Liberal plan is to add up to 3.9 million homes.
As well, last month Ontario presented the Cutting Red Tape to Provide More Homes Act, opening up more rural land for new subdivisions.
More people bring more greenhouse gas emissions, even as we pledge blindly, stupidly, to reduce them. So let's stop pretending that we can cut emissions in a fast-growing population.
As a kid in 1967, I had to sing a dopey centennial song: "Caaa-naaa-daaa, now we are 20 million!" But we've pushed past 40 million now, with emissions rising even as we claim to lead the world in achieving net zero emissions - promising to reduce them by 40 to 45 per cent by 2030, only six years away, though we have made no movement in that direction.
Carmakers have vastly reduced the pollutants that cause smog, but can't change the fact that burning fuel produces carbon dioxide, period.
I was there with Catherine McKenna, then-minister of the Environment, when she said in 2019 that technology would advance in ways we cannot foresee, as it has with cellphone tech (nice campaign newser, with falafels). On the carbon-burning front, we're still waiting. As with the Kyoto Protocol, we have signed up for ambitious targets with no idea how to reach them.
And here's a little-discussed side note: building new homes and the rest requires concrete. That alone is a big greenhouse gas problem.
Scientific American reports that "worldwide every year cement and concrete production generates as much as nine per cent of all human CO2 emissions." The journal Nature puts it slightly lower at eight per cent.
Yes, but if we drop gasoline though electrification? It takes around 25 years (optimistically) to plan and build a new nuclear plant. Our last, Darlington, was budgeted at $3.9 billion and came in at $14.4 billion in the 1990s, and we need a whole lot more of these to be carbon-free. Also, communities need to welcome a new nuclear plant. You think anyone will accept one in urban Ottawa? We are committed, as a nation, to reduce emissions by 40 to 45 per cent by 2030 - a scale we have never even approached.
The federal website on greenhouse gas projections shows two things: First, our emissions dropped sharply in 2008-09 (major recession) and again in 2020-21 (pandemic lockdown). So when people are out of work, emissions drop. But later they pick up.
Second, the feds give us actual emissions figures through 2022, largely showing no significant reductions, BUT from 2023 on, emissions are forecast to drop dramatically. These figures are just projections. I can't wait to see what the actual 2023 numbers look like, but I bet projections for 2024 will look just dandy.
Please stop pretending. If you claim we can reach net zero despite accelerating urban sprawl, show me how. And save the bragging till you've done it. Journalist Tom Spears is a former Ottawa Citizen environment reporter.
