Document 269 of 315

Issues surrounding access dog federal government's $10-a-day child-care system; Parents report difficulty finding openings

Author: Thomson, Stuart

Publication info: Thomson, Stuart. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/issues-surrounding-access-dog-federal-governments/docview/2926318292/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Issues+surrounding+access+dog+federal+government%27s+%2410-a-day+child-care+system%3B+Parents+report+difficulty+finding+openings&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.6&amp;au=Thomson%2C+Stuart&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Less than a year ago, the federal Conservatives unanimously voted in favour of the Liberal government's legislation to secure long-term funding for its burgeoning national childcare system.
Although Conservatives have traditionally favoured giving money directly to parents rather than socialized daycare programs, pollsters reportedly told the party it was foolish to resist the policy, which promised to grant parents $10-a-day care across the country.
Now, waiting lists are bursting, daycare providers are protesting that the program is driving them out of business, and childcare spots are drying up in some cities.
For the Conservatives, the political calculation may have changed.
The number of children under the age of five in child care fell by 118,000 between 2019 and 2023, a decrease of 8.5 per cent nationally, according to Statistics Canada data. In addition, 46.4 per cent of parents reported difficulty finding child care in 2023, which is up from 36.4 per cent in 2019.
Federal Conservatives used to oppose the Liberals'national childcare program, before they started voting for it. Polling showed that in the early stages of the government's daycare plans, Canadians were about equally likely to support giving cash to parents as universal daycare, said Andrea Mrozek, a senior fellow at Cardus, a public policy think tank that conducts extensive research on child care.
"It's always hard for politicians to take things away that people have, but people don't have this," said Mrozek. "As the system is the debacle that it is, I think the people who think money to parents is a win might increase. If it was 50-50, in 2021, I wonder what it would be today."
Government and proponents of the universal daycare program will point to the fact that average parental fees were lower in 2023 compared to 2022. But that's only for the people who can get spots.
"The people who actually are benefiting from $10-a-day child care are as happy as clams," said Gordon Cleveland, an economist at the University of Toronto and a proponent of universal daycare. "Fundamentally, the problem is there has been a huge amount of emphasis on improving affordability and very little on improving access."
But Cleveland said the problem in proposing an alternative to the current system is that the parents who are benefiting from the $10 program are saving thousands of dollars per year in fees and any program to replace that money with direct cash to parents would be massively expensive.
The key for the Liberals in preserving the program would be to work with the provinces to expand access, he said.
But the problems for other parents have been adding up. In Alberta, some childcare centres began rolling closures to protest what they described as a restrictive and underfunded program. In Ontario, the YMCA and other providers have been sounding the alarm, describing the funding model as unsustainable. In B.C., headlines about daycare shortages have been cropping up.
And a recent analysis by the Fraser Institute found that program wasn't even boosting the rate of women in the workforce, a key goal for any universal child care program. The labour force participation rate for women was 61.5 per cent in September 2023 compared to a high of 61.7 per cent in 2015, according to the study.
Opponents of the system like Mrozek argue that the current model tends to pay off for wealthier families, while leaving poor families out in the cold or on lengthy wait lists.
When researchers in B.C. scoured the province for low-income people who had been able to access $10-a-day child care, they only found 13 families. Dr. Lea Caragata, director of the school of social work at UBC and co-author of that study, told CTV News that the program needed to be massively expanded - and that it needed to be deemed off-limits for the wealthiest Canadians.
A report by the Childcare Resource and Research Unit, a non-profit, non-partisan policy research institute, about non-standard work found that "the benefits of government spending on social policy disproportionally accrue to the middle-and upper-class relative to other social groups."
"This can lead to families with lower socioeconomic status receiving the least support, while families who are already more advantaged receive more support," the report reads.
Cleveland said that any improvements on access will require political will and more funding from the provinces, which means the Trudeau government will be relying on conservative governments in some of Canada's largest provinces to expand the program.
He said there are lessons to learn from Prince Edward Island, which has massively boosted wages for early childhood educators, and Manitoba, which is rapidly building modular childcare centres that require less than year of construction time.
Some provincial governments may be biding their time, though, with the belief that it's not worth sinking too much money into a program that could be dramatically different under a potential Poilievre government.
"I think it's true that in Ontario and Alberta, and perhaps other provinces, they're not sure whether it's actually going to be going to be successful so they're sort of holding back," said Cleveland.
National Post stthomson@postmedia.com

Document 270 of 315

Ottawa to receive $176.3 million in federal funds for affordable housing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ottawa-receive-176-3-million-federal-funds/docview/2925590832/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+to+receive+%24176.3+million+in+federal+funds+for+affordable+housing&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa is getting a $176.3-million infusion for affordable homes as its share of the $4-billion federal housing accelerator fund.
The money, which comes after months of negotiations between the city and the federal government, will speed the construction of some 4,400 new homes over the next three years and more than 32,000 in total over the next decade.
The much-anticipated announcement came Monday at Mikinak, a three-building Ottawa Community Housing complex in Wateridge Village on the site of the former Rockcliffe airbase in the city's east end.
Paving the way for the money is the city's agreement to change its zoning regulation to allow four units to be built on any property in the city.
The deal for Ottawa commits the city to introduce a draft bylaw allowing four units as-of-right per lot by June, with final approval of the bylaw in 2025. It would also increase housing density around transit stations, speed the conversion of commercial office buildings to housing, cut red tape and streamline approval processes.
"Business as usual will not solve the drastic housing challenges that we face today," said Jenna Sudds, the minister of families, children and social development, MP for Kanata-Carleton and a former Ottawa city councillor. Sudds made the announcement on behalf of federal housing minister Sean Fraser.
The agreement brings "significant, lasting changes" to the way housing will be built in the city, she said.
When the city first applied for the money from the fund in 2022, it asked for about $150 million. Mayor Mark Sutcliffe credited city staff for negotiating for even more federal money. He called it "a down payment on getting things built faster."
"This funding won't solve the housing crisis on its own, but it is a giant leap forward for our city and allows Ottawa to take bold and immediate actions to mitigate the housing crisis, build more homes and build faster," Sutcliffe said.
"Our job at the City of Ottawa, and the with support of our federal partners, is to make it as easy as possible to build as fast as possible," Sutcliffe said. "And then it's up to the community, the development community and home buyers, to take it from there."
The "vast majority" of the $176.3 million is earmarked for building more homes "and more affordable homes in particular," he said.
Getting the federal government involved in the housing market to help co-ordinate the provincial and municipal governments is a vital step, said Jason Burggraaf, executive director of the Greater Ottawa Home Builders' Association.
"Obviously, the big piece is the four units per lot. We've seen that been the centrepiece of most agreements that have been reached across Canada. It's really going to help the city meet its intensification targets," Burggraaf said.
The city has set a goal of building 90,000 to 100,000 new homes over the next 20 years, half of that coming from increased density along transit lines and the other half from construction in existing neighbourhoods, he said.
"If you want to get 45,000 or 50,000 new homes in those neighbourhoods, you're talking about converting those single family lots into three or four multiple family units. You have to have the zoning in place to get that density."
In addition, the federal and provincial governments have agreed to eliminate the HST on purpose-built rental units that meet the four-per-lot threshold, he said.
"Now you've got a fiscal policy, the housing policy and the zoning you need to build that 'missing middle' of the housing sector," he said.
Increasing density is a politically sensitive issue, with frequent opposition from residents in older, established neighbourhoods with single family homes - the so-called R1 zoning - opposed to the arrival of large, denser multi-unit buildings. But Ontario's controversial Bill 23 outlawed such single-family home zoning and Fraser has made it clear that municipalities that don't comply with the federal goal of four units per lot will be shut out from the accelerator fund.
Earlier this year, the city of Windsor had its $70-million accelerator fund proposal rejected because council had approved four units per lot in only some areas of the city. Windsor Mayor Drew Dilkens said at the time the city wanted to protect its older neighbourhoods from excess densities and that was more important than the federal money.
In contrast, former Mississauga mayor Bonnie Crosbie last fall invoked the controversial "strong mayor powers" afforded by the province to approve fourplexes over her city council's objections. That paved the way for the city to get $112.9 million in accelorator funding. Crosbie then resigned to become leader of the provincial Liberals.
Sudds was asked Monday what would happen if Ottawa council can't come to an agreement on higher densities.
"Not to negotiate in public, but I think we're in a very good place," she replied. "The city has done an incredible amount of work to get to this point. We've reached an agreement that we have confidence in."
The federal housing accelerator fund will create 100,000 new homes in Canada over the next three years and more than half a million over the next decade, she said.

Seniors 'hit between the eyes' with skyrocketing pottery class fees

Document 271 of 315

Ontario Appeal Court rules Ford government's Bill 124 is unconstitutional

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-appeal-court-rules-ford-governments-bill/docview/2925517438/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Appeal+Court+rules+Ford+government%27s+Bill+124+is+unconstitutional&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An Ontario law that imposed wage restraint on public sector workers violated their collective bargaining rights and is unconstitutional, the province's Appeal Court ruled Monday, a decision many labour groups called a major victory.
The law from Premier Doug Ford's government - known as Bill 124 - capped salary increases for public sector workers to one per cent a year for three years.
A lower court struck it down as unconstitutional and the Appeal Court, in a 2-1 decision, largely upheld that decision, writing that the infringement couldn't be justified.
"Because of the Act, organized public sector workers, many of whom are women, racialized and/or low-income earners, have lost the ability to negotiate for better compensation or even better work conditions that do not have a monetary value," the court wrote in its majority opinion.
The Progressive Conservatives enacted the law in 2019 as a way to help the government eliminate a deficit. The province argued the law did not infringe constitutional rights, saying the charter only protects the process of bargaining, not the outcome.
Finance Minister Peter Bethlenfalvy said Monday that the government will examine the ruling "thoughtfully and deliberately" before deciding whether or not to seek leave to appeal to the Supreme Court of Canada.
The Ontario Appeal Court wrote that governments are entitled to try to hold compensation increases to a certain level, but the issue is how they do that.
"Ontario has not been able to explain why wage restraint could not have been achieved through good faith bargaining," the court wrote.
"In the absence of any evidence for the need for expediency or that the same goal cannot be achieved through collective bargaining, it is hard to understand on what basis the Act's salutary effects outweigh its beneficial effects."
The Appeal Court found, however, that the lower court judge erred by striking the entire statute. The law applied both to unionized workers and those not represented by a bargaining organization, and the Appeal Court said the act is only unconstitutional for workers represented by unions, who have different rights because they bargain collectively.
In a dissenting opinion, Justice C. William Hourigan wrote that the evidence showed very real economic reasons for imposing wage restraint, and that the government did so instead of cutting services or jobs.
"According to the application judge's analysis, it would be permissible for the government to temporarily reduce wage costs when the economy was on the brink of collapse, but it would be unconstitutional for the government to act proactively to prevent the inevitable," Hourigan wrote.
"If a government sees an economic cliff on the horizon, courts should not require it to wait till the last moment to act."
The law had sparked widespread outrage among labour groups and opposition parties, with its effects on the health sector a particular focus, as critics argued it was partly responsible for driving nurses out of the profession or into private nursing agencies, where the pay is substantially higher for the same work.
"This sham of a bill has severely impacted access to and quality of care for Ontarians since 2019," said Ontario Nurses' Association provincial president Erin Ariss.
"The trauma inflicted on nurses and health care professionals because of Bill 124 has driven tens of thousands of us out of the health care system and away from the work that we love."
Monday's decision was seen as a big victory for unionized workers.
"I'm feeling like most broader public sector workers are feeling across Ontario: vindicated," said Steven Barrett, a lawyer representing Ontario Federation of Labour in the case.
"The Court of Appeal is clear that freedom of association and the protection of collective bargaining and the right to strike actually are meaningful."
Paul Cavalluzzo, who represents the Ontario English Catholic Teachers Association, said the government's financial records were its own undoing.
"Certainly the government's books now show that this legislation was not necessary, we were in a surplus situation," he said.
"They were not even spending all of the budget money on health and education - the bill was unnecessary."
Two unions representing health-care workers said in a joint statement that Monday's ruling was a win for families and all unions who fought to protect workers' rights to freely bargain a collective agreement.
"We urge Doug Ford to end his attacks on the very people we need to fix Ontario's worsening health-care system," the presidents of CUPE's Ontario Council of Hospital Unions and SEIU Healthcare wrote.
The union representing the province's public elementary teachers said the government never should have appealed the decision in the first place, as it "wasted" taxpayer dollars and undermined their recent contract negotiations.
"Let the court's ruling be a lesson for the Ford government to never circumvent bargaining or trample on workers' democratic rights again," the Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario wrote.
The government gained little from the law, said J.P. Hornick, president of the Ontario Public Service Employees Union.
"They've lost not just the respect of workers, but they've also cost us as workers an exponential lifetime of having to catch up," Hornick said.
"What they have gained is a reinvigorated labour movement."
Since the law was struck down, even while pending appeal, arbitrators have awarded additional retroactive pay to several groups of workers that had "reopener" clauses in their contracts, including teachers, nurses, other hospital workers, public servants, ORNGE air ambulance paramedics, and college faculty.
Several hospitals have told a legislative committee conducting pre-budget hearings that the Bill 124 reopener arbitration rulings are straining their budgets, though the government has committed to reimbursing them.
"Bill 124 settlements are driving hospitals into extraordinary cash flow difficulties, threatening our financial viability and forcing delay of critical capital purchases," Sherri McCullough, board chair of Kingston Health Sciences Centre, told the committee last month.
Ontario's financial accountability officer said in 2022 that Bill 124 was set to save the province $9.7 billion on public-sector salaries and wages, though a successful court challenge would all but wipe that out.
That could cost the province $8.4 billion over five years, he said.
Though the 2019 law was time limited for a three-year period, it has still been affecting collective bargaining because of when some previous contracts expired and the length of some negotiations.
The Canadian Press

Document 272 of 315

Ontario Appeal Court set to rule on constitutionality of wage restraint law Bill 124

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ontario-appeal-court-set-rule-on/docview/2925506161/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+Appeal+Court+set+to+rule+on+constitutionality+of+wage+restraint+law+Bill+124&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: TORONTO - Ontario's top court is set to rule today on the constitutionality of a law that limited raises for more than one million workers in the broader public sector, including nurses and teachers.
The Progressive Conservatives enacted the law, known as Bill 124, in 2019 as a way to help the government eliminate a deficit.
It capped salary increases for public sector workers to one per cent a year for three years.
The law sparked widespread outrage among labour groups and opposition parties, with its effects on the health sector a particular focus as critics say it's partly responsible for driving nurses out of the profession or into private nursing agencies, where the pay is substantially higher for the same work.
Labour groups and unions representing hundreds of thousands of public sector employees challenged the law, and the Ontario Superior Court in late 2022 found it infringed collective bargaining rights, striking it down as unconstitutional.
The government appealed and the Appeal Court says its decision will be released today.
Since the law was struck down, even while pending appeal, arbitrators have awarded additional retroactive pay to several groups of workers that had "reopener" clauses in their contracts, including teachers, nurses, other hospital workers, public servants, ORNGE air ambulance paramedics, and college faculty.
Several hospitals have told a legislative committee conducting pre-budget hearings that the Bill 124 reopener arbitration rulings are straining their budgets, though the government has committed to reimbursing them.
"Bill 124 settlements are driving hospitals into extraordinary cash flow difficulties, threatening our financial viability and forcing delay of critical capital purchases," Sherri McCullough, board chair of Kingston Health Sciences Centre, told the committee last month.
Ontario's financial accountability officer said in 2022 that Bill 124 was set to save the province $9.7 billion on public-sector salaries and wages, though a successful court challenge would all but wipe that out.
That could cost the province $8.4 billion over five years, he said.
Though the 2019 law was time limited for a three-year period, it is still affecting collective bargaining now because of when some previous contracts expired and the length of some negotiations.
Ontario Provincial Police recently started bargaining for a new contract, which would be subject to Bill 124 if the law was in force.
As well, the province's correctional officers late last year received increases of 9.5 per cent over three years after Bill 124 was struck down partway through their bargaining process.
Whichever way the Appeal Court ruling goes, one of the sides may seek leave to appeal at the Supreme Court.
This report by The Canadian Press was first published Feb. 12, 2024.

Document 273 of 315

Fewer doctors on duty amid cuts; End of COVID funding raises alarm in Queensway Carleton Hospital ER

Author: Payne, Elizabeth

Publication info: Payne, Elizabeth. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fewer-doctors-on-duty-amid-cuts-end-covid-funding/docview/2925317201/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fewer+doctors+on+duty+amid+cuts%3B+End+of+COVID+funding+raises+alarm+in+Queensway+Carleton+Hospital+ER&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Payne%2C+Elizabeth&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Queensway Carleton Hospital, which describes its emergency department as one of the busiest in Ontario, has fewer physicians on duty there as of this week, and emergency doctor hours will be cut further in April, officials say.
The cuts are the result of the provincial government winding down special COVID-19 pandemic funding.
The situation, in the midst of a widespread emergency department crisis, is causing alarm.
"It means we are not going to be able to provide as many physician hours of coverage and care. It means we have to cut back shifts," said Dr. Michael Herman, an emergency physician at the hospital who is a spokesperson for the Canadian Association of Emergency Physicians.
"Canada-wide, we see the state of emergency departments. We see people waiting hours on end, and we hear about the harms that are coming from people waiting hours and hours. Cutting these hours is only going to exacerbate the problem."
The funding cuts are part of the Ontario government's move to end special pandemic funding for hospitals.
During the pandemic, the government increased the number of emergency physician hours it would pay for by 20 per cent. It began to gradually reduce those physician hours starting last April.
Because physician emergency department staffing is based on volumes, the cuts weren't felt at Queensway Carleton until now. Last week, the hospital learned the province would fund four fewer hours a day of emergency physician coverage than it had been supporting.
As of April, there will be 10 fewer hours a day of physician coverage in the hospital's emergency department, a hospital spokesperson said.
Although that suggests volumes in the emergency department are now closer to what they were pre-pandemic, Herman says that is misleading and that it will worsen wait times in emergency departments.
"I think anyone who has been in an emergency department knows that quote-unquote lower volumes are still really high and we are certainly not in the pre-pandemic world anymore," he said.
Herman says the acuity and complexity of patients coming to the emergency department has changed in recent years, in part because many of them no longer have primary caregivers. That means many required more time than the current funding formula allows for. That funding model for determining how many emergency physicians are required is based on an old study, he said. It is currently being updated by the province. He also notes that the funding model does not count patients who leave without being treated, a growing problem as emergency department waits increase.
Over the weekend, the hospital posted an advisory on its website asking people who were coming to the emergency department to be prepared to wait. "Please be patient as we experience longer than usual wait times in our ED due to patient volumes."
Emergency departments across Ontario and Canada have been experiencing historically long wait times for care due, in part, to nursing shortages, shortages of hospital beds and increasingly complex patient needs. Reducing the number of physicians working will make the situation worse, Herman said.
This past week, Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau signed a $3.1-billion health funding deal under which Ontario will hire more health workers. During a media conference, Ford suggested stretched emergency departments would be on his radar.
"I'm going to be zoned in on these emergency departments," he said in response to a question about ballooning emergency wait times.
In a statement, a spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones said the additional funding for emergency physicians "was part of temporary, extraordinary measures funding given to hospitals during the COVID-19 pandemic to reimburse them for the costs incurred as a result of patient surge due to the global pandemic. Hospitals across the province received this temporary reimbursement funding and were notified well in advance of when this temporary funding would be wound down."
Herman says the timing to roll back the funding for emergency doctors doesn't make sense.
"I think our volumes and wait times reflect that this is absolutely necessary (physician) coverage. This is coverage that is keeping our head above water with us paddling as fast as we can," he said.
"For all the talk of investing in health care, this sure feels like the opposite."

Subject: Hospitals; Emergency medical care; Departments; Funding; Pandemics; Physicians; COVID-19

Business indexing term: Subject: Hospitals Physicians

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: queensway; carleton; hospital; describes; emergency; department

Title: Fewer doctors on duty amid cuts; End of COVID funding raises alarm in Queensway Carleton Hospital ER

Publication title: The Ottawa Citizen; Ottawa, Ont.

First page: A.1

Publication year: 2024

Document 274 of 315

Pandemic funding cuts mean fewer emergency doctors at Queensway Carleton Hospital

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/pandemic-funding-cuts-mean-fewer-emergency/docview/2924745245/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pandemic+funding+cuts+mean+fewer+emergency+doctors+at+Queensway+Carleton+Hospital&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Queensway Carleton Hospital, which describes its emergency department as one of the busiest in Ontario, has fewer physicians on duty there as of this week, and emergency doctor hours will be cut further in April, officials say.
The cuts are the result of the provincial government winding down special COVID-19 pandemic funding.
The situation, in the midst of a widespread emergency department crisis, is causing alarm.
"It means we are not going to be able to provide as many physician hours of coverage and care. It means we have to cut back shifts," said Dr. Michael Herman, an emergency physician at the hospital who is a spokesperson for the Canadian Association of Emergency Physicians.
"Canada-wide, we see the state of emergency departments. We see people waiting hours on end, and we hear about the harms that are coming from people waiting hours and hours. Cutting these hours is only going to exacerbate the problem."
The funding cuts are part of the Ontario government's move to end special pandemic funding for hospitals. During the pandemic, the government increased the number of emergency physician hours it would pay for by 20 per cent. It began to gradually reduce those physician hours starting last April.
Because physician emergency department staffing is based on volumes, the cuts weren't felt at Queensway Carleton until now. Last week, the hospital learned the province would fund four fewer hours a day of emergency physician coverage than it had been supporting. As of April, there will be 10 fewer hours a day of physician coverage in the hospital's emergency department, a hospital spokesperson said.
Although that suggests volumes in the emergency department are now closer to what they were pre-pandemic, Herman says that is misleading and that it will worsen wait times in emergency departments.
"I think anyone who has been in an emergency department knows that quote-unquote lower volumes are still really high and we are certainly not in the pre-pandemic world anymore," he said.
As of April, there will be 10 fewer hours a day of physician coverage in the Queensway Carleton Hospital's emergency department, a hospital spokesperson said.
Herman says the acuity and complexity of patients coming to the emergency department has changed in recent years, in part because many of them no longer have primary caregivers. That means many required more time than the current funding formula allows for. That funding model for determining how many emergency physicians are required is based on an old study, he said. It is currently being updated by the province. He also notes that the funding model does not count patients who leave without being treated, a growing problem as emergency department waits increase.
Over the weekend, the hospital posted an advisory on its website asking people who were coming to the emergency department to be prepared to wait. "Please be patient as we experience longer than usual wait times in our ED due to patient volumes."
Emergency departments across Ontario and Canada have been experiencing historically long wait times for care due, in part, to nursing shortages, shortages of hospital beds and increasingly complex patient needs. Reducing the number of physicians working will make the situation worse, Herman said.
This past week, Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau signed a $3.1-billion health funding deal under which Ontario will hire more health workers. During a media conference, Ford suggested stretched emergency departments would be on his radar.
"I'm going to be zoned in on these emergency departments," he said in response to a question about ballooning emergency wait times.
In a statement, a spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones said the additional funding for emergency physicians "was part of temporary, extraordinary measures funding given to hospitals during the COVID-19 pandemic to reimburse them for the costs incurred as a result of patient surge due to the global pandemic. Hospitals across the province received this temporary reimbursement funding and were notified well in advance of when this temporary funding would be wound down."
Herman says the timing to roll back the funding for emergency doctors doesn't make sense.
"I think our volumes and wait times reflect that this is absolutely necessary (physician) coverage. This is coverage that is keeping our head above water with us paddling as fast as we can," he said.
"For all the talk of investing in health care, this sure feels like the opposite."
Related
Vaccination gap has local officials scrambling as measles cases surge around the world
Ottawa clinics, care providers waiting to learn if they can take on more patients

Subject: Hospitals; Emergency medical care; Patients; Departments; Funding cuts; Pandemics; Physicians

Document 275 of 315

Federal government signs $3.1-billion health accord deal with Ontario

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/federal-government-signs-3-1-billion-health/docview/2924248019/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Federal+government+signs+%243.1-billion+health+accord+deal+with+Ontario&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario will spend $3.1 billion in federal health-care funding on increasing access to family doctors, reducing backlogs and adding more health-care workers, the premier and the prime minister announced Friday as they formalized a decade-long health accord.
The funding covers three years out of the $8.4-billion 10-year agreement in principle the federal government reached last year with the province, part of an overall $198.6 billion for health funding across the country. Ontario is the fifth province to officially sign on for its share.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau said the money will help ease pressures that health systems across the country are facing.
"Access to family doctors and nurse practitioners is not what it needs to be," he said at a news conference.
"Emergency rooms are overwhelmed. People are waiting too long for surgeries and health-care workers ... are under immense pressure, working in incredibly trying conditions. So we are focused on addressing those challenges."
Premier Doug Ford said Ontario has made progress on shortening wait times and increasing access to primary care, but there is more to do.
"This $3.1 billion investment will be used for targeted initiatives to continue building up our health workforce, improve access to family health services, enhanced mental health and addiction programs, and expand access to digital health," he said.
"It will help to ensure that people across Ontario will continue to get the high quality convenient care they need when they need it."
The Ontario Nurses' Association questioned the rosy statistics presented at the news conference by the premier and Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones, including numbers of new nurses in the province.
"If we have so many new nurses, why is it that Ontario is seeing hundreds of emergency departments continuing to have to close due to nurse staffing shortages?" president Erin Ariss wrote in a statement.
Ontario's auditor general found in a report late last year that between July 2022 and June 2023 there were 203 temporary emergency department closures across the province.
Amid that strain on the system, one in five patients who visited ERs were only there because they did not have a family doctor, and they did not need urgent care, the report found.
According to the Ontario Medical Association, 2.3 million people in Ontario do not have a family doctor, and the association predicts that number will double within two years.
"Ontario's doctors welcome this funding that will help address the most urgent issues," OMA president Dr. Andrew Park wrote in a statement.
"While we tackle the most urgent issues, we also need to ensure we have a long-term, stable funding formula to fix the underlying issues in system and build for future, knowing we have an aging and more medically complex patient population."
The OMA said it had hoped Ottawa would increase the federal share of health-care costs from 22 per cent to 35 per cent, as provinces had urged. The agreement boosts the percentage to 24 per cent.
Many of the items Ontario committed to using the funding for are previously announced multi-year programs, and officials say it will help the province build on what is already underway.
The province says $30 million per year will help create more primary care teams, including doctors, nurse practitioners, nurses, pharmacists and social workers.
Ontario says it will also put $69 million per year to help internationally educated health professionals, including extending a program that enables internationally trained nurses to work in the province faster and launching a program to quickly assess whether an international doctor can practice immediately.
As well, the province has agreed to put $40 million per year toward expanding enrolment in medical schools, with 60 per cent of postgraduate spots dedicated to family medicine, and 71 positions specifically at the Northern Ontario School of Medicine.
All provinces that agree to the deal must also work to modernize their health data systems, starting with making patient records more accessible and transferable between jurisdictions.
Provinces must measure their progress against targets set by the Canadian Institute for Health Information and publicly report that progress each year.

Subject: Health care access; Primary care; Nurse practitioners; Funding

Document 276 of 315

Protesters rally for trans youth as Alberta premier visits capital; Province announced new rules to limit medications, restrict sports involvement

Author: Glass, Marlo

Publication info: Glass, Marlo. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/protesters-rally-trans-youth-as-alberta-premier/docview/2923181151/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Protesters+rally+for+trans+youth+as+Alberta+premier+visits+capital%3B+Province+announced+new+rules+to+limit+medications%2C+restrict+sports+involvement&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Glass%2C+Marlo&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Protesters gathered in downtown Ottawa on Monday to show support for transgender youth in light of Alberta Premier Danielle Smith's visit to the capital.
Last week, Smith announced that her United Conservative Party government would ban puberty blockers and hormone therapy for children 15 and younger who have not already begun those treatments. Her government also plans to require parental consent for kids 15 and under to go by a different name or pronoun at school. Teenagers aged 16-17 would not need permission, but schools would have to notify their guardians.
Gathering across from Parliament Hill, rallygoers chanted, "Trans rights are human rights," while a small group of counter-protesters held signs that read, "Thanks Danielle," and "Leave the kids alone."
Holly Brown attended the rally as a mother of a gender-diverse child, carrying a sign that read, "Blockers are saving my kid's life."
"There's not a question in my mind," she said, adding that hormone therapy, which delays physical changes brought on by puberty, should be accessible to all.
"If this came to Ontario tomorrow, my child's life would immediately be put at risk," Brown said.
While the Canadian Pediatrics Society says hormone blockers should not be prescribed before puberty, access to hormone blockers has been associated with lowering the risk of suicidal thoughts.
Fae Johnstone, one of the organizers of the rally and executive director of the Society for Queer Momentum, said the rally was joining parents, young people and advocates across the city, "speaking up against Danielle Smith's dangerous attacks on the rights of trans kids."
Smith's proposed legislation is the latest in a wave of policies dictating how trans and nonbinary students can identify in schools. Last summer, New Brunswick's provincial government made it mandatory for trans and nonbinary students under 16 to obtain parental consent in order for their preferred names and pronouns to be used by teachers.
Fellow Progressive Conservative Premier Scott Moe followed suit with a similar consent policy in Saskatchewan.
"It's not just kids, it's about rolling back social progress while we fight for a more free and equal future," Johnstone said. "So we've got to show up in numbers."
Liberal Employment Minister Randy Boissonnault told reporters earlier Monday he requested a meeting with Smith to discuss her proposed changes. Boissonnault, who is openly gay and the only Liberal cabinet minister from Alberta, said Smith is pushing "draconian" measures on some of the province's most vulnerable youth.
Smith was in town Monday to open a new provincial office in Ottawa, and also gave a luncheon speech to members of the Economic Club of Canada. - With files from Canadian Press

Subject: Non-binary gender; Puberty; Endocrine therapy; Consent

Identifier / keyword: protesters; gathered; downtown; ottawa; support; transgender

Document 278 of 315

Alberta Premier Danielle Smith in Ottawa as Liberals decry 'draconian' policies

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/alberta-premier-danielle-smith-ottawa-as-liberals/docview/2922593385/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Alberta+Premier+Danielle+Smith+in+Ottawa+as+Liberals+decry+%27draconian%27+policies&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith defended her controversial new suite of proposed transgender youth policies Monday in Ottawa as federal Liberals accused her of pushing an anti-LGBTQ agenda.
Smith announced last week that her United Conservative Party government would ban puberty blockers and hormone therapy for children 15 and younger who have not already begun those treatments.
Her government also plans to require parental consent for kids 15 and under to go by a different name or pronoun at school. Teenagers aged 16-17 would not need permission, but schools would have to notify their guardians.
The policies have spurred several days of protest.
There is no "single voice" that can speak on behalf of the entire transgender community, Smith suggested during an event in the national capital to mark the opening of a new provincial-federal liaison office.
She said has spoken to some transgender people who expressed concerns about children's ability to transition.
Smith defended the package of proposals as part of a "considered approach" she wants to take when it comes to children with diverse gender identities.
The goal is to ensure children are "fully informed" about the decisions they are making in the event they may regret them later in life, she added.
"We had to have a conversation about what is the appropriate ages to be able to make those life-altering decisions."
Earlier in the day, Liberal Employment Minister Randy Boissonnault said he requested a meeting with Smith to discuss the proposed changes.
Boissonnault, who is openly gay and the only Liberal cabinet minister from Alberta, said Smith is pushing "draconian" measures on some of the province's most vulnerable youth.
After some back-and-forth between their respective offices, the pair had been expected to meet later Monday.
The federal government's ability to ask for a Supreme Court reference on any use of the notwithstanding clause by provinces is an "important legal tool," Boissonnault noted.
"We will be playing very close attention to see what's in Premier Smith's legislation, which I hope never makes it to the floor of the (legislature)," he said.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, asked about the proposals during a news conference Monday in Brampton, Ont., said: "Let parents raise kids and provinces run schools and hospitals."
Poilievre did not specifically address Smith's proposals - notable, since his office told MPs last week to "refrain" from speaking about Alberta's specific measures, and to "flag" any related media requests that come in.
In Toronto, Ontario Premier Doug Ford signalled that his government had no plans to follow Alberta's lead.
"No. We have a law here and we're leaving everything alone," he said, echoing previous remarks from the province's health minister.
Local advocates in Ottawa, meanwhile, were organizing an "emergency" protest downtown later Monday in light of Smith's visit to the capital.
The Alberta government office Smith opened in Ottawa is intended to help advance the province's priorities and fortify federal-provincial ties, the Alberta government said in a news release.
It said the office would "help create stronger relationships with governments in Ottawa and across Canada while increasing the province's advocacy on matters of importance to Albertans."
The Canadian Press

Document 279 of 315

Ontario tells municipalities to find ways to reduce costs at daycares

Author: Jones, Allison

Publication info: Jones, Allison. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-tells-municipalities-find-ways-reduce/docview/2922287849/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+tells+municipalities+to+find+ways+to+reduce+costs+at+daycares&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Jones%2C+Allison&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario has ordered municipalities that directly operate childcare centres to do a value-for-money audit of their programs to determine if they could instead be operated by a "third party," raising concerns about privatization.
The move also has some advocates concerned the provincial government is looking to find savings on the backs of early childhood educators, as higher staff pay in municipal centres is often the reason they can cost more to run.
Ministry of Education funding guidelines sent to licensed childcare operators late last year say municipal childcare administrators should have the independent audits finished by the end of 2024.
"The purpose of the value for money audit is to determine whether provincial funding is being used efficiently and effectively by directly operated centres, and whether the child care services could be offered by a third party provider instead," the ministry wrote.
Funds from the federal $10-a-day program or regular provincial administration funding can be used to pay for the audits, it wrote.
The ministry has also told municipalities it is cutting administration funding. Ontario is the only province with a mandated role for municipalities in delivering child care and funding for that administration has until now come entirely from the province.
The City of Ottawa is working with the province to determine how the 10 city-run childcare centres will be impacted by the federal funding guidelines and the new provincial funding approach, Jason Sabourin, the city's director of children's services, said in an email.
Sabourin said in 2021 the city completed an internal review of its childcare services, including a full cost-benefit analysis, to ensure the city was serving families with barriers to accessing child care. The city will share the results of this audit with the province, "and will comply with any audit requirements," he said.
In the meantime, Ottawa "will continue to follow provincial direction and guidelines and remains committed to working collaboratively with all stakeholders to ensure the best possible outcomes for children and families in our community."
The province announced in 2019 it was asking municipalities to contribute 20 per cent of the cost of creating spaces, where the province had previously contributed all of it. The province also said administration funding would be split 50-50 going forward, but has since provided $220 million in transitional funding that is now expiring.
The Ontario Coalition for Better Child Care says that will amount to a cut of $85.5 million.
NDP childcare critic Teresa Armstrong says the system is in desperate need of more funding, not less.
"Many families are already having to drain their bank accounts to pay for child care, and providers such as the YMCA are warning of imminent closures if crisis funding isn't received," she wrote in a statement.
The ministry wrote in a memo to municipalities the transitional funding was in place due to impacts of the pandemic and to support the service managers while they "found efficiencies and adjusted their administrative cost structures."
A spokesperson for Education Minister Stephen Lecce said Ontario was committed to "optimizing the municipal childcare system to better serve families."
Others are not so sure.
Alana Powell, the executive director of the Association of Early Childhood Educators of Ontario, said it sounded like the province was trying to save money by reducing ECE wages.
Municipally run programs that have been through previous value-for-money audits often end up closed, Powell said. The Region of Waterloo announced in 2020 it was closing its five licensed childcare centres after an audit found it would save $7.1 million a year. The Canadian Press, with files from Marlo Glass, Postmedia News

Document 281 of 315

Supreme Court rules Ford government doesn't have to disclose mandate letters

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/supreme-court-rules-ford-government-doesnt-have/docview/2921516092/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Supreme+Court+rules+Ford+government+doesn%27t+have+to+disclose+mandate+letters&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Premier Doug Ford's government can keep marching orders to cabinet ministers confidential, the Supreme Court of Canada ruled Friday, marking an end to a long-running legal battle over access to the documents.
The CBC had asked - under the province's freedom-of-information law - for the letters written to ministers after Ford won the 2018 election, but the request was denied on the basis that releasing the documents would reveal the substance of cabinet deliberations.
The Information and Privacy Commissioner disagreed, ordering the government to disclose them, and two levels of Ontario courts also sided with the CBC.
But the Supreme Court ruled that the letters are exempt from public disclosure.
While the outcome was unanimous, one of the justices on the panel of seven who heard the case came to the same conclusion as the majority, but for different reasons.
"Freedom of information (FOI) legislation strikes a balance between the public's need to know and the confidentiality the executive requires to govern effectively," Justice Andromache Karakatsanis wrote for the majority.
"Both are crucial to the proper functioning of our democracy."
Ford's government had contended that cabinet confidentiality, candour and solidarity are fundamental to a system where responsible ministers collectively decide government policy, but the CBC argued disclosure is key to an informed public and accountable government.
While there is no doubt that public access to government-held information is vital to the democratic process, Karakatsanis wrote, in this case the information and privacy commissioner interpreted the freedom-of-information exemption around cabinet deliberations too narrowly.
"In our constitutional democracy, the confidentiality of Cabinet deliberations is a precondition to responsible government because it enables collective ministerial responsibility," Karakatsanis wrote.
"At base, Cabinet confidentiality promotes executive accountability by permitting private disagreement and candour in ministerial deliberations, despite public solidarity."
The information and privacy commissioner found that the letters indicate topics that may have arisen during a cabinet meeting or may be considered at a future meeting, and represent the "end point" of deliberations, rather than the substance.
But, Karakatsanis wrote, the commissioner did not give due consideration to the impact that "premature disclosure of policy priorities at an early stage of the process may have on the efficient workings of government."
"Far from being mere 'topics' like items on an agenda, the Letters reflect the views of the Premier on the importance of certain policy priorities, and mark the initiation of a fluid process of policy formulation within Cabinet," the court wrote.
"The Letters are revealing of the substance of Cabinet deliberations, both on their face and when compared against what government actually does."
Kathleen Wynne became the first Ontario premier to proactively disclose the letters, in 2014, with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and several other premiers following suit.
The 2018 Ontario mandate letters themselves were made public in September after a source provided them to Global News.
Ontario's opposition leaders said Ford should have released the letters and keeping them secret suggests he has something to hide.
"It's clear that almost every decision that this Conservative government has made is in the interest of a select few of their friends - not the best interests of Ontarians," NDP Leader Marit Stiles wrote in a statement.
"This government wasted five years and huge amounts of taxpayer dollars to keep these documents secret."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Ontarians deserve to know what the premier instructs his ministers to do.
"If this government were truly 'for the people,' this would include being for the people's right to know what their government is doing," she wrote in a statement.
"Ontario citizens should be able to trust that their premier will act in the public's best interest."
Related
'Rolling Thunder' protester who drove through police barricade found guilty of dangerous driving
Sanctions against Ottawa school board trustee stayed until judicial review is heard

Document 282 of 315

Let's shrink university administrations; Savings from cutting managerial bloat can be better used, says Robert Stacey

Author: Stacey, Robert

Publication info: Stacey, Robert. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/lets-shrink-university-administrations-savings/docview/2920914691/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Let%27s+shrink+university+administrations%3B+Savings+from+cutting+managerial+bloat+can+be+better+used%2C+says+Robert+Stacey&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Stacey%2C+Robert&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Without question, Ontario universities have been put in an impossible financial position because of government funding shortfalls and a freeze in student tuitions. As a professor and departmental chair, I have seen the results up close: increased class sizes; cancellation of elective courses; hiring freezes for tenured professors; the cranked-up exploitation of part-time instructors; cuts to extracurricular activities; cuts to student services; cuts to visiting speakers, conferences and workshops - cuts to practically everything that makes a university a secure and energizing space for intellectual development and creative thinking.
Though it is the right thing to do ethically and economically, the recently announced cap on international students will only make matters worse for universities that have kept the lights on by increasing the ranks of higher-paying international students - students who, too often, are left to languish in institutions that want their money, but aren't willing to do the hard (and costly) work of facilitating their transition to a different cultural and academic environment.
As it stands, students, the professoriate and front-line service and support workers are bearing the brunt of budgetary shortfalls for which they are in no way responsible. Increasing tuition fees is not the answer: students should be paying less, not more. (They are already ruining their studies by working so much.) Obviously, the provincial government needs to rethink its priorities and provide better funding.
But our increasingly corporate-minded university administrations need to take some responsibility and greatly reduce their share of university spending. At the same time that faculties have shrunk and departments are being nickel-and-dimed for refreshments, the ranks of upper management have grown significantly, along with the cost of consultants whose services those administrators have increasingly sought out.
A recent message from my university president thanking me in advance for my co-operation with respect to the next round of cuts deemed necessary to forestall the financial ruin of our institution blames only the provincial government and campus unions seeking salary adjustments following the judicial overturning of Bill 124 (which had limited civil service wage increases). I share my president's frustration with the Doug Ford government - and let's face it, with the voters who elected that government, failing to realize that their reluctance to pay taxes was going to hurt them and their children down the line. But the president's message gives little indication that the upper administration is prepared to bear a fair and reasonable proportion of these cuts.
Here are five things that university administrations must do before students, professors and campus workers can trust them to lead the fight against unfair provincial funding models: 1). Impose an across-theboard 10 per cent salary cut for all senior management, including university presidents and provosts. 2). Eliminate the greatest possible number of "associate-vice-president in charge of x" positions. Then repatriate those former professors back to their departments where they can teach classes. 3). Slash the offices of marketing and internal communications, as well as any position that begins with the word "strategic" or contains the words "brand" or "branding." 4). Place a moratorium on consultants and third-party contracts until the upper administration has reorganized itself in line with a learning-centred philosophy that places students, professors and researchers at the heart of the university. 5). Re-set labour relations so that administrations can work with, not against or in defiance of, campus unions. Unions need to be integrated into the decision-making processes of the university. The current climate of hostility and distrust between administrations and their university's unions (and, to some extent, among the union groups themselves) is making otherwise conscientious people short-sighted and mean.
There is a reason that university employees who make more than $100,000 a year are on the provincial Sunshine List: we are civil servants providing a service to Ontarians. Going forward, every university position should be obliged to justify itself in relation to a university's primary mandate, which is to educate students and produce knowledge. Savings generated by reducing bureaucratic and managerial bloat should then be redirected towards faculty renewal and better wages for frontline staff.
However unfair the current situation in which Ontario university's find themselves, it provides us with an opportunity to reimagine the size, function and culture of our universities'central administrations. Such thinking shouldn't have needed a budgetary crisis to occasion it. But if ever there was a time for professors, students and campus workers to refuse the creeping corporatization and managerialism of the university system, it is now. Robert Stacey is associate professor and chair of the department of English, University of Ottawa.

Subject: College presidents; Foreign students; Colleges & universities; Funding; College campuses; Extracurricular activities

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities

I just don't believe this is the time

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=I+just+don%27t+believe+this+is+the+time&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Stacey: It's time to shrink Ontario's university administrations

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/stacey-time-shrink-ontarios-university/docview/2920601081/se-2?accountid=15090

Document 285 of 315

Ont. plans to refurbish nuclear power plant; Government says work is needed to meet future electrical demands

Author: Jones, Allison

Publication info: Jones, Allison. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ont-plans-refurbish-nuclear-power-plant/docview/2920372279/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ont.+plans+to+refurbish+nuclear+power+plant%3B+Government+says+work+is+needed+to+meet+future+electrical+demands&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Jones%2C+Allison&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Power Generation is moving ahead with a plan to extend the life of the aging Pickering Nuclear Generating Station by decades as the province tries to secure more electricity supply in the face of increasing demand.
Energy Minister Todd Smith had asked OPG in 2022 to study the feasibility of refurbishing four of the units at the nuclear plant, which have been operating since the 1980s, and he announced Tuesday that the project would proceed.
"Our province still needs this station and its workers," Smith said at a media conference outside the nuclear plant. The construction phase will create about 11,000 jobs, he said, and provide about 6,000 jobs for decades.
OPG plans to spend $2 billion on engineering and design work and securing key components for the project that is expected to be completed in the mid-2030s.
Neither Smith nor OPG officials would give an estimate for how much the entire refurbishment would cost.
"It would be irresponsible at this point in time to put a number out there, because it's this essential design and scoping and engineering work that is going to get us to the place where we can have a number," Smith said.
OPG said a refurbishment at its Darlington Nuclear Generating Station was costing $12.8 billion and was on time and on budget.
Ken Hartwick, CEO of OPG, said the Darlington refurbishment as well as one at Bruce Power would help guide the Pickering life extension.
"We have learned a lot about what it takes to refurbish a nuclear station the right way with thousands of lessons learned from Darlington and Bruce Power that we will apply to Pickering," Hartwick said.
The four units produce 2,000 megawatts of electricity, enough to power two million homes.
The Independent Electricity System Operator has said Ontario's electricity demand is expected to grow by about two per cent each year, but could be even higher. A promise to build 1.5 million homes by 2031 and several large-scale manufacturing investments such as electric vehicle battery plants are helping to push demand higher.
The province needs more supply particularly starting in the mid-2030s, the IESO has said.
Keith Stewart, a senior energy strategist with Greenpeace Canada, said the price of wind and solar power with battery storage had "dropped like a stone" and should be more central to Ontario's energy policy.
"Any credible independent cost-benefit analysis would find that we should be investing in the renewable-powered energy system of the future," Stewart wrote in a statement.
Pickering produces about 14 per cent of Ontario's electricity, but its current licence to operate the four units in question expires at the end of this year. OPG has asked the Canadian Nuclear Safety Commission to extend that to 2026, but a public hearing for that application has not yet been scheduled.
Green party Leader Mike Schreiner said Greens understood that nuclear power would continue to be part of the energy mix for decades, but Ontario also needed much more wind and solar power and no more natural gas generation.
"Instead of attracting jobs and investment in low-cost renewables, the Ford government is making Ontario's grid dirtier and more expensive by prioritizing dirty fossil gas plants and the costly, poor-performing Pickering plant," Schreiner wrote in a statement.
The IESO announced in December that it was looking to add 2,000 megawatts of non-emitting electricity generation online such as wind, solar, bioenergy and hydro to the system. However, it also said natural gas was still required to ensure supply and stability in the short to medium term.
Ontario's electricity system was 94 per cent emissions free in 2020, but today that figure has fallen to 90 per cent.

Subject: Solar energy; Electricity; Employment; Nuclear power plants; Natural gas

Business indexing term: Subject: Employment Nuclear power plants

Company / organization: Name: Bruce Power; NAICS: 221113

Identifier / keyword: ontario; power; generation; moving; ahead; extend

Title: Ont. plans to refurbish nuclear power plant; Government says work is needed to meet future electrical demands

Publication title: The Ottawa Citizen; Ottawa, Ont.

First page: A.4

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 31, 2024

Dateline: Pickering,Ont.

Section: City

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Ottawa, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Publication subject: General Interest Periodicals--Canada
