Document 286 of 315

Ottawa is overhauling its zoning bylaws. It will change the city forever

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/ottawa-is-overhauling-zoning-bylaws-will-change/docview/2919760244/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ottawa+is+overhauling+its+zoning+bylaws.+It+will+change+the+city+forever&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The City of Ottawa is about to begin the largest overhaul of its zoning bylaws in a quarter-century, one that will profoundly change the way the city builds and grows.
And, if the words "zoning bylaw" makes you zone out, you shouldn't. The impact of the changes will be felt from Kinburn to Cumberland, from Manotick to Mechanicsville. It will set the ground rules for everything from residential density to building heights to how much street parking is needed.
Work begins on the Comprehensive Zoning Bylaw Amendment in March and likely won't be complete until the end of 2025. It's the first major revision to bylaws since amalgamation in 2001 and may be the single biggest task of Mayor Mark Sutcliffe and the current term of council.
"It seems geeky, but this is going to affect everyone," said Kitchissippi Coun. Jeff Leiper, chair of the city's planning and housing committee. "It's the thing I'm probably the most excited about."
The zoning revamp will open up new areas of Ottawa to infill development, which until now has been largely concentrated in and around the downtown core.
"Kitchissippi has been a development hot spot for a couple of decades now, and that's coming everywhere else," Leiper said. "The building boom is going to slow down in my ward as the land values have skyrocketed, but the same dynamic that's affected Westboro and Hintonburg is coming to Carlington and Bay Ward and Overbrook. It's going to come to Orléans."
At its core is the move by Ottawa and cities across Canada to end what's known as "exclusionary zoning" - the traditional post-war suburb with single-family homes and broad green lawns. Those idyllic Leave it to Beaver communities are car-dependent, wasteful of space and expensive to service. It's labelled "exclusionary" because the cost of housing there puts those neighbourhoods out of the reach of low-income families.
In Ottawa, those areas are known as R1 zoning. The federal government has made it clear, and the Ontario government has made it law, that exclusionary zoning must end. Ottawa's official plan, passed in 2022, effectively ended R1 zoning. Premier Doug Ford's controversial Bill 23, the More Homes Built Faster Act, directs all Ontario municipalities to allow three or more residential units per building lot.
Federal housing minister Sean Fraser has also made it known that money from the $40-billion federal housing accelerator fund will only go to municipalities that agree to end exclusionary zoning. (Sutcliffe announced this past week that Ottawa had reached a deal to receive accelerator funds, much of which would be earmarked for affordable housing. The city requested $150 million, but the exact amount it will receive has not been announced).
"The province passed legislation that requires municipalities to allow three units on a lot, and as quickly as we could we drafted a bylaw that allows three units on any lot," Leiper said. "But, as far as I'm concerned, it's still a bit of a creaky bylaw. It was a quick response to a directive."
Ottawa's zoning bylaws still contain rules about things like setbacks from property lines and building heights that are counter to the increased density goals of the official plan. Squeezing three or more units on one lot is difficult if the bylaw says you can only build so high and must be a certain number of metres from property lines.
"The developers are looking at our most recent official plan that really doubles down on intensification and saying, 'These are the kinds of buildings that our official plan tells us we should be able to build. Why doesn't the zoning reflect that?'" Leiper said. "And residents are wondering, 'Why are developers getting changes to zoning when we're relying on our zoning to tell us how are neighbourhood is going to evolve?'
Jeff Leiper is the city councillor for Kitchissippi Ward and chair of the city's planning and housing committee.
"So getting the zoning up to date is critical to provide the kind of orderly development according to the vision council has adopted," Leiper added. "The process of getting a rezoning is complex. It's expensive. It's time-consuming. One of the most important things we can do as a city to address the housing crisis is to be sure that the as-of-right permissions are in place to allow builders to build housing rather than seek regulatory approvals."
Many of those disputes end up before the Ontario Land Tribunal, an expensive and time-consuming process that can further slow home construction and add costs.
Developers, of course, are eager for the coming clarity.
"We're hoping there's going to be a lot more simplicity," said Jason Burggraaf, executive director of the Greater Ottawa Home Builders' Association. "There are hundreds of different zoning labels. We think of R1, R2, R3, R4 and R5, but there are dozens and dozens of little appendices they add on: R1aa and R1ab ... There's a tremendous variations for every type of conceivable change for lot size and location and particularity. It's so gargantuan and hard to navigate."
Some of the zoning bylaws are leftovers from pre-amalgamation days. Burggraaf said one developer was stymied when trying to build an apartment in an area designated as R4, which allows low rises of up to four storeys.
"There was a rule that said, 'R4 apartments, but no senior residences.' Why? Why ban that type of use? It could have been instituted decades ago because someone didn't want a seniors' residence near their house and it just got carried over the years and through amalgamation. It's those weird kind of particularities that we have to clean out."
That Ottawa has a housing crisis is no secret. Council declared a "housing emergency" in 2020. The wait list for a place to live with Ottawa Community Housing is 12,000 names long and the average wait time is more than five years.
When the city began working on its latest official plan, the assumption was that Ottawa's population would grow by about 400,000 by the year 2046. With the surge in immigration in recent years, that number is already outdated. Canada welcomed more than one million newcomers in 2023 alone.
"The numbers coming from Ontario Ministry of Finance just this past summer say the number is going to be closer to 560,000," Burggraaf said. "That's a big difference from 400,000. The concern now is we're already planning to be under-shortaged for housing.
Jason Burggraaf is executive director of the Greater Ottawa Home Builders' Association.
"That sheer number of population growth means we can't afford to cut off any avenue for housing growth. Yes, it's more densification in neighbourhoods. Yes, it's more height along corridors and near transit stations. And, yes, it's new communities adding on to the suburbs. We can't achieve the housing we want to get by only doing one or two of those. We have to do all three."
The federal and provincial governments are trying to spur even denser growth, offering builders GST and HST breaks if they build four units to a lot. That's something homebuilders would like to take advantage of, Burggraaf said, and something Leiper said could be accommodated by a tweak of city bylaws. Allowing basement apartments, for example, turns a duplex into a four-plex.
Building heights are always a source of conflict, whether it's a soaring high-rise that casts a shadow across a neighbourhood or a battleship-like infill duplex that looms over its next-door neighbour.
Originally, the official plan sought to limit building heights to four storeys along minor corridors - roads like Churchill Avenue and Fisher Avenue, for example. Ontario over-rode that, after some hiccups tied to its controversial plan to expand urban boundaries, and raised the height limit to six storeys along minor corridors, a change now embraced by Ottawa council.
Workers brave the winter elements at a construction site in the Gloucester area of Ottawa earlier this past week.
Parking is another perennial zoning headache. Increased density means more people and fewer parking spots. Can those people be enticed into relying on public transit instead of private cars? Cue the OC Transpo complaints.
"Any new project, whether it's inside the Greenbelt wards or even in the suburbs, on-street parking is a big issue," Burggraaf said. "Are we going to change how we think about parking? Will we allow more communal parking lots versus individual driveways?
"What happens with transit has a lot to do with parking, too. The more the city can do about the reliability of transit helps address some of those parking issues."
The comprehensive bylaw amendment process "is a multi-fold issue that goes beyond the construction of homes," Burggraaf said. "It's truly about the growth of the city and how it will operate."
Work on the zoning bylaw amendments began almost as soon as the official plan was complete. Leiper's planning committee expects to have a first draft available for the public to see in March.
"In May, that will become even more precise," Leiper said. "Then we'll spend much of the rest of the 2024 receiving industry feedback and resident feedback and having a really meaty debate over the specifics of the bylaw."
The final bylaw probably won't be voted on by city council until December 2025.

Document 287 of 315

Politicians cross party lines to praise Ed Broadbent at state funeral

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/politicians-cross-party-lines-praise-ed-broadbent/docview/2919502879/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Politicians+cross+party+lines+to+praise+Ed+Broadbent+at+state+funeral&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The power of Ed Broadbent's never-ending pursuit of equality and justice was everywhere across the Canadian political spectrum late Sunday afternoon.
Family, friends and politicians of all stripes, dating back five decades, were united in their praise for Broadbent during his state funeral at the Carleton Dominion-Chalmers Centre in downtown Ottawa.
Broadbent, who died Jan. 11 at the age of 87, is the first party leader who was neither a prime minister nor an official Opposition leader to receive state funeral status.
Invited guests lined up around the block before the ceremony to pay tribute to Broadbent, whose 21-year political career included 14 years as leader of the New Democratic Party.
Former NDP leader Ed Broadbent is seen here in a file photo from 2016.
In keeping with the respect he received across party lines, former Conservative prime minister Joe Clark took in the ceremony while sitting beside current Liberal Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
As NDP leader, Broadbent routinely debated with former prime minister Pierre Trudeau - Justin's father.
Broadbent was a champion for women's and Indigenous rights, and blue-collar workers across the country. His character, and the "Honest Ed" handle that he carried, helped the NDP rise steadily in popularity.
Family, friends, dignitaries and fellow politicians joined together for the state funeral of former NDP leader Ed Broadbent on Sunday, Jan. 28, 2024. The memorial was held at the Carleton Dominion-Chalmers Centre. Broadbent died Jan. 11 at the age of 87.
His family arrived to the service together Sunday afternoon.
At one point in 1987, the NDP surged ahead of both the Conservatives and Liberals in polling. The NDP secured 43 seats in the 1988 election, its highest total until a later wave under Jack Layton's leadership in 2011.
Current NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, who spoke at the funeral, says Broadbent was much more than a mentor. Singh would routinely tell him, "Ed, I want to be like you when I grow up."
Singh says Broadbent's commitment to fighting for the little guy was inspiring.
Family, friends, dignitaries and fellow politicians joined together for the state funeral of former NDP leader Ed Broadbent on Sunday, Jan. 28, 2024. The memorial was held at the Carleton Dominion-Chalmers Centre. Broadbent died Jan. 11 at the age of 87.
His family arrived to the service together Sunday afternoon.
At first, Broadbent resisted the pull of politics, more interested in teaching. He grew up in Oshawa, earned a Doctor of Philosophy degree in political science from the University of Toronto in 1966 and became a professor at York University. In his first federal election representing Oshawa-Whitby in 1968, he defeated former Conservative MP and cabinet minister Michael Starr by a mere 15 votes.
From there, he rose to become NDP leader in 1975, replacing David Lewis.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau arrived and spoke to media before the service Sunday afternoon.
But even after his 14-year run atop the NDP and 19 years representing the riding ended, he returned to the federal scene again in 2004 at Layton's urging.
Broadbent represented Ottawa Centre from 2004-06, retiring from politics again following the death of his wife, Lucille, to breast cancer. The Carleton Dominion-Chalmers Centre is in the Ottawa Centre riding where he served.
While his political career was finished, he continued to push for more rights for all through the Broadbent Institute.
"You see a life of incredible dedication to his vision for a better world," said Singh. "He never forgot who we fight for. He made it very clear. The job of government, the job of elected people was a fight for the vulnerable, not to protect the powerful."
Family, friends, dignitaries and fellow politicians joined together for the state funeral of former NDP leader Ed Broadbent on Sunday, Jan. 28, 2024. The memorial was held at the Carleton Dominion-Chalmers Centre. Broadbent died Jan. 11 at the age of 87.
Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew also says Broadbent mastered the art of making his case "passionately, but never personally," striving not to be divisive.
"At a time of angry populists around the globe, of separate social media ecosystems that are worlds apart, of identity politics that obscure the simple truth that we are all related, Ed stood for the opposite," Kinew said. "He stood for thoughtful leadership. And perhaps most importantly, he stood for the vision that we are one country."
Justin Trudeau also paid tribute.
"Canada is significantly better for his years of service, both in politics and out of it," Trudeau said, speaking to reporters before the ceremony began.
Former Conservative prime minister Brian Mulroney and former Liberal prime minister Jean Chrétien offered their condolences via video at the service.
Elizabeth May, leader of the Green Party of Canada, took part in the special service Sunday.
Chrétien likened their relationship to that of players on rival hockey teams, who fight endlessly during games to win, but can still share a beer afterwards.
While Chrétien often disagreed politically with Broadbent, he praised the longtime NDP leader for being "civilized and never nasty."
That was also a sentiment offered up by Brian Topp, chair of the board of Broadbent Institute, who said Broadbent could "disagree without being disagreeable."
Topp says Broadbent's life goals were simple.
"Everything he learned led him to a set of clear, powerful principles."
kwarren@postmedia.com

Document 288 of 315

Trudeau was faced with incompetence

Author: Robinson, Sinclair

Publication info: Robinson, Sinclair. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-was-faced-with-incompetence/docview/3126974501/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+was+faced+with+incompetence&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=Robinson%2C+Sinclair&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What necessitated the illegal imposition of the Emergencies Act by the federal government to put an end to the convoy protest was incompetence and lack of leadership on the part of three people: then-mayor Jim Watson, then-police chief Peter Sloly, and Premier Doug Ford. Sinclair Robinson, Ottawa

Document 289 of 315

Changing gears; Canada wants to be an EV nation, but without incentives are consumers willing to switch?

Author: Coulton, Marisa

Publication info: Coulton, Marisa. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/changing-gears-canada-wants-be-ev-nation-without/docview/3126973962/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Changing+gears%3B+Canada+wants+to+be+an+EV+nation%2C+but+without+incentives+are+consumers+willing+to+switch%3F&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.9&amp;au=Coulton%2C+Marisa&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The federal government wants electric vehicles to be the obvious choice - and eventually the only choice - for Canadian drivers, but so far that doesn't appear to be the case.
At Raman Grewal's Kia and Subaru dealership in Whitehorse, Yukon, for example, there is considerable demand for EV and plug-in hybrid vehicles, but many of his customers still have "range anxiety." That means they're worried the battery will die before they can find a charging station, and that they'll be left stranded on the Alaska Highway between B.C. and the Yukon.
"Currently, EVs work if you are commuting in around (the) 200 kilometres per day range," Grewal wrote in an email. "(Especially) in our cold climate in the north. Some customers are living at greater distances or are living off grid without hydro."
In urban centres, which currently have lower rates of EV adoption than the surrounding suburbs, there are other issues, said Jimmy Hearn, author of a recent report by S&P Global that found zero-emission vehicles accounted for a record 13.3 per cent of new vehicles purchased in the third quarter of 2023.
Urbanites tend to rely on street parking, where it is harder to find EV charging stations, Hearn said, whereas suburbanites can install charging stations at their homes.
Even in Montreal, which is home to more than 2,000 public charging station ports, such concerns are front of mind, according to Norman John Hébert, president and chief operating officer of Groupe Park Avenue, which operates a Mercedes-Benz dealership in the city that sells EVs.
Wait times can be an issue, too.
And then there's the sticker price.
"Generally, our customers find EV prices to be too high compared with an equivalent format of ICE (internal combustion engine) vehicle," Hébert said in an email.
The average sticker price of an EV is $73,500, according to Canadian Black Book, around $6,000 more than a new gas powered car, which tops $67,000 according to a recent price index report by AutoTrader, an online car marketplace.
Though S&P forecasts EV market share will only continue to grow, reaching 18.4 per cent by the end of 2024, there are signs consumer demand is not keeping up with soaring expectations.
In recent months, some automakers have scaled back EV production targets, citing sluggish sales and faltering demand.
Ford Motor Co. recently announced it would reduce production of its F-150 Lightning electric pickup truck because demand was lower than expected. General Motors Co. announced that it, too, would slow plans for electric truck production, delaying the launch of a Detroit-based factory to 2025.
As Ottawa embarks on its ambitious mandate of having 100 per cent of all new car sales be electric by 2035 - a target that goes hand in hand with tens of billions of dollars in subsidies for EV supply chain projects - getting consumers on board is seen as critical by many in the industry. If they can't be convinced EVs are the best option, reaching those goals will be an uphill climb.
"We have to make sure the consumer is coming along at the pace required to support this transition," said Brian Kingston, president of the Canadian Vehicle Manufacturer's Association.
To ensure there is adequate demand for EVs, the government must enhance the grid, provide consumers with incentives, keep investment flowing into the North American EV supply chain, and build more charging infrastructure, he said.
"Achieving these very aggressive sales targets requires a 'Team Canada'approach to boosting EV sales across Canada," he said.
Without it, governments could be forced to rely more heavily on the costly subsidies that continue to play a major role in convincing consumers to buy EVs.
People who buy EVs from BMW Aurora, a dealership north of Toronto, generally qualify for federal incentives of up to $5,000, said Thomas An, assistant sales manager.
At Grewal's dealership in Whitehorse, meanwhile, customers can take advantage of the federal government rebate of around $5,000 on purchases and leases, plus a provincial rebate of up to $5,000 - a perk that is unavailable in Ontario because that province's $14,000 rebate was eliminated by Doug Ford when he became premier in 2018.
Some of the variance in demand for EVs across dealerships - they account for 20 per cent of car sales for An, but 43.5 per cent for Grewal - is likely due to the differences in subsidies, said S&P's Hearn, noting that Quebec and B.C., which lead the pack in terms of EV sales, also both have strong EV incentives.
In the third quarter of 2023, EV sales made up around 26.4 per cent of all new vehicle registrations in B.C., where consumers can get up to a $4,000 rebate on a new EV, and 22.8 per cent of sales in Quebec, where consumers can get an even higher rebate of $7,000.
"If you compare Toronto's ZEV (zero-emission vehicle) growth to Montreal and Vancouver, Toronto ... is trailing behind those two smaller cities," said Hearn.
There are around 8,800 EVs in Toronto, compared to 10,100 in Vancouver and 12,100 in Montreal, likely because Ontario lacks a provincial EV incentive, notes the S&P report.
"From what I see in the data, the vehicles that people are registering in British Columbia and Quebec are the vehicles that meet the provincial ZEV initiatives and rebates as well as the federal rebates," Hearn said.
Customers are stacking the incentives in order to get the most bang for their buck.
While Bibra said recent news coverage suggests EV uptake at some dealerships has slowed, the big picture data says otherwise.
"When we look at just the hard facts, the sales continue to increase," she said. "It's really important to look at what's happening as an overarching trend."
Indeed, new ZEV sales fluctuate year to year, but are increasing on the whole: they totalled 55,600 countrywide, up from just 5,200 in the same quarter in 2017.
At the national level, Canada has made good progress in securing investments in the EV supply chain, part of its bid to become an EV superpower, said Kingston.
The signature investments so far include Volkswagen AG's plans to build a battery plant in St. Thomas, Ont., supported by $13 billion in government funding and Stellantis NV-LG Energy Solution Ltd.'s battery plant in Windsor, Ont., supported by $15 billion in funding.
Others include Ford's plan to retool its factory in Oakville to assemble EVs and battery packs, as well as a $35-million investment by Bobaek America Inc., a Korean electric vehicle parts manufacturer, toward the construction of an EV factory in Windsor.
But Kingston and a number of dealerships pointed to the need for better infrastructure as a key stumbling block to widespread adoption.
"The government should definitely implement more EV charging infrastructures in the cities," said An.
Grewal, too, called for better charging infrastructure on the Alaska Highway.
Though most customers appreciate the savings on gas, which amount to around $3,000 a year, EVs are still only the favoured option for a particular subset of consumers: those commuting short distances, those who are willing to wait several months to over a year for their car, and those who are "innovators that are interested in new technology," said Hébert.
Many of his customers seem willing to pay for EVs that have found a "sweet spot" for pricing, such as the Audi Q4 e-Tron, which retails for $64,000, the BMW i4 which retails for between $55,000 to $80,000, and the Volvo XC40 Recharge, which starts at around $60,000.
But it's not enough to cater to Canadians with means, said Bibra; the federal government needs to work harder to reach "mainstream" Canadians who fall into the low-tomiddle income brackets. Simply mandating that 100 per cent of car sales be electric by 2035 does not ensure the goal will be met, said Kingston.
"I'd like to see a more co-ordinated approach led by the federal government but with other levels of government participating - provinces and municipalities - on a very well-thought-through plan to achieve the targets that the government has established," Kingston said. "We can't do this on our own." Financial Post mcoulton@postmedia.com

Document 290 of 315

It will change the city forever; Ottawa overhauling zoning bylaws; Revamp opens areas to infill development that has mostly been limited to downtown

Author: Crawford, Blair

Publication info: Crawford, Blair. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-change-city-forever-ottawa-overhauling/docview/3126973957/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It+will+change+the+city+forever%3B+Ottawa+overhauling+zoning+bylaws%3B+Revamp+opens+areas+to+infill+development+that+has+mostly+been+limited+to+downtown&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Crawford%2C+Blair&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The City of Ottawa is about to begin the largest overhaul of its zoning bylaws in a quarter-century, one that will profoundly change the way the city builds and grows.
And if the words "zoning bylaw" make you zone out, you shouldn't. The impact of the changes will be felt from Kinburn to Cumberland, from Manotick to Mechanicsville. It will set the ground rules for everything from residential density to building heights to how much street parking is needed.
Work begins on the Comprehensive Zoning Bylaw Amendment in March and likely won't be complete until the end of 2025. It's the first major revision to bylaws since amalgamation in 2001 and may be the single biggest task of Mayor Mark Sutcliffe and the current term of council.
"It seems geeky, but this is going to affect everyone," said Kitchissippi Coun. Jeff Leiper, chair of the city's planning and housing committee.
"It's the thing I'm probably the most excited about."
The zoning revamp will open up new areas of Ottawa to infill development, which until now has been largely concentrated in and around the downtown core.
"Kitchissippi has been a development hot spot for a couple of decades now, and that's coming everywhere else," Leiper said. "The building boom is going to slow down in my ward as the land values have skyrocketed, but the same dynamic that's affected Westboro and Hintoburg is coming to Carlington and Bay Ward and Overbrook. It's going to come to Orléans."
At its core is the move by Ottawa and cities across Canada to end what's known as "exclusionary zoning" - the traditional post-war suburb with single-family homes and broad green lawns. Those idyllic Leave it to Beaver communities are car-dependent, wasteful of space and expensive to service. It's labelled "exclusionary" because the cost of housing there puts those neighbourhoods out of the reach of low-income families.
In Ottawa, those areas are known as R1 zoning. The federal government has made it clear, and the Ontario government has made it law, that exclusionary zoning must end. Ottawa's Official Plan, passed in 2022, effectively ended R1 zoning. Premier Doug Ford's controversial Bill 23, the More Homes Built Faster Act, directs all Ontario municipalities to allow three or more residential units per building lot.
Federal Housing Minister Sean Fraser has also made it known that money from the $40-billion federal Housing Accelerator Fund will only go to municipalities that agree to end exclusionary zoning. (Sutcliffe announced this past week that Ottawa had reached a deal to receive accelerator funds, much of which would be earmarked for affordable housing.
The city requested $150 million, but the exact amount it will receive has not been announced.) "The province passed legislation that requires municipalities to allow three units on a lot, and as quickly as we could we drafted a bylaw that allows three units on any lot," Leiper said. "But, as far as I'm concerned, it's still a bit of a creaky bylaw. It was a quick response to a directive."
Ottawa's zoning bylaws still contain rules about such things as setbacks from property lines and building heights that are counter to the increased density goals of the Official Plan. Squeezing three or more units on one lot is difficult if the bylaw says you can only build so high and must be a certain number of metres from property lines.
"The developers are looking at our most recent Official Plan that really doubles down on intensification and saying: 'These are the kinds of buildings that our Official Plan tells us we should be able to build. Why doesn't the zoning reflect that?'" Leiper said. "And residents are wondering, 'Why are developers getting changes to zoning when we're relying on our zoning to tell us how are neighbourhood is going to evolve?'"So getting the zoning up to date is critical to provide the kind of orderly development according to the vision council has adopted," Leiper added. "The process of getting a rezoning is complex. It's expensive. It's time-consuming. One of the most important things we can do as a city to address the housing crisis is to be sure that the as-of-right permissions are in place to allow builders to build housing rather than seek regulatory approvals."
Many of those disputes end up before the Ontario Land Tribunal, an expensive and time-consuming process that can further slow home construction and add costs.
Developers, of course, are eager for the coming clarity.
"We're hoping there's going to be a lot more simplicity," said Jason Burggraaf, executive director of the Greater Ottawa Home Builders' Association.
"There are hundreds of different zoning labels. We think of R1, R2, R3, R4 and R5, but there are dozens and dozens of little appendices they add on: R1aa and R1ab....
There's a tremendous variation for every type of conceivable change for lot size and location and particularity.
It's so gargantuan and hard to navigate."
Some of the zoning bylaws are leftovers from pre-amalgamation days. Burggraaf said one developer was stymied when trying to build an apartment in an area designated as R4, which allows low rises of up to four storeys.
"There was a rule that said, 'R4 apartments, but no senior residences.'Why? Why ban that type of use? It could have been instituted decades ago because someone didn't want a seniors'residence near their house and it just got carried over the years and through amalgamation. It's those weird kind of particularities that we have to clean out."
That Ottawa has a housing crisis is no secret. Council declared a "housing emergency" in 2020.
The wait-list for a place to live with Ottawa Community Housing is 12,000 names long and the average wait time is more than five years.
When the city began working on its latest Official Plan, the assumption was Ottawa's population would grow by about 400,000 by the year 2046. With the surge in immigration in recent years, that number is already outdated. Canada welcomed more than one million newcomers in 2023 alone.
"The numbers coming from Ontario Ministry of Finance just this past summer say the number is going to be closer to 560,000," Burggraaf said. "That's a big difference from 400,000. The concern now is we're already planning to be under-shortaged for housing.
"That sheer number of population growth means we can't afford to cut off any avenue for housing growth. Yes, it's more densification in neighbourhoods. Yes, it's more height along corridors and near transit stations. And, yes, it's new communities adding on to the suburbs.
We can't achieve the housing we want to get by only doing one or two of those. We have to do all three."
The federal and provincial governments are trying to spur even denser growth, offering builders GST and HST breaks if they build four units to a lot. That's something homebuilders would like to take advantage of, Burggraaf said, and something Leiper said could be accommodated by a tweak of city bylaws. Allowing basement apartments, for example, turns a duplex into a four-plex.
Building heights are always a source of conflict, whether it's a soaring highrise that casts a shadow across a neighbourhood or a battleship-like infill duplex that looms over its next-door neighbour.
Originally, the Official Plan sought to limit building heights to four storeys along minor corridors - roads such as Churchill Avenue and Fisher Avenue, for example. Ontario overrode that, after some hiccups tied to its controversial plan to expand urban boundaries, and raised the height limit to six storeys along minor corridors, a change now embraced by Ottawa council.
Parking is another perennial zoning headache. Increased density means more people and fewer parking spots. Can those people be enticed into relying on public transit instead of private cars? Cue the OC Transpo complaints.
"Any new project, whether it's inside the Greenbelt wards or even in the suburbs, on-street parking is a big issue," Burggraaf said. "Are we going to change how we think about parking? Will we allow more communal parking lots versus individual driveways? "What happens with transit has a lot to do with parking, too. The more the city can do about the reliability of transit helps address some of those parking issues."
The comprehensive bylaw amendment process "is a multifold issue that goes beyond the construction of homes," Burggraaf said. "It's truly about the growth of the city and how it will operate."
Work on the zoning bylaw amendments began almost as soon as the Official Plan was complete. Leiper's planning committee expects to have a first draft available for the public to see in March.
"In May, that will become even more precise," Leiper said. "Then we'll spend much of the rest of the 2024 receiving industry feedback and resident feedback and having a really meaty debate over the specifics of the bylaw."
The final bylaw probably won't be voted on by city council until December 2025.

Subject: Bylaws; Councils; Planning; Housing developments; Zoning

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing developments

Location: Canada

Document 291 of 315

Today's letters: The federal court got it wrong on the trucker convoy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-federal-court-got-wrong-on-trucker/docview/2918972167/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+The+federal+court+got+it+wrong+on+the+trucker+convoy&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Government acted correctly to end convoy protest
Re: Liberal government's invoking of Emergencies Act during convoy protest unreasonable, unjustified: ruling, Jan. 23.
Well, the federal court got that completely wrong. The judge obviously hadn't lived through the terrible times and suffering of thousands of people in downtown Ottawa and across the region.
If the truckers had held a protest for the weekend as promised, all would have been good. Instead, they occupied Wellington and Sparks Streets and disrupted businesses, lives and health and wellbeing. They turned the whole downtown into a hell of noise, irritation and disruption. All for various causes with little intelligent reason.
Disabled people were denied access to Para Transpo, medicine delivery, food delivery etc. The utter selfishness of the protesters ruined any sympathy the rest of Canada had for them.
The federal government acted appropriately. Once the Emergencies Act was used, the protest was ended very quickly. Thank God.
Ray Farmilo, Ottawa
Emergencies Act needs a rewrite
Where do constitutionally embedded rights to protest end and "peace, order and good governance" begin? I assume there are limits to both. And if the courts have to decide that one takes precedence over the other, then the fault seems to be with the legislation that our country is bound to uphold.
There are restrictions that Canadians accept and abide by, ranging from not driving on the wrong side of the road to facing a judge to determine if an accused is a danger to society.
The federal government made use of the Emergencies Act as a last resort to return order to the streets of Ottawa where the normal policing authorities of the city, province and federal governments could not. If the act is not clear on when that authority can be used, or is restricted to the point where nothing beyond an alien invasion can trigger it, then it has to be rewritten.
The demand on the government to eliminate all pandemic measures was unreasonable from a general health and safety perspective. As a democracy, Canada has provisions to uphold the cherished freedom to protest but it also has consequences for all those who abuse this privilege.
Peter Haley, Ottawa
Trudeau had to act to end occupation
Ethics go both ways. If Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's use of the Emergencies Act is considered a violation, then what would you call a whole truck-city occupation of our downtown? As far as I can see, all other levels of government threw Trudeau under the bus.
The Emergencies Act was his only solution at the time. There were no pied pipers leading the occupiers out of the city.
Shirley Sears-MacKay, Ottawa
Trudeau was faced with incompetence
What necessitated the illegal imposition of the Emergencies Act by the federal government to put an end to the convoy protest was incompetence and lack of leadership on the part of three people: then-mayor Jim Watson, then-police chief Peter Sloly, and Premier Doug Ford.
Sinclair Robinson, Ottawa
Right to protest, but other rights too
There is a strong case being made for the right to protest by Canadians. But there are other rights to consider:
. Ottawa taxpayers' right to have access to all their roads, businesses, condominiums etc.
. The right to go to work for people employed on Parliament Hill, at the Château Laurier, Rideau Centre or even Tim Hortons.
. The right of businesses to welcome customers and recoup their capital investment made on promises from the city for uninterrupted basic services.
. The right of tenants and condominium owners around downtown to have noise-free, hassle-free access to nearby roads and services and their own property.
. The right of all Ottawa taxpayers to have access to emergency services such as paramedics, fire and ambulance.
Even tourists have a right to access their hotels and the major Ottawa sites.
Peter B. Aikat, Nepean
Will Liberals pay a price for their error?
Everyone who actually went downtown to see what the protest was all about said they felt totally safe. I went, not to antagonize, but to see if it was as bad as the news media made it out to be.
It was not. People stopped and talked to you, said good morning as you walked by. It was friendlier than if you had walked down there today. The aggravating factor was the horns. That was finally stopped, though.
The courts have ruled that the Liberals broke the law, acted unconstitutionally. Are those Liberals going to have their bank accounts seized? Are they going to spend months in jail with no bail? Are they at least going to apologize to all Canadians, even though we've seen this hollow act before?
D.J. Phillips, Gloucester
Can't we bridge this bureaucracy?
Re: Winter closing of bridge leaves skiers frustrated, Jan. 24.
What's with the Chief William Commanda Bridge? It all could have been so simple.
The newly rebuilt, repurposed and renamed $23.9-million bridge between Ottawa and Gatineau was opened in the summer, only to be closed some three months later. It seems it wasn't intended for winter use because of possible damage to its wooden planked roadbed and ironwork from road salt.
Muddying the waters, as it were, is the fact that the wooden planked roadbed and supporting ironwork of the nearby Alexandra Bridge has been open to pedestrians and cyclists year-round for decades.
Now Marc Lafortune, a volunteer ski patroller and retired teacher, and friends are keen to groom the Commanda Bridge as a link between cross-country ski trails on both sides of the river. A fine idea, and easily workable with perhaps just a bit of snow fencing! The NCC, the City of Gatineau, and Hydro-Québec are said to be "quite enthusiastic" too.
As for Ottawa? Well, the city is reported to be "monitoring the bridge weekly" but telling us to stay off until they can "gain a better understanding of snow accumulation on the bridge during the winter as well as the potential to maintain it for pedestrian and cycling use without damaging the structure."
Do I detect just a hint of bureaucracy in all this? Open the Commanda Bridge to the public now!
John J. Gahan, Ottawa
Don't mess up Kettle Island area
Re: Letter, Build a new bridge at Kettle Island before replacing the Alexandra, Jan. 22.
No one with any awareness at all of the environmental sensitivity and species' diversity of the the Kettle/Upper Duck/Lower Duck Island chain would recommend subjecting the trio to the poison of construction and maintenance of a bridge.
Next summer, take a canoe or kayak through the area to see what I mean.
Thomas Brawn, Orléans
Boyle tells it exactly like it is
Re: What it means to be homeless - and without hope, Jan. 20.
The article by Peter Boyle last Saturday is one of the most brilliant pieces of writing I've seen in the Ottawa Citizen.
He does not ask for sympathy or pity, in fact he doesn't even ask for help. He just tells it like it is. It may be an attempt to engender understanding for a complex and mysterious condition of life that is completely unfathomable to most of us.
This man has a glorious talent.
Gill Magnan, Ottawa
The city can build and own homes
Lately, the news is filled with reports on the lack of affordable housing and the poor, or non-existent response by various cities. I find this strange, as I was born in a city-owned home. The bulk of homes in the Netherlands are owned by the town in which they are located, and this has been the case for my birth home for at least 100 years.
This is also the case with the bulk of homes in Britain, referred to as "council houses." These units are properly built, maintained and have earned their initial cost back to the city numerous times over since they were constructed.
If villages, towns, cities - in those two countries, and I don't know how many more worldwide - are managing to build, own, and rent housing to their population, it must mean it is not just possible, but economically feasible.
The City of Ottawa owns land, the City of Ottawa can qualify for mortgage loans, the City of Ottawa is reported to have many people requiring homes. So, get into providing homes for your citizens. And don't forget: the more of these you build, the more equity you have to build more.
Peter Vanderburg, Ottawa
Teach them about diversity too
Re: Ontario preparing 'back to basics' curriculum for kindergarten, Jan. 23.
As a retired elementary school teacher, who was respectful of the groundwork laid by fellow kindergarten teachers, I am pleased that the education ministry will increase teaching of the "basics" of the kindergarten curriculum (reading, writing and arithmetic).
I wish to also emphasize a key foundation of our young: to express and respect Canada's rich diversity and thereby affirm our underlying identity. The great increase in immigration among young families from diverse countries is a clear call for an early, enriched, intercultural and inter-racial milieu at the beginning, kindergarten-education level.
Cindy Bailey, Ottawa
OCDSB shouldn't neglect English
Re: High quality English-language teaching shouldn't be an afterthought in Ottawa, Jan. 23.
I have waited a long time to hear someone address the English-language program within the Ottawa-Carleton District School Board. Randall Denley said it perfectly: "If the board wants equity, it should offer every student the choice of a good English program to maximize their abilities."
My grandchildren attend their local school, which caters to the mostly French-immersion parent (notice the emphasis on parent). If your child can't cope with the early FI program, there is one option only: a 1,2,3 split-grade class within the local school.
The other option: send your child to the Ottawa Catholic School Board, where you have to contend with the emphasis on a Christian slant in education but where French immersion is introduced at Grade 4. A lot of the parents in the local school would embrace the Grade 4 option but prefer to have their children educated in a more secular environment.
I agree with Denley: Go to a system where every child has a chance. Establish their English first, then introduce the second language. Everyone wins here; even the French-immersion parent.
Sandra (McCarthy) McGrath, teacher of 40 years, OBE/OCDSB, Ottawa
See dementia as a social problem
Re: We need better ways to care for those with dementia, Jan. 22.
Indeed a revolution is needed, and the understanding of dementia requires rethinking; it begs its conceptualization as a "social" phenomenon as opposed to a medical condition.
We do not have the medical resources to provide all the services needed by persons who suffer the social consequences of cognitive impairment, memory loss, disorientation, changes in judgment etc. In addition, these changes in functioning impair the usual working of family roles. A home care program that offers basic, limited personal hygiene attention cannot assure comprehensive service to persons living with dementia at home.
Many family caregivers are hanging on by their fingertips to get through each day physically and psychologically. We do the best we can with the institutions we have. Time for a revolution and reconsideration of dementia care as a social problem.
Celia M. Carter, Brockville
Advance Directives must be allowed
Re: The MAiD lobbyists pushing to extremes, Jan. 24.
This is one of many articles we have seen on the understandably contentious issue of extending medically assisted death to people with mental health problems.
However, it is not the only issue on the agenda of Bill C-7. Also to be discussed and voted on is the subject of Advanced Directives. This seems to have been forgotten.
Because express consent must be given for MAiD, those experiencing dementia are not considered able to give consent. An Ipsos Reid poll shows that 82 per cent of Canadians are in favour of Advanced Directives. That is, people want to be able to express their wishes for what treatment they would want, or not want, should they become mentally incompetent.
Although I am still competent to make such a decision, at 91 I am aware that my cognitive capacity is declining. I have a horror of ending up mouldering in a long-term care home unless the Advanced Directive for MAiID is passed.
Jacqueline MacDonald, Ottawa
Demand more from our elected leaders
Paraphrasing Washington Post columnist Ramesh Ponnuru: A Constitution's checks and balances don't operate automatically, like a machine. Democracies pre-suppose a higher degree of virtue than other forms of government.
Political leaders have to exercise self-restraint instead of looking for every loophole; elected bodies have to pass laws for which they are willing to be held accountable; and voters have to insist on both.
These days, the idea that those we elect should be men and women of high character is considered naïve or outdated. If, at the same time, our democracies are working poorly, perhaps it is not an accident.
John Morris, Ottawa

Today's letters: Federal plan to cap student visas is harmful on many levels

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-federal-plan-cap-student-visas-is/docview/2918723370/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+Federal+plan+to+cap+student+visas+is+harmful+on+many+levels&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Student visa plan indescribably stupid
Re: Ottawa announces two-year cap on international student admissions, Jan. 22.
Some time back, the Doug Ford government looked around at the college system and became quite concerned. Colleges in Toronto were thriving, even adding campuses. Colleges in outlying areas were struggling. A huge part of the difference was that a lot of international students were coming to Canada, and Toronto was an appealing locale.
There were four obvious options. First, government officials could do nothing and watch the outlying colleges shrivel. Second, they could funnel many millions of additional taxpayers' money into them on an ongoing basis. Third, for millions of dollars, help them build a Toronto satellite campus. And fourth, they could find credible private colleges that would partner with them, and that would bear the brunt of cost and agree to be evaluated by the same criteria as the public colleges.
So off they went, bringing education to many, and revenue to both kinds of colleges. Not only that, of course, but also bringing millions of dollars into the local economy. And as part of the agreement, being evaluated by the same academic and other criteria.
Fine.
Along comes the federal government. The Liberals are sinking like a stone in the polls and are portrayed as having done nothing about several important matters. So they do something indescribably stupid: they take action that distorts the reality of where international students go; will kill the best of the private colleges; critically wound the outlying public colleges; and toss away many millions of dollars from the local economy, while closing colleges and throwing many out of work.
Spare me the "puppy mills" nonsense. They are the problem of the Ford government. And spare me the prejudice against private education. Twenty-four of the 28 American universities on the global best 100 universities list are private universities. Time to grow up.
Ken MacQueen, Nepean, former President of Huntington University and Principal of Vancouver School of Theology
Here's why parents choose immersion
Re: High quality English-language teaching shouldn't be an afterthought in Ottawa, Jan. 23.
In his column on education, Randall Denley does not mention one very important fact: most attractive jobs in the Ottawa-Hull area call for bilingualism.
So even though 60 per cent may speak English at home, Ottawa parents send their children to French immersion for many different reasons - and one of them is to prepare them as much as possible for the job market.
I do agree that English language schooling should be of the very best quality.
Patricia Shapiro, Greely
Farm Boy, copy Loblaw's policy
I am one of those who welcome the reduced prices on best-before food items. Now that Loblaw has returned to the 50 per cent discount, it would be very nice if Farm Boy would follow that example and change its existing 30 per cent discount to 50 per cent as well.
Aviva King, Ottawa

Today's letters: At least young people realize how important trees are
Today's letters: Student visa crackdown targets the wrong people

Subject: Local economy; Foreign students; Private colleges & universities; Private schools; Education

Business indexing term: Subject: Local economy

Identifier / keyword: student; indescribably; stupid; ottawa; announces; international

Title: Today's letters: Federal plan to cap student visas is harmful on many levels

Publication title: The Ottawa Citizen (Online); Ottawa

Document 293 of 315

Student visa plan indescribably dumb; Letters to the editor

Author: Macqueen, Ken

Publication info: Macqueen, Ken. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/student-visa-plan-indescribably-dumb-letters/docview/2918653328/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Student+visa+plan+indescribably+dumb%3B+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Macqueen%2C+Ken&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Re: Ottawa announces twoyear cap on international student admissions, Jan. 22. Some time back, the Doug Ford government looked around at the college system and became quite concerned. Colleges in Toronto were thriving, even adding campuses. Colleges in outlying areas were struggling. A huge part of the difference was that a lot of international students were coming to Canada, and Toronto was an appealing locale.
There were four obvious options. First, government officials could do nothing and watch the outlying colleges shrivel. Second, they could funnel many millions of additional taxpayers'money into them on an ongoing basis. Third, for millions of dollars, help them build a Toronto satellite campus. And fourth, they could find credible private colleges that would partner with them, and that would bear the brunt of cost and agree to be evaluated by the same criteria as the public colleges.
So off they went, bringing education to many, and revenue to both kinds of colleges. Not only that, of course, but also bringing millions of dollars into the local economy. And as part of the agreement, being evaluated by the same academic and other criteria. Fine. Along comes the federal government. The Liberals are sinking like a stone in the polls and are portrayed as having done nothing about several important matters. So they do something indescribably stupid: they take action that distorts the reality of where international students go; will kill the best of the private colleges; critically wound the outlying public colleges; and toss away many millions of dollars from the local economy, while closing colleges and throwing many out of work.
Spare me the "puppy mills" nonsense. They are the problem of the Ford government. And spare me the prejudice against private education. Twenty-four of the 28 American universities on the global best 100 universities list are private universities. Time to grow up. Ken MacQueen, Nepean, former president of Huntington University and Principal of Vancouver School of Theology

Subject: Local economy; Foreign students; Private colleges & universities; Private schools

Business indexing term: Subject: Local economy

Identifier / keyword: ottawa; announces; twoyear; international; student; admissions

Title: Student visa plan indescribably dumb; Letters to the editor

Document 294 of 315

Students join colleges in decrying cuts to international student visas

Author: Warren, Ken

Publication info: Warren, Ken. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/students-join-colleges-decrying-cuts/docview/2918653178/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Students+join+colleges+in+decrying+cuts+to+international+student+visas&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Warren%2C+Ken&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When Mohamed Faris was exploring global options to pursue a mechanical engineering degree five years ago, he embraced the opportunity that came with coming to Carleton University.
"Canada was a safe haven, giving me an ability to be myself within a Canadian environment," said Faris, a native of India who grew up in Dubai. "Carleton was a beautiful campus in the capital city. It was a good program. It checked all the boxes."
While spending more than $50,000 annually in tuition and living expenses, Faris has since become fully immersed in the Ottawa university experience.
He lived in residence in his first year. When COVID-19 lockdowns were in place, he studied from Dubai via Zoom. During the past few years, he has also become deeply involved in campus political life, climbing the ranks to become the first South Asian president of the Carleton University Student Association in 2023-24. He says he would "absolutely" be interested in working in Canada once his degree is complete.
All of that gives him a unique perspective on the federal government's decision this week to cut 35 per cent of the visas given to out-of-country post-secondary students - down to 364,000 - for the next two years.
There are currently more than 807,000 international students in Canada, according to the Canadian Bureau for International Education, but an influx of immigration, coupled with a shortage of affordable housing, has created a crisis.
There are no hard numbers yet on where, exactly, the cuts will come, but, due to the number of visas typically issued to Ontario schools, they could receive cuts of as much as 50 per cent. CHANGING CAMPUS DIVERSITY Immigration Minister Marc Miller says the reduction is necessary "in order to maintain a sustainable level of temporary residence in Canada."
Faris understands the problem, acknowledging the housing situation has changed significantly since he first arrived in 2019.
But he says a cap on incoming international students is not the best way to deal with the issue and could drastically shake up the post-secondary environment.
Universities Canada says 18 per cent of full-time students in Ontario are international, including 15 per cent of undergraduates.
The University of Ottawa says 22 per cent of its enrolment comes from non-Canadian students, and that number is 34 per cent at Algonquin College. Carleton University did not respond to a request for the percentage of international students at its school.
While Faris is not in danger of losing his visa - the new mandate would be limited to new students and doesn't apply to master's degree or doctoral students - he says the opportunity he received may not be available to someone else.
"At first glance, it seems very punitive to students," said Faris, who advocated against the pending change last fall as part of the Canadian Alliance of Student Associations. "International students bring a lot of different thoughts, diversity and knowledge to a campus. I've talked to a lot of students. I have seen how this unfolded. I actually predicted this might happen, but I thought it might come in five years, not in five months."
THE BIG BUSINESS OF F OREIGN STUDENTS The cut in student visas isn't going over well at the top levels of college and university administrations, either.
"International students enrich our university community and the communities across Canada they settle in after graduation," Jacques Beauvais, uOttawa's interim provost and vice-president of academic affairs, said in a statement to Postmedia. uOttawa also says foreign students are not contributing to the housing crisis here. There was room for all students requiring residence space for 2023-24.
Algonquin College president and chief executive Claude Brulé said he wasn't surprised by the announcement, given all the previous speculation.
"In general, my reaction is that this is going to have an impact," Brulé said. "There are limited levers to address (the housing shortage), but it should be about how (students) are contributing to the labour market and the local economy. We have more than 200 programs of study and some of the programs are very attractive to international students."
The cuts will have a major effect on revenue streams.
In 2019, Ontario Premier Doug Ford abandoned the previous Liberal government's free tuition program for low-and middle-income students. He replaced it with a 10-per-cent across the board cut in tuition and has frozen fees since then.
Meanwhile, the costs for those students from outside Canada have risen dramatically to make up the difference, and the gap is striking.
In 2023-24, annual tuition for an undergrad at Carleton ranged from $6,060 to $10,700 for Canadian residents and from $29,800 to $46,000 for international students.
The numbers are similar at University of Ottawa, with Canadians paying $6,100 to $6,700 and international students paying $43,300.
In 2022-23, non-Canadian students at Algonquin paid a premium ranging from $13,300 to $14,340.
The average tuition for non-Canadian undergrads in Ontario is $46,400, the highest of all provinces.
A huge gap also exists in postgraduate tuition costs.
At Carleton, Canadian students pay between $4,900 and $7,200 and non-Canadians pay between $13,000 and $17,700. Again, uOttawa numbers are comparable: $6,300 to $8,600 for Canadians and $17,500 to $31,500 for international students.
Graduate students paid an additional $11,100 premium at Algonquin in 2022-23.
Add it all up and foreign students are, in effect, subsidizing Canadian students.
According to a 2022 report by then Ontario attorney general Bonnie Lysyk, international students made up only 14 per cent of the province's university population, but accounted for 45 per cent of tuition revenue.
In that same report, Lysyk found 68 per cent of all college tuition fees in the province came from non-Canadian students. SHUT TING D OWN THE 'FAKE' SCHOOLS Critics of the federal government's decision to cut visas say the issue isn't so much with legitimate, long-standing post-secondary institutions, but more about shady new private educational facilities that prey on desperate international students.
"We think a more refined, targeted approach would be better policy and not impact the good performers that rely on that funding or revenue from international schools to operate," Steve Orsini, president and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities, told The Canadian Press.
Orsini said the federal government should be more concerned with institutions that "have exponential growth in students that don't provide supports, the wraparound services or housing."
The government has, in the past, made a point of attacking those schools for exploitation.
"There are, in provinces, the diploma equivalent of puppy mills that are just churning out diplomas, and this is not a legitimate student experience," Miller said at a December media conference. "There is fraud and abuse and it needs to end."
International students who graduate in Canada can apply for work visas, which has given rise to an increase in unscrupulous schools and employers who take advantage of the situation.
In December, in an effort to curtail those situations, the federal government mandated that those applying for new student visas would need to prove they had access to at least $20,635 in funds beyond tuition. That would be more than double the previous requirement of $10,000 security to prove they could afford increased accommodation costs in Canada.
The federal government also says provincial governments have legislation in place to shut down institutions that operate against the spirit of the international student visa program.
"From my perspective, I don't take it personally," said Brulé of Algonquin College. "As a public institution, we deliver high-calibre education in our programming for students. We don't feel like we fall in that category. We stand by excellence."
Brulé and officials at uOttawa and Carleton don't know exactly how much the new government policy will affect their schools because they don't know yet how Ontario will choose to allocate the student visas provided by the federal government.
Faris, the Carleton mechanical engineering student, is fully engaged in the discussions.
"That increase in the required funds is going to limit the number of people who apply," he said. "It's not equitable for all (international) students. The criteria is based on a proof of having money and not based on academics." kwarren@postmedia.com X: Citizenkwarren

Subject: Foreign students; Passports & visas; Schools; College campuses; Tuition; Federal government; College presidents; Colleges & universities; Immigration; Mechanical engineering

Business indexing term: Subject: Colleges & universities

Location: Canada; Dubai United Arab Emirates; United Arab Emirates

Company / organization: Name: University of Ottawa-Canada; NAICS: 611310; Name: Carleton University; NAICS: 611310; Name: Algonquin College; NAICS: 611310

Identifier / keyword: mohamed; faris; exploring; global; options; pursue

Title: Students join colleges in decrying cuts to international student visas

Publication title: The Ottawa Citizen; Ottawa, Ont.

First page: A.1

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 26, 2024

Section: City

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Ottawa, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

ProQuest document ID: 2918653178

Document 296 of 315

What is the cut to international student visas all about?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/what-is-cut-international-student-visas-all-about/docview/2918470891/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What+is+the+cut+to+international+student+visas+all+about%3F&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Graduate students paid an additional $11,100 premium at Algonquin in 2022-23.
Add it all up and foreign students are, in effect, subsidizing Canadian students.
According to a 2022 report by Ontario Attorney General Bonnie Lysyk, international students made up only 14 per cent of the province's university population, but accounted for 45 per cent of tuition revenues.
In that same report, Lysyk found that 68 per cent of all college tuition fees in the province came from non-Canadian students.
SHUTTING DOWN THE 'FAKE' SCHOOLS
Critics of the federal government's decision to cut visas say the issue isn't so much with legitimate, long-standing post-secondary institutions, but more about shady new private educational facilities that prey on desperate international students.
"We think a more refined, targeted approach would be better policy and not impact the good performers that rely on that funding or revenue from international schools to operate," Steve Orsini, president and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities told The Canadian Press.
Orsini said the federal government should be more concerned with institutions that "have exponential growth in students that don't provide supports, the wraparound services or housing."
The government has, in the past, made a point of attacking those schools for exploitation.
"There are, in provinces, the diploma equivalent of puppy mills that are just churning out diplomas, and this is not a legitimate student experience," Miller said at a December media conference. "There is fraud and abuse and it needs to end."
International students who graduate in Canada can apply for work visas, which has given rise to an increase in unscrupulous schools and employers who take advantage of the situation.
In December, in an effort to curtail those situations, the federal government mandated that those applying for new student visas would need to prove they had access to at least $20,635 in funds beyond tuition. That would be more than double the previous requirement of $10,000 security to prove they could afford increased accommodation costs in Canada.
The federal government also says provincial governments have legislation in place to shut down institutions that operate against the spirit of the international student visa program.
"From my perspective, I don't take it personally," said Brulé of Algonquin College. "As a public institution, we deliver high-calibre education in our programming for students. We don't feel like we fall in that category. We stand by excellence."
Brulé and officials at uOttawa and Carleton don't know exactly how much the new government policy will impact their schools because they don't know yet how Ontario will choose to allocate the student visas provided by the federal government.
Faris, the Carleton mechanical engineering student, is fully engaged in the discussions.
"That increase in the required funds is going to limit the number of people who apply," he said. "It's not equitable for all (international) students. The criteria is based on a proof of having money and not based on academics."
kwarren@postmedia.com
X: Citizenkwarren

Universities, colleges worried after federal Liberals announce international student cap
Glavin: Canada's immigration strategy is in utter chaos. It can't go on

Document 297 of 315

Cornwall mayor refutes rumours on Great Wolf waterpark project

Author: Hambleton, Todd

Publication info: Hambleton, Todd. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cornwall-mayor-refutes-rumours-on-great-wolf/docview/2918399915/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Cornwall+mayor+refutes+rumours+on+Great+Wolf+waterpark+project&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Hambleton%2C+Todd&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In a rare move at Monday's council meeting before attendance had been taken, Cornwall Mayor Justin Towndale provided what he felt was an important update regarding the proposed Great Wolf Lodge hotel and indoor waterpark.
Towndale was aiming to bat down rumours the project might not happen, saying that was simply not the case, and that on Jan. 15 he had a meeting with Steven Jacobsen, vice-president of development with Chicago-based Great Wolf Resorts.
"We went over the plans for the project, the next steps, and the City of Cornwall's investment in the infrastructure that will be required to support the project," Towndale said, adding Jacobsen presented the city with the project book for the proposed waterpark.
Towndale said he was bringing up the topic because in hearing from council members of the business community and residents, he believed there was no shortage of rumours and rumour-mongering in the community.
"(There are people) saying the project is cancelled, it's not going to happen, etc.," Towndale said.
"I want to just reiterate that (the hotel/waterpark) is happening, the project is moving forward ... spreading rumours and things that aren't true really doesn't benefit anybody, and I don't see what it accomplishes except creating a cloud of doubt about the city, so I want to encourage our residents not to fall into that trap and not listen to everything they hear necessarily, and (question) the source it's coming from."
Towndale in November told the Ottawa Business Journal Great Wolf Resorts had asked Ontario for $30 million in support to proceed with the project; the company was quoted in the same piece saying there were still steps to complete before it could confirm its intentions to build in Cornwall, but that at the time it remained optimistic.
In December, Stormont-Dundas-South Glengarry MPP Nolan Quinn, one of the speakers at the start of Cornwall Economic Development's 2023 Year in Review, addressed the pending arrival of the hotel and indoor water park.
He said that, with a shortage of skilled-trades workers in Eastern Ontario, an influx of as many as 2,500 of them could be headed this way at some point.
Always advertised by the city as a longer-term project that likely wouldn't have the resort open until 2026 or beyond, it's been about 21 months since Great Wolf Resorts signed an agreement of purchase and sale to acquire 40 acres in the business park.
Indications things have been progressing a little too slowly for some emerged earlier in 2023, including in the summer, when Stormont-Dundas-Glengarry Warden Tony Fraser wrote a letter to Ontario Premier Doug Ford asking for the province to support the project.
In the fall, when addressing Cornwall council, Quinn said getting the province on board with the proposed development has been a top priority, and he said the ball was moved forward when he spoke directly with Ford and had "a really fruitful discussion about that one. ... I've put all my eggs in the (GWL) basket because I know the importance of it."
Quinn also said the would-be $350-million investment, with the tertiary effects that would almost certainly follow, could see an investment of $1 billion in the city over the next decade.
One development directly related to the waterpark is the extension of Nick Kaneb Drive to McConnell Avenue.
Economic development manager Bob Peters, in one of his many updates, said extension work will begin in the first quarter of 2024.
That extension is tied to the development of several other parcels in the business park where other agreements of purchase have been signed. thambleton@postmedia.com
