Document 298 of 315

Act not needed to end protest, lawyer says; Federal Court justice rules use of law 'unconstitutional' and 'unjustified'

Author: Laucius, Joanne

Publication info: Laucius, Joanne. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/act-not-needed-end-protest-lawyer-says-federal/docview/2918399869/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Act+not+needed+to+end+protest%2C+lawyer+says%3B+Federal+Court+justice+rules+use+of+law+%27unconstitutional%27+and+%27unjustified%27&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Laucius%2C+Joanne&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Emergencies Act invoked in response to the 2022 convoy protest was "unreasonable, unjustified" and violated the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, according to a Federal Court decision released Tuesday.
But the raucous protest could also have been ended using other laws, says the lawyer who filed for the injunction that silenced the truck horns for 10 days.
"The question comes down to what level of government really failed. It was clear it was the municipal and provincial levels of government. The federal government didn't have jurisdiction, and it all unfolded on their doorsteps," Paul Champ, an Ottawa human rights lawyer, said Wednesday.
"We should have applied more pressure on the province."
Civil liberties groups had argued that the Liberal federal government went beyond its powers, violated the Charter and was not justified in invoking the Emergencies Act. The Liberals argued they had reasonable grounds to believe the protests posed a threat to national security and could not be dealt with in any other way.
Federal Court Justice Richard Mosley found that the record did not support a conclusion that the convoy protest had created "a critical, urgent and temporary situation" that was national in scope and could not effectively be dealt with under any other law of Canada. Although the harm being caused to Canada's economy, trade and commerce was very real and concerning, it did not constitute threats or the use of serious violence to persons or property, he wrote.
However, Mosley also found that the events of the convoy had gone "beyond legitimate protest" and were an "unacceptable breakdown of public order."
Former Somerset ward councillor Catherine McKenney said she had thought long and hard about the convoy participants since the report from the Public Order Emergency Commission chaired by Commissioner Paul Rouleau was released in February 2023.
"The participants violated the rights of people to live within our community in safety," McKenney said Wednesday.
"The Ottawa Police Service should have done their job, the city should have taken responsibility for its residents. Almost immediately people were afraid to be out in public. I remember reaching out a few days later and asking: 'What's the plan?'I got no response," McKenney said.
"Had they taken responsibility, I don't believe that three weeks later we would have needed the Emergencies Act."
Michelle Hurtubise, executive director of the Centretown Community Health Centre, said people who lived in downtown Ottawa at the time of the protest were concerned about the lack of action.
People reported being intimidated and harassed, she said. Residents who had Pride flags or Black Lives Matter flags had feces thrown at their doors and balconies, she said. The Centretown Community Health Centre had an evacuation plan in effect.
Hurtubise remembers wearing a mask on her way to a vaccination clinic for children and being bumped at least half a dozen times as she walked into the "red zone" of the protest.
"Working downtown with diesel truck fumes was toxic," Hurtubise said. "I agree with the right to peaceful protest. I don't believe that includes bringing in eighteen-wheelers."
The Federal Court decision minimized the trauma that people continue to live with, Hurtubise said.
"We were failed by municipal authorities, we were failed by provincial authorities, we were failed by federal authorities," she said. "It was not peaceful assembly and the tools that were available were not useful in this situation."
Mosley's ruling stemmed from four legal challenges by civil liberty groups, including the Canadian Civil Liberties Association and the Canadian Constitutional Foundation.
The groups argued the federal government exceeded its powers by invoking the Emergencies Act on Feb. 14, 2022, in response to ongoing convoy protest blockades in Ontario and Alberta.
"Emergency is not in the eye of the beholder. Emergency powers are necessary in extreme circumstances, but they are also dangerous to democracy. They should be used sparingly and carefully. They cannot be used even to address a massive and disruptive demonstration if that could have been dealt with through regular policing and laws," the CCLA said in a statement.
Rouleau's findings criticized the lack of communication between the federal and Ontario governments and the City of Ottawa, with all three levels of government expressing frustration. Ontario Premier Doug Ford argued that management of the convoy protest was a national issue, but the province is ultimately responsible for effective policing in Ottawa, Rouleau's report said.
Mosley's decision was not much different from Rouleau's conclusion, which was that it was "a failure of federalism," Champ said.
Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland has promised the government will appeal Mosley's decision.
That appeal will go to the Federal Court of Appeal. Champ said he believed it would have a reasonable chance of success.
"I think it all highlights that it should be exceptional circumstances to invoke these powers. That's the lesson for future leaders," Champ said.
Meanwhile, it appears there is resolve to implement measures to ensure that protests like this never happen again in downtown Ottawa. The city in January and February 2022 has become a textbook example of what not to do, Champ said.
For example, the Rolling Thunder motorcycle convoy in April 2022 resulted in at least 10 arrests and hundreds of Ottawa bylaw tickets for no-parking and no-stopping violations.
"The police were very clear: If you think you're going to bring a truck downtown, that isn't going to happen," Champ said.
Hurtubise said a lot of people still felt uneasy with the second anniversary of the convoy's arrival in Ottawa approaching next week.
"There are a lot of residual concerns in the community," she said. "People are still very anxious when any protest comes to town."
Mayor Mark Sutcliffe said the city had learned a lot since the protest and would be better prepared in the future.
"My focus is not on past decisions or on court rulings, but in making sure we have the right plans in place to protect the people of Ottawa," Sutcliffe said Wednesday.
"I think our police service has done a lot of work - as has city staff - in making sure what happened two years ago never happens again and that measures are in place to handle those kinds of threats more effectively." With files from Blair Crawford

Subject: Court decisions; Emergencies; National security; Evacuations & rescues; Civil liberties

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: emergencies; invoked; response; convoy; protest; unreasonable

Title: Act not needed to end protest, lawyer says; Federal Court justice rules use of law 'unconstitutional' and 'unjustified'

Publication title: The Ottawa Citizen; Ottawa, Ont.

First page: A.5

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 25, 2024

Section: City

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Document 299 of 315

Invoking Emergencies Act represented a 'failure of federalism,' says lawyer who obtained injunction

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/invoking-emergencies-act-represented-failure/docview/2918293308/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Invoking+Emergencies+Act+represented+a+%27failure+of+federalism%2C%27+says+lawyer+who+obtained+injunction&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Document 300 of 315

'The decision ... was not justified'; Govt's use of emergencies act to banish truck convoy violated charter, judge rules

Author: Nardi, Christopher

Publication info: Nardi, Christopher. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/decision-was-not-justified-govts-use-emergencies/docview/2918028726/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27The+decision+...+was+not+justified%27%3B+Govt%27s+use+of+emergencies+act+to+banish+truck+convoy+violated+charter%2C+judge+rules&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-01-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=Nardi%2C+Christopher&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Liberal government's decision to invoke the Emergencies Act in response to the 2022 anti-mandate convoy protests was unreasonable, unjustified and violated the Charter, the Federal Court has ruled.
In a lengthy ruling published Tuesday, Federal Court Justice Richard Mosley found that though the protests in early 2022 were causing harm to Canada's economy, trade and commerce, they did not rise to the level of a threat to national security as defined by the law.
He thus sided with civil liberties groups who argued that the Liberal government went beyond its powers and was not justified in its historic invocation of the Emergencies Act in February 2022.
"I have concluded that the decision to issue the Proclamation (of the Emergencies Act) does not bear the hallmarks of reasonableness - justification, transparency and intelligibility - and was not justified in relation to the relevant factual and legal constraints that were required to be taken into consideration," Mosley wrote.
On the same day, Deputy Prime Minister Chrystia Freeland said the government did not agree with Mosley's decision and promised to appeal it.
"The public safety of Canadians was under threat, our national security, which includes our national economic security, was under threat," she said.
"I was convinced at the time. It was the right thing to do. It was the necessary thing to do."
Mosley's ruling stems from four legal challenges by civil liberty groups, including the Canadian Civil Liberties Association (CCLA) and the Canadian Constitutional Foundation (CCF).
The groups argued that the Liberals exceeded their powers when they invoked the unprecedented powers of the act on Feb. 14, 2022, in response to ongoing convoy blockades in Ontario and Alberta.
Displaying an uncommon level of introspection in a ruling, Mosley admitted that at the outset of the hearing, his view was "leaning" towards the belief that the government's invocation of the Act was reasonable.
He said he considered the events of the convoy to have gone "beyond legitimate protest" and were an "unacceptable breakdown of public order." He also admitted that had he been at the government's table at the time of the decision, he may have agreed it was necessary to invoke the act.
"I acknowledge that in conducting judicial review of that decision, I am revisiting that time with the benefit of hindsight and a more extensive record of the facts and law than that which was before the (government)," he wrote.
But months of time to "carefully deliberate" on the evidence and arguments, as well as arguments by the CCF and CCLA, changed his mind.
"Their participation in these proceedings has demonstrated again the value of public interest litigants. Especially in presenting informed legal argument. This case may not have turned out the way it has without their involvement," he wrote.
Conser vative Leader Pierre Poilievre also reacted to the news on X (formerly Twitter) by placing the blame solely on Trudeau.
"He caused the crisis by dividing people. Then he violated Charter rights to illegally suppress citizens. As PM, I will unite our country for freedom," he wrote.
National Post

Subject: Charters; National security; Judicial reviews; Civil liberties

Business indexing term: Subject: Charters

Identifier / keyword: liberal; decision; invoke; emergencies; response; convoy

Title: 'The decision ... was not justified'; Govt's use of emergencies act to banish truck convoy violated charter, judge rules

Document 301 of 315

'No government could afford it'; Why a newly proposed guaranteed basic income is likely doomed

Author: Lévesque, Carine

Publication info: Lévesque, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-government-could-afford-why-newly-proposed/docview/2917702342/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27No+government+could+afford+it%27%3B+Why+a+newly+proposed+guaranteed+basic+income+is+likely+doomed&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.1&amp;au=L%C3%A9vesque%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The controversial idea of a guaranteed basic income, which could cost tens of billions of dollars each year, might be making its way to the House of Commons this spring.
But the chances of a guaranteed income becoming actual policy are "very close to zero," said Kevin Milligan, an economics professor at the University of British Columbia who specializes in government social spending.
"No government could afford it. Any government who was wise would see that this is not a good way to work on the important issues of poverty," Milligan said.
The Senate has been studying Ontario Sen. Kim Pate's Bill S-233, which strives to develop a national framework to provide people over the age of 17 in Canada with access to a "guaranteed livable basic income," including temporary workers, permanent residents and refugee claimants.
The bill would not actually implement a guaranteed basic income (GBI), but would force the finance minister to consult with provinces and territories, as well as Indigenous and other groups, to prepare a report setting out a framework for the policy.
If it passes a final vote in the Senate, the bill will head to the House of Commons. Liberals and Conservatives are currently refusing to take a clear position on the bill, arguing that they are waiting for the legislation to reach the lower chamber before they decide where they stand.
That sparked speculation in the following days from right-wing commentators such as the Western Standard's Cory Morgan and the Post Millennial's David Krayden, and also Conservative MP Leslyn Lewis, who wondered aloud on social media whether a basic income will end up becoming Trudeau's policy Hail Mary, with his party continuing to tank in the polls.
On Dec. 29 in sharing Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's new video on "debtonation" on Facebook, Lewis blamed the federal Liberals for creating "the debt problem that's connected to our inflation crisis" and wondered if Canadians would trust them again once "our traditional safety nets are destroyed."
"My guess is after they burn it all down, they will propose a solution where they have control: let's see ... a digital and programmable Universal Basic Income perhaps?" she asked.
Many progressive senators and Liberal MPs have been championing the idea of a basic income for years, and it seemed to take on new life during the pandemic as Ottawa rolled out large, widespread income supports.
But even then, Trudeau did not seem very enthusiastic about the idea: When he was asked about it, he said he was more interested in staying focused on controlling COVID-19.
The notion of a basic income is not new. Nobel laureate and free-market economist Milton Friedman championed the idea of a negative income tax - which would provide a certain basic income to people below a certain earning threshold, while eliminating all other government welfare programs.
Friedman's version, promising more efficiency, less bureaucracy and the freedom for recipients to spend their income as they see fit, appeals to some conservatives.
When he was the opposition finance critic, Poilievre wrote in the National Post in 2018 that "many basic income supporters have the right goal." Deputy party leader Melissa Lantsman told broadcaster Stephen LeDrew in 2020 that she "somewhat agree(s) with that" and that "Conservatives can own" the issue of a basic income.
The progressive version is different: Politicians and economists on the left have been instead championing a basic income on top of other welfare subsidies. They argue that the income would in time lift recipients out of poverty, reducing their reliance on other social supports.
"We're talking about insuring that people have adequate incomes but we're not talking about some Keynesian model that leaves everybody with just a bit of money and no other supports," said Sen. Pate last year.
That's why UBC's Milligan compares trying to define a basic income to nailing Jell-O to a wall. "Because any time you kind of nail it down to what you think they mean, it changes," he said.
Senator Pate, having been appointed under Trudeau, comes at it from the progressive side. She said Canada already provides supports similar to a basic income for some groups: the Guaranteed Income Supplement for low-income seniors; the Canada Child Benefit for families with children; and the soon-to-be Canada Disability Benefit for people with disabilities, for example.
"It's like we're inching toward it. Why not just take the next step?" she said.
But passing bills that are not government priorities can be a tedious and long process. S-233 was first introduced in the Senate in December 2021, at the same time as an identical one, C-223, was introduced in the House of Commons by NDP MP Leah Gazan.
Gazan's bill has remained untouched in the House, whereas the Senate bill was adopted on division in second reading in April 2023 to be further studied at committee. Even Conservative senators agreed to take a further look at S-233, despite claiming their caucus does not support it.
"I think that the light of day will expose the weaknesses in this bill," said the Conservative party's critic for the bill, Nova Scotia Senator Michael L. MacDonald, at the time. Sen. Pate, however, claims that there are "many, many people within all of the parties who are supportive" of her bill, and she remains hopeful its study in committee will wrap up soon after the Senate returns next month and be submitted to a final vote in the Upper Chamber in the spring.
Attempts have been made in the past to pilot basic income projects, but any promising results ran up against serious cost concerns. There was Manitoba's "Mincome" in the 1970s, Ontario's brief pilot in 2017-2018, and P.E.I. has been running a targeted basic income pilot program with a few hundred recipients since 2021.
British Columbia and Quebec have studied it but concluded that a targeted approach is better than a universal basic income.
"The needs of people in this society are too diverse to be effectively answered simply with a cheque from the government," wrote the B.C. Basic Income panel in its December 2020 report on the concept. "A basic income is a very costly approach to addressing any specific goal, such as poverty reduction."
The Parliamentary Budget Officer produced an analysis in 2021 that concluded that a national guaranteed basic income using parameters of Ontario's 2017 pilot project could cut Canada's poverty rate by half in just one year, but at a hefty cost of $91 billion in 2024-25 and $93 billion in 2025-26. That's nearly double what Ottawa spends, for example, on health transfers to the provinces.
"So I think the probability of a UBI (universal basic income) being implemented in Canada on a widespread basis, not as small pilot or something, is very close to zero," said Milligan.
Even Sen. Pate admits that there are impediments for political parties to move forward with a basic income, given the initial cost of the measure and the fact that any given party is unlikely to reap all of the benefits of cracking down on poverty in Canada in one electoral cycle. But she remains hopeful that she will see some sort of basic income become reality in her lifetime.
"I think it's much like medicare," she said. "Many people thought it was a good idea, (but) people were afraid what it would cost and afraid that they wouldn't see the benefit within the electoral cycle. And then eventually, Saskatchewan just decided to do it and once it started rolling out, everybody wanted in."
Milligan said he agrees with the B.C. panel's approach, which is to "roll up your sleeves and do the hard work of expanding, improving, replacing, revamping our existing social support system."
"It's complicated, it's messy, it's hard work," he said. "I think a lot of the basic income stuff is just people looking for a magical solution to a tough problem."
National Post calevesque@postmedia.com

Today's letters: Book of obituaries reflects so many rich lives

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/todays-letters-book-obituaries-reflects-so-many/docview/2916876299/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Today%27s+letters%3A+Book+of+obituaries+reflects+so+many+rich+lives&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Summing up such rich lives
Re: Decade of newspaper death notices was 'anthropological playground', Jan. 13.
Many thanks to Bruce Deachman for his article about Donna Sharkey's book on death notices. Like her, I read the obituaries in the Citizen and other daily papers of the various cities where I have lived. Is it an obsession with death and dying? An interest in mankind? A desire of recognition for that life lived?
Unlike Sharkey, I do not read obituaries for solace and companionship. Instead, I reflect on the life, the achievements, the chosen charities, and the illness that resulted in the death. But mostly, my takeaway is the importance of and love for family. The times shared together at home, on vacations and of course at the lake and cottage. Too often we forget what really is important in life and yet the obituary usually sums it up nicely.
We have all lived, played, worked, had career and athletic accomplishments, travelled and volunteered, and now some of us are dead. The sum of our lives becomes a short story in an obituary. Some lives, I wish I could know more about. And yes, reading about pets softens my heart.
This past October, I wandered through the old St. Duthus Cemetery in the town of Tain, County of Ross in Scotland. I do the same at McGuigan Cemetery in Merrickville. Both cemeteries are no longer "active," yet abound with stories that I will never know. My imagination runs wild.
And so, each newspaper obituary allows me to peek into a life before it fades away into a grave or urn. A final review.
Donna Ross, Merrickville
Kudos to OCH for buying townhouses
Re: Townhouse purchase shows city's incoherent approach to social housing, Jan. 16.
In critiquing the Ottawa Community Housing purchase of 311 aging townhomes, Randall Denley suggests this is an idea that does not easily sell itself. He's wrong about that.
Transferring ownership of existing moderately affordable properties from market investors to a non-profit owner preserves existing relatively affordable rents at a much lower cost than building new. More important, 90 per cent of the cost to buy is covered by a mortgage loan and will be repaid by tenant rents (as is the case in all investment properties).
This was a savvy investment by OCH at less than half the cost to build new affordable units with similar rents. As revealed in data from rentals.ca, rents for new tenancies are increasing at double-digit rates as investors take advantage of vacancy decontrol. Instead of such exorbitant increases, OCH will preserve affordable rents.
That's an idea that DOES sell itself.
Steve Pomeroy, Senior Research Fellow, Carleton University, Centre for Urban Research and Education (CURE)
Pinecrest School: Board has lost its way
Re: How many Pinecrest Public School teachers were 'investigated?' No one will say, Jan. 14.
I applaud the Citizen for its ongoing reporting about the disturbing circumstances at Pinecrest Public School in 2022. Unfortunately, the report on the situation, based on an investigation by the Ottawa-Carleton District School Board's Office of Human Rights and Equity, is not being made public.
Hiding behind a veil of privacy legislation, when personal information could have been redacted, only fosters the suspicion that the school board is suppressing conclusions that are incongruent with its Diversity, Equity and Inclusion ethos and would call into question the competency of board administration.
Why the Office of Human Rights and Equity was tasked to investigate a school that appears to have descended into chaos was puzzling in the first place. Whose rights did the board believe were being violated? Surely not those of the roaming students who were asked to put away their cellphones and return to class.
Equalling puzzling is one of the solutions shared with parents: the school will work to be more inclusive. Does the OCDSB believe that yet more expensive Diversity, Equity and Inclusion training for teachers will prevent this dystopian crisis from recurring? Would you send your child to Pinecrest Public School? Sadly, some families have no choice.
Education is about imparting knowledge and developing skills within a culture of discipline and mutual respect. As it lurches from one crisis to another, it is obvious the OCDSB has lost sight of that mission.
J.T. Waterhouse, Ottawa
Why seniors pay their school taxes
Re: Letter, Give seniors a break on school taxes, Jan. 6.
The writer wants seniors who have paid school taxes for 25 years to be exempt from them due to the "luxuries" teachers want and the millions of dollars spent "subsidizing" the homeless, the poor, immigrants and the less fortunate.
Spending money educating young people is an investment in their future and the future of our country. In addition, I think it is our duty and obligation to help the homeless, the poor, immigrants and the less fortunate so that they can hope for a better life and contribute more to our society.
My home is paid off and I feel fortunate. I worry about young people who feel that home ownership is an out-of-reach dream. Paying taxes enables us to enjoy needed services like education, health care, or the many services we don't realize our taxes provide to enhance our collective standard of living.
I agree with another of your letter-writers that we do need to have a conversation about taxes, why we pay them, and what good they can do for society.
Rajiv Kalsi, Ottawa
Property owners are already ahead
Here's a fact: property owners in Ontario pay school taxes; nobody else. If you own a property, you are way ahead financially of those who do not.
I suppose the writer is suggesting that with inflation, seniors may be having difficulty making ends meet. Although this is true for many, I doubt if it applies to most senior homeowners who live without mortgages.
Our municipal taxes go to support our services and quality of life. We don't all have children; we don't all use community centres; we don't all drive or walk in the winter or use the bus; we may never need the police or a paramedic; but this is part of living in a democracy. It is our shared responsibility.
Cathy Haley, Ottawa
Something's rotten at Loblaw
The decision by Loblaw to stop selling expiring groceries and produce at 50 per cent off (now it is only 30 per cent), and the quiet manner in which they did this, speaks volumes about the "values" of the company.
While the chain and executives are reaping in profits, many less fortunate people and those on fixed income rely on 50-per-cent discounts to save money amid rising food prices.
Loblaw is still going to have to pay to dispose of unsold food - more of which may end up in waste stream if the discount is only 30 per cent. Perhaps the "tipping fees" for disposing of unsold grocery products should be raised to provide an incentive to Loblaw et al. to make more food available at reasonable prices (which will lead to more sales and less waste).
Kris Nanda, Ottawa
Police board must embrace transparency
Re: Police board sets information-sharing policy following convoy protest audit, Dec. 27.
As a former Ottawa Police Services Board member, I recognize this as a continuation of efforts that began with the April 2020 approval of the "Internal Communication Protocols" document, a motion I strongly advocated for.
These measures, then and now, are vital for enhancing transparency and effective communication within the board.
The 2020 document laid the groundwork for clear, consistent internal communication, a necessity for effective governance and public trust. The recent policy, catalyzed by the 2022 convoy protest, builds on this by formalizing the information-sharing process.
It emphasizes the need for board staff, the vital link between the board and its stakeholders, to be well-informed and capable of accurately conveying the board's perspectives and decisions.
In an era where transparency is paramount, it is essential that these practices are not only in place but actively embraced in spirit. The OPSB's commitment to open communication is crucial for maintaining public confidence and ensuring that the board's actions reflect the community's values and expectations.
Daljit S. Nirman, Ottawa
Ed Broadbent will be deeply missed
Re: Broadbent to be honoured with state funeral in Ottawa, Jan. 15.
Ed Broadbent was a very good friend of mine. I am also a lifelong supporter of the federal and provincial New Democratic Party. Broadbent worked hard for First Nations' rights and equality for all Canadians, particularly women's rights and decent pensions for all.
He was also a strong supporter of all labour unions and a great communicator, who created the Broadbent Institute.
Ed Broadbent was the "Greatest prime minister Canada never had." He will be deeply missed by many.
Jack Berry, Ottawa
Headline conflated two different polls
Re: Trump is more popular among Canadians than Trudeau, Jan. 15.
The source of the headline seems to be a Spark Advocacy poll that asked Canadians if they would vote for Trump or Biden. Thirty-three per cent of Canadians would apparently vote for Trump in the U.S. election. An editor then used these results to wrongly craft a headline that Trump is more popular than Trudeau. Apparently this conclusion was reached by comparing these results to another poll about Trudeau's approval rating in Canada.
The Spark Advocacy poll didn't ask Canadians if they prefer Trudeau or Trump. To draw this conclusion from two disparate polls is false.
Charles Crisp, Ottawa
Sure, let's look at Poilievre's vacation
Re: Letter, And what about Poilievre's holiday? Jan. 17.
A reader suggests that in view of the amount of attention given to the ethics of the prime minister's recent holiday in Jamaica, the Citizen should also run a story on Pierre Poilievre's Christmas vacation.
I agree, but suspect that a comparison between the two, particularly in the cost area, would be startling and do much to reinforce Poilievre's popular views on spending restraint.
Charles Morton, Manotick
Send immigrants to Trudeau directly
I have a partial solution to Toronto's concerns over the cost of housing the great influx of immigrants there. The city could load up three buses and send them to Justin Trudeau's residence, the immigration minister's residence and the NDP leader's residence, since he continues to support Trudeau's policies.
These buses could just drop the people off and tell them to knock on the aforementioned doors. This would no doubt get some kind of action.
Stan Painter, Kanata
What does the business community want?
Re: The deadline to repay small-business loans must be extended, Jan. 12.
Private sector leaders and organizations regularly
tell governments to act more like businesses. Then when a government does act like a business - by requiring repayments of loans freely entered into by their recipients -governments are criticized. So what kind of government does the private sector really want?
John Butcher, Ottawa
Why Canada really needs high-speed rail
Re: To fight climate change, move people from highways and planes to high-speed trains, Jan. 15.
A family member spends a great part of her life commuting by Via Rail between Ottawa and Toronto. Delays happen much of the time and in any weather.
Sharing tracks with freight trains makes a passenger trip slow, uncertain and uncomfortable. More frequent trains would only make the existing problems worse. Any plan for improvement should be for a new high-speed system.
It should also be a long-term plan to eventually take in the whole of the country from the Maritime provinces to the Pacific. The goal should be a high-speed system to take in the whole of Canada.
High-speed trains would bring a cross-country trip, as an alternate to flying, into the realm of possibility. I lived in Europe 30 years ago and even then, you could travel between cities by train in about half the time it took by car or even air. Now, intercity travel by air has been virtually eliminated in Europe.
True, those countries are smaller, and ease of land expropriation for the track beds is less politically fraught than it would be in Canada. It would take a strong federal government to smooth out differences between provinces. I don't think this would be the case under the present leadership.
A cross-country high speed rail system would go a long way toward achieving our climate-change goals by reducing the frequency of air travel for every necessary trip.
Valerie Burton, Ottawa
Emmy's story lifted my heart
Re: Ottawa preemie becomes the first in world to receive stem cell treatment in clinical trial, Jan. 18.
What a wonderful story to read on the front page of the Citizen. With all the woes of the world at our doorstep, it made my day to read of the success enjoyed by Emmy Cogan and her parents.
Al Jones, Almonte

Document 303 of 315

Consultant now heads anti-Israel TV channel; Feds cancelled contract after vitriolic posts

Author: Dawson, Tyler

Publication info: Dawson, Tyler. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/consultant-now-heads-anti-israel-tv-channel-feds/docview/2916537785/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Consultant+now+heads+anti-Israel+TV+channel%3B+Feds+cancelled+contract+after+vitriolic+posts&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-01-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=NP.2&amp;au=Dawson%2C+Tyler&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Laith Marouf, the government-funded anti-racism consultant whose record of hateful tweets became an embarrassing controversy for the Liberals, is now the director of an anti-Israel online broadcaster.
Free Palestine Television (FPTV) was launched after the Hamas massacre of Israeli civilians on Oct. 7 and has featured footage of Hamas's attacks on Israeli soldiers as well as manoeuvres from Hamas-affiliated militias such as the Mujahideen Brigades and Al-Qassam Brigades, which operate out of Gaza and the West Bank. It also published speeches with live translation to English from Hezbollah leader Sayyed Hassan Nasrallah and it publishes press releases attributed to various terror groups, such as Islamic Resistance in Iraq, an umbrella group of Iran-backed militias.
Marouf, who's identified on its site as FPTV's founder, is a senior consultant with the Community Media Advocacy Centre, which had a $122,000 contract with the Liberal government in 2022 to develop an anti-racism strategy for the broadcasting sector.
It subsequently emerged that Marouf had a history of hateful social media posts about Jews, francophones and Black people.
One read: "You know all those loud mouthed bags of human feces, a.k.a. the Jewish White Supremacists; when we liberate Palestine and they have to go back to where they come from, they will return to being low voiced b-----s of their Christian/Secular White Supremacist Masters."
The tweets were exposed in news reports and the government cancelled the contract in August 2022, although it was revealed that the federal government had known about the tweets a month earlier and had done nothing.
Marouf 's lawyer at the time said the posts were not directed at all Jews, just certain ones.
Canadian Heritage has since changed the way it vets funding requests for community and anti-racism projects.
T he federal government has sought to recoupits funds from Marouf through a collections agency, the Canada Revenue Agency and the courts, but has reported no success. Reports in October 2022 said Marouf was living in Beirut.
Since the scandal over funding to Marouf's organization, he has become a regular on social media programs, offering opinions on Palestinian politics, Israel and other geopolitical issues. He has also appeared on Critical Hour, a podcast hosted by Sputnik News, a state-owned Russian broadcaster, and on programs by Russian propaganda network Russia Today.
FPTV's website says it launched "with the outbreak of the Al-Aqsa Flood" - the name Hamas and other Palestinian groups have given to the Oct. 7 massacre. The online station is based out of Lebanon, and is staffed primarily by students, professors and community members, Marouf said in an interview.
"Knowing that there is a huge gap left in news programming in English that is supportive of resistance and the right to liberation ... we decided to bring together our communities here to start a project, a community television station," Marouf said in the interview with Activist News Network in November 2023.
The Middle East Forum, an American conser vative think tank, published a report on Friday claiming that FPTV had links to the Lebanon-based group Al-Tajammu. According to Michael Barak, a researcher at the Institute for Counter-Terrorism at Israel's Reichmann University, Al-Tajammu is "an international pro-Iranian platform to leverage the resistance axis against the U.S., Israel and their allies."
Marouf described the attacks on Al-Tajammu as "frivolous" and accused the Middle East Forum of being a "racist organization."
Marouf and FPTV also downplayed any connections to Al-Tajammu.
In a series of posts to X on Saturday, FPTV alleged that "the largest Jewish White Supremacist accounts in Apartheid Canada are mounting a disinformation campaign against our Community Television station," and that it "is unaffiliated with any political party or groups, including Al-Tajammu which hosted our studios for a brief time & we have since moved as we expanded."
The Middle East Forum report, and subsequent social media posts, suggested that Free Palestine TV was "established" in Canada.
Marouf told National Post: "It's not established in Canada. It's not based in Canada. There's no connection there."
In mid-December, FPTV hosted a fundraiser for the channel on Zoom, which featured guest speakers who are prominent figures in Indigenous advocacy in Canada. Among them were Sleydo', also known as Molly Wickham, a Wet'suwet'en chief and prominent opponent of the Coastal GasLink pipeline in British Columbia; Judy Da Silva, a prominent member of the Grassy Narrows First Nation in northwestern Ontario; and Isaac Murdoch, an artist and member of the Serpent River First Nation. National Post did not attend the event and was unable to confirm that all the guests appeared.
National Post

Document 304 of 315

Falling into the 'population trap'

Author: Kheiriddin, Tasha

Publication info: Kheiriddin, Tasha. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/falling-into-population-trap/docview/2915816043/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Falling+into+the+%27population+trap%27&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Kheiriddin%2C+Tasha&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada's immigration policies are damaging the economy. That's the finding of Canada's top bank economists, who issued a dire warning to the federal government. By admitting 455,000 new permanent residents and more than 800,000 non-permanent residents last year, Canada is on the brink of a recession, or worse.
"I'll put it bluntly: We've fallen into the population trap," said Stéfane Marion, chief economist at National Bank of Canada. An increase in the standard of living is no longer possible because "you don't have enough savings to stabilize your capital-to-labour ratio."
We also learned that the government was warned two years ago by its own officials about high immigration causing a housing shortage - and ignored those warnings.
When asked about this, Housing Minister Sean Fraser replied, "I don't think anybody needs a briefing note to understand that having more people in the housing market impacts the housing market, but having gone through in consecutive years the process of building Canada's immigration levels plan, I can tell you that there are an enormous number of factors that go into them."
OK then, so what are those factors? Why did the government decide that immigration levels should increase at this pace? Why does it continue to allow hundreds of thousands of foreign students to work unlimited hours? Why did it admit so many people into the country in such a short amount of time? The classic answer is that we need to increase the labour supply. Businesses are short of workers. Canadians are not having enough children to replace them. That's the argument of the Century Initiative, the business community and immigration advocates.
But simply pulling in adult immigrants and temporary workers is not the same as boosting fertility in the existing population. Those workers have needs that are very different from those of children.
They won't live under their parents'roof; they need their own housing. They won't be ferried about by their parents; they need independent transportation. They will be buying adult levels of food, clothing and other necessities. In short, they will be competing with other adults for basic needs, and that increased demand means two things: price inflation and resource scarcity. Which is exactly what Canada is now experiencing.
Fraser is right. You don't need a study to figure this out. It's simple common sense. So again, why did the government do this - especially after it was warned not to by its own bureaucrats? The cynic in me sees one reason: votes.
Immigrants tend to settle in cities. City dwellers tend to vote for "progressive" parties. In Canada, since the 1960s that has translated to increased Liberal support. According to research done at Western University, a oneunit shift in the "urbanity score" for a riding produces an increase of eight percentage points in the Liberal party's vote share in that riding. No surprise then, that in the 2021 election, the Liberals won 109 of the 150 most urban ridings, while the Conservatives took only 23.
Immigrants are also grateful to the party that brings them in. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau learned this from his father. Pierre Elliott Trudeau took credit for the citizenship of millions of Canadians, even though many had come to Canada under previous governments. Why? Because they took their oath under his watch. And when it came to elections, the immigrant communities of the day - Italians, Greeks, Haitians, Vietnamese - tended to vote Liberal.
The implications of Trudeau Jr.'s replication of this policy are dire. Studies show that they are turning Canadians against immigration: even 62 per cent of current immigrants think we're letting in too many people. They are impoverishing Canadians, both current and newcomer, according to the bank study. And they are also helping fuel Quebec separatism, as francophones look with alarm at rising immigrant populations in the Rest of Canada. By the end of the century, Quebec risks becoming a bit player in Confederation, with only 15 per cent of its population.
This cannot continue. Instead of flooding the country with newcomers, the government needs to boost domestic productivity. That's a harder fix - and one that won't give them more votes. But then again, if Canadians can't afford a decent life, the Liberals may not get their votes, either. Tasha Kheiriddin is Postmedia's national politics columnist.

Subject: Canadians; Immigrants; Immigration

Location: Canada; Quebec Canada

Identifier / keyword: immigration; policies; damaging; economy; finding; economists

Document 305 of 315

Ontario lets hospitals 'hang by a thread' amid financial insecurity: health critics

Author: Payne, Elizabeth

Publication info: Payne, Elizabeth. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-lets-hospitals-hang-thread-amid-financial/docview/2915815480/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+lets+hospitals+%27hang+by+a+thread%27+amid+financial+insecurity%3A+health+critics&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Payne%2C+Elizabeth&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Most are reluctant to speak publicly about the financial uncertainty they are facing right now, but behind closed doors many Ontario hospital officials are desperate, opposition health critics say.
"Many of them are worried about how they are supposed to continue their operations and respond at a time when they are facing unprecedented pressure," said Dr. Adil Shamji, who is the Ontario Liberal caucus critic for health.
Shamji said he was hearing about hospitals that were considering limiting surgeries amid financial insecurity and pressure from emergency department and hospital overcrowding.
"At a time when pressures in our emergency departments and our patient care are at their highest, hospitals need the funding in order to be able to respond and serve our communities. The big risk is that they do not have the capability and the funding in order to do so and at the end of the day, there are lives on the line."
He said the province's willingness to allow hospitals to "hang by a thread" was a damning indictment of their risk management and signalled their satisfaction with the status quo.
"The near universal state of hospitals running deficits in our province is frankly unprecedented," Shamji said.
This newspaper has reported that the vast majority of the 140 hospitals in Ontario are projecting deficits this year. In addition, some have taken out high-interest loans to keep going and others have had to dig into reserves earmarked for specific projects.
The Ontario government has issued special waivers to most hospitals allowing them to carry deficits, the Ontario Hospital Association says. Hospitals in the province are, under normal circumstances, required to balance their budgets and such waivers are rarely used.
"I have never seen it this bad," said France Gélinas, who is health critic with the Official Opposition NDP.
"I have had many CEOs call me directly and had long talks about how desperate they are, and so are their boards," she said.
The situation is particularly dire in the North and among rural hospitals, which increasingly rely on agency nurses to remain open - that costs the hospital significantly more than they pay regular staff.
At some locations in the North, nursing agency representatives sit in the parking lot and hire nurses as they are leaving their shifts at the hospital.
"The next day the nurse comes in at twice the wage. It is the same nurse that has been there for 10 years and now doesn't have to work night shifts or weekend and can pick and choose her shifts," Gélinas said. It then becomes more difficult to fill those shifts and nursing agencies charge hospitals a premium to hire temporary nurses to do so, exacerbating a hospital's financial strain, she said.
Gélinas has tabled a bill calling for regulation of private nursing agencies.
A key issue for all hospitals is that the Ontario government has not fully reimbursed them for retroactive payments to hospital staff covered by the wage restraint legislation Bill 124, which was declared unconstitutional by the courts.
Last month, the Ontario Hospital Association wrote to provincial officials urging them to fully reimburse hospitals for the retroactive payouts, but the OHA also said the worsening financial woes facing the province's hospitals go beyond those payments and reflect years of underfunding. Ontario has the fewest hospital beds per population among Canadian provinces and the lowest rate among countries followed by the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development.
The discontinuation of special pandemic-related funding last year, even as Ontario is experiencing one of its biggest pandemic waves, has added more stress to hospitals. OHA president and chief executive Anthony Dale said he thought that COVID-19 should continue to be a "fixed cost" for hospitals for the foreseeable future.
Shamji said he wanted to see the provincial government come forward with funding for the retroactive payments, to restore pandemic funding and to regulate temporary nursing agencies.
He also said performance in emergency departments across the province was directly linked to "inadequate funding."
Gélinas, too, said she fears for patient safety, given the current situation.
"The biggest file for the provincial government is health and the biggest health file is hospitals. I can't believe this is where we end up in Ontario," Gélinas said. "It shouldn't be like this. And I have no doubt that people's lives and health will suffer."
The Ontario Ministry of Health did not respond to requests for comment.

Subject: Emergency medical care; Nursing; Funding; Nurses; Pandemics; Critics

Document 306 of 315

'I have never seen it this bad': Hospital officials are desperate about their financial uncertainty, opposition MPPs say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/i-have-never-seen-this-bad-hospital-officials-are/docview/2915683476/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Full text: Most are reluctant to speak publicly about the financial uncertainty they are facing right now, but behind closed doors many Ontario hospital officials are desperate, opposition health critics say.
"Many of them are worried about how they are supposed to continue their operations and respond at a time when they are facing unprecedented pressure," said Dr. Adil Shamji, who is the Ontario Liberal caucus critic for health.
Shamji said he was hearing about hospitals that were considering limiting surgeries amid financial insecurity and pressure from emergency department and hospital overcrowding.
"At a time when pressures in our emergency departments and our patient care are at their highest, hospitals need the funding in order to be able to respond and serve our communities. The big risk is that they do not have the capability and the funding in order to do so and at the end of the day, there are lives on the line."

This week, a spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones's office said the government has communicated it would provide financial support to hospitals "facing financial restraint."
The Ontario government has issued special waivers to most hospitals allowing them to carry deficits, the Ontario Hospital Association says. Hospitals in the province are, under normal circumstances, required to balance their budgets and such waivers are rarely used.
"I have never seen it this bad," said France Gélinas, who is health critic with the Official Opposition NDP.
"I have had many CEOs call me directly and had long talks about how desperate they are, and so are their boards," she said.
The situation is particularly dire in the North and among rural hospitals, which increasingly rely on agency nurses to remain open - that costs the hospital significantly more than they pay regular staff.

At some locations in the North, nursing agency representatives sit in the parking lot and hire nurses as they are leaving their shifts at the hospital.
"The next day the nurse comes in at twice the wage. It is the same nurse that has been there for 10 years and now doesn't have to work night shifts or weekend and can pick and choose her shifts," Gélinas said.
It then becomes more difficult to fill those shifts and nursing agencies charge hospitals a premium to hire temporary nurses to do so, exacerbating a hospital's financial strain, she said.
Gélinas has tabled a bill calling for regulation of private nursing agencies.
A key issue for all hospitals is that the Ontario government has not fully reimbursed them for retroactive payments to hospital staff covered by the wage restraint legislation Bill 124, which was declared unconstitutional by the courts.
Last month the Ontario Hospital Association wrote to provincial officials urging them to fully reimburse hospitals for the retroactive payouts, but the OHA also said the worsening financial woes facing the province's hospitals go beyond those payments and reflect years of underfunding. Ontario has the fewest hospital beds per population among Canadian provinces and the lowest rate among countries followed by the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD).
The discontinuation of special pandemic-related funding last year, even as Ontario is experiencing one of its biggest pandemic waves, has added more stress to hospitals. OHA president and CEO Anthony Dale said he thought that COVID-19 should continue to be a "fixed cost" for hospitals for the foreseeable future.
Shamji said he wanted to see the provincial government come forward with funding for the retroactive payments, to restore pandemic funding and to regulate temporary nursing agencies.
He also noted that performance in emergency departments across the province was directly linked to "inadequate funding."
Gélinas, too, says she fears for patient safety, given the current situation.
"The biggest file for the provincial government is health and the biggest health file is hospitals. I can't believe this is where we end up in Ontario," Gélinas said. "It shouldn't be like this. And I have no doubt that people's lives and health will suffer."
The Ontario Ministry of Health did not respond to requests for comment.
On Wednesday, a spokesperson for Jones said the ministry is working with hospitals and the Ontario Hospital Association "to ensure all of our hospitals have the tools they need to continue to deliver high-quality care patients deserve.
"This includes communicating to the OHA and hospital corporations across the province that our government would provide financial support to hospitals facing financial restraint," said spokesperson Hannah Jensen.
Jensen also said the Progressive Conservative government has made record investments in health-care, including increasing the health-care budget by more than 18 billion since 2018. This year's $80 million in funding includes a four per cent increase to the hospital sector and an additional $44 million to tackle emergency department wait times, in addition to an "historic" investment in pediatric health care.
Jensen also said more than 17,000 new nurses and 2,400 new physicians have been added this year. The organization that represents registered practical nurses says that the number of new RPNs is not much more than the number that have left the profession during the same period.

Document 307 of 315

Why Canada must build high-speed rail; Canadians deserve better, faster and cleaner service, David Landry writes

Author: Landry, David

Publication info: Landry, David. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/why-canada-must-build-high-speed-rail-canadians/docview/2915439903/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Why+Canada+must+build+high-speed+rail%3B+Canadians+deserve+better%2C+faster+and+cleaner+service%2C+David+Landry+writes&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Landry%2C+David&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Imagine walking into Ottawa Station, grabbing breakfast, and hopping aboard your train at 10 a.m. You settle down, open your laptop and finish up a couple of reports for work. Two hours later, you step off the train in Toronto's Union Station just in time for lunch with an important client.
After, you take a few calls and clear your inbox. At 5 p.m., you hop on your train back to Ottawa, have dinner, and have a quick nap after a long day. By 7 p.m., you're in a cab on your way home to your family for the night.
This would be the future if Canada were to build highspeed rail in the Quebec City-Windsor corridor.
With the growing threat of a climate crisis, Canadians deserve frequent, reliable, high-speed and non-polluting rail service between our biggest population centres, just as other G7 countries have.
Electrified rail service is far more energy-efficient than airplane or automobile travel, and with more Canadians incentivized to ride the rails, there would be fewer planes in the sky and cars on the road.
Unfortunately, high-speed rail does not appear to be a priority for the current federal government. The Liberals are currently awaiting proposals from three contenders to build a high-frequency network between Toronto and Quebec City, but not a high-speed one.
While having more frequent trains would be a marked improvement over today's service, it would be a lost opportunity to build a new intercity rail system without being able to offer high-speed travel.
What's more, the high-frequency rail plan would leave out southwestern Ontario, skipping over major economic centres including Hamilton, Kitchener-Waterloo, London and Windsor.
Leaving these communities out of the plan would be very unfortunate, especially considering the very forgiving agricultural landscapes west of Toronto.
As it stands, if you were to take an Ottawa-Toronto journey today, you would either have to fly, drive or take Via Rail.
Flying would be timeconsuming with ground transportation to and from suburban airports, security lineups, and boarding waiting time.
If you were to drive, you'd lose hours of productive time and energy behind the wheel.
If you were to take Via Rail, you'd enjoy the convenience of downtown-to-downtown transit, but your scheduled 4.5-hour ride could be riddled with delays thanks to the private freight operators who own and manage most tracks between Windsor and Quebec City.
There is a reason Air Canada offers 12 daily round-trip flights between Ottawa and Toronto-Pearson: the train just isn't good enough for many travellers.
Canadians deserve better, faster, cleaner and more convenient connectivity between communities, especially in this corridor, which is home to 18 million people.
Students in London should be able to visit their families in Ottawa without it being a major expedition. Public servants in Quebec City should be able to make reasonable day trips to Montreal.
Rail projects can be expensive and time-consuming to build, but to take this step into the future, we must act now. The best time to build high-speed rail was yesterday. The second-best time is today.
If Canada wants to show it's taking serious action on climate change, it would be great to start by getting people off the highways, out of the skies and onto high-speed trains. David Landry is a sustainable transportation advocate pursuing a bachelor of arts and Science at McMaster University. Email: davidlandry.mcmaster@gmail.com

Subject: Canadians; High speed rail

Document 308 of 315

Non-Public Funds workers take to picket lines, demand fair contract

Author: Morrison, Carine

Publication info: Morrison, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/non-public-funds-workers-take-picket-lines-demand/docview/2915439721/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Non-Public+Funds+workers+take+to+picket+lines%2C+demand+fair+contract&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;issn=08393222&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=Morrison%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Almost 500 Non-Public Funds workers represented by the Public Service Alliance of Canada (PSAC) made their way to picket lines in Ontario and Quebec on Monday morning to demand a fair contract agreement.
Members working in Ottawa rallied together by the Canadian Forces Morale and Welfare Services (CFMWS) and the CANEX Retail Store buildings on Labelle Street starting at 8 a.m. Monday. Pickets were bundled in winter jackets, hats and ski pants as the wind chill made it feel like -20 C.
According to PSAC, strike action is affecting Non-Public Funds workers in Bagotville, Kingston, Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu, Ottawa, Petawawa and Valcartier. Some members made their way to picket lines by 6:30 a.m.
Julie Laska, picket captain and president of Local 70682 under the Union of National Defence Employees, said she has worked as a Non-Public Funds employee for almost eight years.
Laska said it's bittersweet to be on strike, but said workers need to be on the line to fight for their salaries.
"Do we want to be here? No. We want to reach an agreement with the employer," said Laska, who is a member of the bargaining team. "People are here. We're cold, but we're here. It's important."
CFMWS is a separate agency, with NPF workers supporting Canadian Armed Forces members, veterans and their families by delivering morale and various welfare services. Forming part of the federal public service, they work in retail and food service through the Canadian Forces Exchange System, in financial planning for Canadian Armed Forces members through SISIP financial and in community service, physical fitness and recreation.
According to the Government of Canada website, CFMWS operates on behalf of the chief of defence staff and under the authority of the defence minister. As staff are not National Defence or Treasury Board employees, they have their own pension and pay system, and collective agreements.
Without a contract since 2022, members took part in strike votes from Nov. 20 to Dec. 14. On average, 94 per cent of members voted in favour of strike action. This is the first time NPF workers have voted for a strike mandate and the first PSAC strike since the national movement last spring.
NPF workers are fighting for fair wages, a national pay grid for workers across Canada and better job security. According to PSAC, NPF workers in Petawawa make less than a dollar per hour more than the federal minimum wage.
"We are some of the lowest paid public servants," Laska said, adding that about 75 members are working in Ottawa, though low wages cause a high rate of turnaround. "I love that I get to support the military every single day, and I just want to be supported to do that role."
PSAC national president Chris Aylward said this strike is about wages. While the parties are "not that far apart," he said they're still "not there."
"You could be doing one job here in Ottawa and somebody in Edmonton could be doing the exact same job but yet get paid six or seven dollars more an hour," Aylward said. "For far too long, these members have been underpaid, and now we're looking basically to do catch up. It's almost like a domino effect. You pay the workers a low wage, they move on and the military are the ones who suffer because there's a constant changeover and turnover in the staff who provide those services."
Alex Silas, PSAC's regional executive vice-president for the National Capital Region, said last week that the agency was using "scare tactics," encouraging workers to cross the picket line, signalling their pay would be stopped and that benefits would be cut off.
In a statement, NDP labour critic Alexandre Boulerice said it was "deeply disappointing that the federal government has been unable to reach a fair agreement."
"These dedicated workers answer calls from our Canadian Forces seeking crucial support for their well-being and for morale," Boulerice said. "We call on the Liberals not to interfere in these workers'right to strike, and to swiftly come to the negotiation table with a fair agreement that respects these workers."
Andrée-Anne Poulin, a spokesperson for the Department of National Defence, said the CFMWS is "prepared for strike action."
Poulin said operational committees are in place to support the organization during a strike and ensure "minimal disruptions" to key operations as well as the protection of personnel, property and facilities.
Diana Ebadi, press secretary to Minister of National Defence Bill Blair, said the minister believes that the best deals are made at the table and encourages both PSAC and CFMWS to work together on achieving a fair contract for employees.

Subject: Agreements; Strikes; Defense; Employees; Armed forces; Wages & salaries

Location: Canada

Company / organization: Name: Canadian Forces; NAICS: 928110

Title: Non-Public Funds workers take to picket lines, demand fair contract

Publication title: The Ottawa Citizen; Ottawa, Ont.

First page: A.1

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Jan 16, 2024

Section: City

Publisher: Postmedia Network Inc.

Place of publication: Ottawa, Ont.

Document 309 of 315

Non-Public Funds workers take to the picket lines on Monday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/non-public-funds-workers-take-picket-lines-on/docview/2915122485/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Non-Public+Funds+workers+take+to+the+picket+lines+on+Monday&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Citizen+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Almost 500 Non-Public Funds workers represented by the Public Service Alliance of Canada (PSAC) made their way to picket lines in Ontario and Quebec on Monday morning to demand a fair contract agreement
Members working in Ottawa rallied together by the Canadian Forces Morale and Welfare Services (CFMWS) and the CANEX Retail Store buildings on Labelle Street starting at 8 a.m. Monday morning. Pickets were bundled in winter jackets, hats and ski pants as the windchill made if feel like -20 C.
According to PSAC, strike action is impacting Non-Public Funds workers in Bagotville, Kingston, Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu, Ottawa, Petawawa and Valcartier. Some members made their way to picket lines by 6:30 a.m.
Julie Laska, picket captain and president of Local 70682 under the Union of National Defence Employees, said she has worked as a Non-Public Funds employee for almost eight years. She said it's bittersweet to be on strike, but said workers need to be on the line to fight for their salaries.
"Do we want to be here? No. We want to reach an agreement with the employer," said Laska, who is a member of the bargaining team. "People are here. We're cold, but we're here. It's important."
CFMWS is a separate agency, with NPF workers supporting Canadian Armed Forces members, veterans and their families by delivering morale and various welfare services. Forming part of the federal public service, they work in retail and food service through the Canadian Forces Exchange System, in financial planning for Canadian Armed Forces members through SISIP financial and in community service, physical fitness and recreation.
According to the Government of Canada website, CFMWS operates on behalf of the chief of defence staff and under the authority of the defence minister. As staff are not National Defence or Treasury Board employees, they have their own pension and pay system, and collective agreements.
Without a contract since 2022, members took part in strike votes from Nov. 20 to Dec. 14. On average, 94 per cent of members voted in favour of strike action. This is the first time that Non-Public Funds workers have voted for a strike mandate and the first PSAC strike since the national movement last spring.
Non-Public Funds workers walk the picket line on Monday.
NPF workers are fighting for fair wages, a national pay grid for workers across Canada and better job security. According to PSAC, NPF workers in Petawawa make less than a dollar per hour more than the federal minimum wage.
"We are some of the lowest paid public servants," Laska said, adding that about 75 members are working in Ottawa, though low wages cause a high rate of turnaround. "I love that I get to support the military every single day, and I just want to be supported to do that role."
PSAC national president Chris Aylward said this strike is about wages. While the parties are "not that far apart," he said they're still "not there."
"You could be doing one job here in Ottawa and somebody in Edmonton could be doing the exact same job but yet get paid six or seven dollars more an hour," Aylward said. "For far too long, these members have been underpaid, and now we're looking basically to do catch up. It's almost like a domino effect. You pay the workers a low wage, they move on and the military are the ones who suffer because there's a constant changeover and turnover in the staff who provide those services."
Alex Silas, PSAC's regional executive vice-president for the National Capital Region, said last week that the agency was using "scare tactics," encouraging workers to cross the picket line, signalling that their pay would be stopped and that benefits would be cut off.
In a statement, NDP labour critic Alexandre Boulerice said it was "deeply disappointing that the federal government has been unable to reach a fair agreement" with PSAC.
"These dedicated workers answer calls from our Canadian Forces seeking crucial support for their well-being and for morale," Boulerice said. "We call on the Liberals not to interfere in these workers' right to strike, and to swiftly come to the negotiation table with a fair agreement that respects these workers who serve to ensure that Canada's forces and their families are provided with the services and programs they need."
Andrée-Anne Poulin, a spokesperson for the Department of National Defence, said the CFMWS is "prepared for strike action."
"CFMWS services that could be impacted include, but are not limited to, physical fitness facilities, CANEX (military retail store), and recreation programs," Poulin said. "CFMWS aims to maintain the same level of service and programming so we can continue to serve our members despite the strikes."
Non-Public Funds workers walk the picket line on Monday.
Poulin said operational committees are in place to support the organization during a strike and ensure "minimal disruptions" to key operations as well as the protection of personnel, property and facilities. Poulin said plans are in place to continue providing "certain services," adding that the impact on other services is being mitigated by reducing facility opening hours and rescheduling non-essential events.
"CFMWS regrets that an agreement could not be reached between the parties to date," Poulin said. "We are hopeful that the parties can reach a negotiated agreement at the table, and remain dedicated to the collective bargaining efforts aimed at achieving a fair contract for our employees."
Diana Ebadi, press secretary to Minister of National Defence Bill Blair, said the minister believes that the best deals are made at the table and encourages both PSAC and CFMWS to work together on achieving a fair contract for employees.
"He remains hopeful that a good, negotiated agreement can be reached," Ebadi said.
Aylward said PSAC is waiting for the employer to come back to the table, adding that the union is ready to go back tomorrow morning if the agency has "something fair and decent" to present.
